Q: Quick Def of evidence

"Evidence" is being used here to mean something like, "an observation that seems to count in favor of (or justify) a particular belief." Public evidence consists in observations that we can share (e.g. photos, videos, arguments.) Private evidence consists in observations that we can't share. (e.g. the feeling of my head aching \-- i can \*tell\* you about it, but i can't share the feeling.)

Why should we think there's such a thing as private evidence? The basic thought is: sometimes we really do experience things that genuinely seem to justify our having certain beliefs (e.g. an experience of seeing an angel descend from the heavens to speak to me, a headache), even though we cannot share those experiences with others. The fact that we cannot share those observations doesn't seem to render them *evidentially worthless*, in other words.

Q: On the connection between the evidence principle and the problem of induction (and why philosophers mostly act as though the Problem can be solved)

You've hit on an important problem / methodological point.

The Fine-tuning Argument relies on this Evidence Principle and, you might well wonder, what is the justification for that principle? How does it relate to the Problem of Induction?

The answer is: the Evidence Principle is inductively justified and it assumes that the Problem of Induction can be resolved.

 

To the first point:

The evidence principle relies on some strong assumptions about the uniformity of nature ("in the past, evidence has been like this, so all evidence must be like that...") and it is, itself, largely plausible b/c of it's own history of successes. (e.g. "the evidence principle has worked so far in that past, so...") I think the evidence principle is a good, plausible principle\! We should accept it. But doing so requires us to set aside the Problem of Induction.

This is an important methodological point about philosophy that I cannot stress enough:

Basically, all philosophers go about their philosopher-business on the assumption that the Problem of Induction fails. Not because we all agree about why it fails. (And, indeed, for many us, we actually don't have any solution in mind\!) But we assume it fails because we can't really do otherwise. It's paralyzing to hold onto Inductive Skepticism; your life comes to a grinding halt. So we don't do that\! We are aware of the Problem, but must operate under the assumption that it's soluble. (likewise, we're all aware of Descartes' arguments for external world skepticism\! But we continue to operate as though we can know things about the external world.)

The Evidence Principle seems really good from that assumption. It's a genuinely attractive principle, so long as we assume that inductive reasoning is justified.

Q: On why the “will the sun rise tomorrow” example is well-chosen

Ah. That's interesting. 

 Far from being poorly chosen, the sun example is canonical *precisely because* it's such a weird thing to raise doubts about. We want to say, *of course the sun will rise tomorrow\! how could it not?* We use the rising of the sun to drive home how deeply undermining the Problem of Induction is. Even seemingly-obvious inferences (*the sun will rise tomorrow)* are threatened.

 

To your point about it being *not even imaginable:* there are lots of possibilities to consider\!

(i) Maybe it explodes during the night

(ii) Maybe the earth stops turning during the night

(iii) Maybe God destroys the universe during the night

(iv) Maybe aliens come and drag the earth away to an empty spot between galactic clusters in the night.

None of those seem likely, of course\! But that's not the point. The point is that our reasons for dismissing those possibilities **are inductive.** Why do we feel confident that aliens won't drag the earth away? Because that's never happened before. Nothing like that has ever been observed. Since we've never observed anything like that, **we infer inductively,** that won't happen tonight.

But now we confront the same problem all over again,

*Why should we think that inductive inferences like that one are justifying*?

Q: On Dreams and unreliability and daydreams

Interesting questions\!

To the first one:

The basic idea is that dream events do not consistently track real world events. That's why you shouldn't form your beliefs on the basis of things you observe in dreams. Suppose, e.g., that you have a dream in which I send this class an email saying that everyone will get an A in the course, and you don't need to turn anything else in. When you wake up, should you believe that to be true? No \- obviously not. The fact that it happened in a dream, is not a good reason to believe. We know that dreams are unreliable.

As you point out, though, dreams are not perfectly unreliable. Dreams are not false by definition. (e.g. the fact that X happened to you in a dream is not a reason to think that X will never and could never happen IRL\! Sometimes dreams mirror real life\!) That would actually be kinda helpful, if it were true – i.e. if dreams were necessarily false. If dreams were perfectly unreliable, then I could learn things about the world from my dreams. If, e.g., I dreamed that my partner cheated on me, I could conclude that my partner has not cheated on me.   
But, of course, since dreams don’t work like that, aren’t anti-reliable, we can’t conclude even that much from our dreams.

To the second one:  
Daydreams are interesting because they are not very dream-like. At least, for me, when I'm daydreaming, I am persistently aware that I am daydreaming. I never get confused about whether the fantasy I'm spinning out in my head is real life. Which is not true of my actual, nighttime dreams. I really do (every night\!) get confused about whether the fantasies my sleeping mind is spinning out for me are real life.

That's why daydreams don't seem to give rise to any skeptical concerns: we don't ever get tricked by daydreams.

Q: On circular reasoning and why we can’t conclude \~C from a bad argument for C

Maybe this will help you get your head around circular reasoning?

 

Consider this bad argument:

P1) Everything is blue

P2) The sky is a thing

C) The sky is blue

 

Notice, this argument sucks. (P1 is false\!) But the conclusion is true. You should believe that the sky is blue, but you shouldn't believe that on the basis of this argument.

In general, one should be careful to separate these two thoughts: (i) This is a bad argument for C, (ii) C is false. We should not infer from a bad argument that the conclusion of that argument is false. We should only infer that this particular argument fails.

Something similar can be said of circular reasoning. I can give you a circular justification for a true claim:

You should believe that the sky is blue because I say so. You should believe whatever I say because I'm infallible. And you should believe I am infallible because I am telling you that I am. And since I'm infallible, you can believe it. Etc..

Once again, the sky really is blue. But you shouldn't believe that merely because of the last paragraph of (bad, circular) argumentation.

SO. All that's to say: if you know X to be true, you shouldn't doubt X merely because someone gives you a circular justification for X. We can give bad justifications for true claims\!  
But the trouble arises when you only have circular justifications for X. If a circular justification is all you have, is your only reason for believing X, you're in trouble.

Q: On “what about when I feel tired/hungry/etc” replies to skepticism

Here's what I think Descartes might say about that:

You're right that there seems to be a connection between the actions we (seem to) take and the (apparent) states of minds. When it seems to me like I don't sleep for 3 days, my mind feels fuzzy and tired. When I don't eat, I feel weak.

But, as before, there are two explanations for what's going on:

(i) I actually do have a body. When I treat that body poorly by, e.g., not resting it, my brain functioning suffers as a direct causal result

(ii) I do not have a body; I am a brain in a vat. When the supercomputer notices that I am choosing to mistreat my "body" (i.e. when it notices that I am making cognitive choices such that, were I **actually** embodied, those choices would cause me to feel bad), it causes my brain to enter the mental states corresponding to my bad choices. If I choose to pull an all-nighter, the computer makes me feel tired. If I spend 3 days fasting, the computer makes me feel weak and hungry.

As before, our experiences are equally compatible with those two scenarios. Thus, our experiences do not count in favor of (i) \-- do not give us evidence that we actually do have bodies.

Thoughts about that line of reply?

Q: Why the Prob of Ind implicated probabilistic reasoning too

This is good\! And it's an important extension of the Problem of Induction.

 

As it happens, our use of probability itself seems to be reliant on inductive reasoning.

 

Think about, e.g., one of those exercises you do in Middle School where you flip a coin 100 times and record all the outcomes. The teacher (and the students) all go into the exercise believing that they know what will happen, roughly speaking. Their beliefs are probabilistically justified. And that justification turns on (a) things they know about probability and (b) past experiences.

But both (a) and (b) are themselves inductive.

One thing you might know about probability is that coin flips are independent events. But that's an inductive bit of knowledge. (Do you see why? I'll leave you to fill in the details. But think about how we learn of causal regularities.)

One thing you know about past experiences is that these exercises consistently produce the same sorts of results. That's also an inductive bit of knowledge. (Again, I'll leave you to fill in the details.)

 

Or think about a bookie who has set odds on 5,000-ish sports events over any given week. Let's suppose this week that 100 of those events have 9 to 1 odds. (90% chance of one outcome; 10 % chance of the other.) He gambles his livelihood on those events panning out in a particular way. He expects, roughly 90 of those events will go the way I expect and the other 10-ish will be surprise upsets. That expectation is inductive. (It's worked so far\! He's making a living, after all.)

And, we might note: Where do his odds-assignments come from in the first place? How does he decide how to arrange each bet? From the past \- from past observations of events like these. That, too, is inductive.

So, canonically at least, probabilistic reasoning has been thought to be hostage to the Problem of Induction, too. (In my logic courses \-- and in logic textbooks more generally \-- probabilistic arguments are taught as a kind of inductive argument, for precisely this reason.)

Q: On whether the brain in a vat hypothesis undermines our knowledge that WE exist (since our thoughts may not even be ours\!)

That's an excellent question\! I'm not 100% sure what to say about that \-- nor 100% sure what Descartes would want to say about that.

Here're some options:

(1) Your cognition and thought processes (if you're anything like me) exhibit a fair amount of regularity. You have certain thoughts that you return to often; other thoughts that you have rarely if ever. You have stable preferences, persistent likes and dislikes. You make similar choices in similar situations.

On the Memory/Personality view we talked about a couple weeks ago, all that stuff taken together **just is YOU.** 

So what happens if all that stuff is caused by the supercomputer and doesn't really issue from your own decisions and choices?

Maybe nothing\! Those stable, persistent traits may not originate from you but they still **are** you. Those are **your** thoughts; they are the thoughts that **your** brain is having, thoughts consistent with **your** personality. 

That's one thing we might say.

(2) Maybe we could say that what the supercomputer is doing to your brain (causing you to have particular thoughts) isn't all **that** different from what our the external world environment is supposed to do to us in the good case (where everything is the way that it seems.) Like, if someone on the bus shouts "FIRE\!", you are now thinking about fire. That thought wasn't *your choice;* it was forced on you by that other person. Or if you get slapped in the face, you are now feeling pain sensations in your cheek. Those thoughts / perceptions aren't anything you selected for yourself; they've been imposed on your mind from without.

Maybe something similar can be said about the supercomputer? (It's still ultimately pushing thoughts into **your** brain so maybe that's what makes them **your** thoughts?)

Is that satisfying at all? Do you (or anyone else\!) have other reactions to that possibility?

Q: Why would the supercomputers let us entertain this idea?

That's a really interesting line of thought.

(Suppose we are brains in vats. Why are we allowed to even consider that possibility? Why would the supercomputers let us contemplate **the truth**?)

I like both of your possibilities. Those are good answers. Another possibility:

*Maybe the supercomputers or superneuroscientists thinks it's funny when the brain-in-a-vat contemplates the truth and then immediately rejects it as stupid.*

Like, one very common reaction to External World Skepticism is, "That's so dumb and unpersuasive. Of course I know that there's an external world." That reaction would be kinda funny to observe from the outside, watching the brain-in-a-vat come \*this close\* to the truth and the confidently walk away. (also, for the rare brain that actually lets itself be convinced, no harm is done. What is it gonna do? It's a brain-in-a-vat\! It can't fight back\!)

Q: Don’t religious experiences prove we aren’t brains in vats?

Ah, interesting\!

Here's what I think Descartes might say about that:

Suppose I have a religious experience. That is, I have an experience and it seems to me to have a *religious component.*

As before, there are two explanations for what's going on:

(i) I have a real body and the external world is more-or-less the way I think it is. I am having a religious experience because something about my environment is causing certain neurons in my brain to fire in ways that give rise to particular religious sensations. (e.g. maybe I am seeing an angel\! In that case, my visual cortex is firing in such a way as to give me that perception of an angel.) Maybe I am seeing an angel because photos are bouncing off the angel and entering my eye. Or maybe God has reached down and messed with my brain a little to cause me to hallucinate an angel. Or maybe I am high as a kite on hallucinogens and *they* are causing that neural firing pattern

(ii) I am a brain in a vat; I do not have a body. The supercomputer, with its encyclopedic knowledge of human brain states, is firing my neurons in a pattern that gives rise to a religious seeming experience. If I'm seeing an angel, that's because the computer is causing my visual cortext to fire in the way the corresponds to angel-seeing\! Or maybe God is guiding the supercomputer to do that. Or maybe the computer has dropped some hallucinogens into my brain-vat.

As before, it seems our experiences are equally compatible with those two scenarios. Thus, our experiences do not count in favor of (i) \-- do not give us evidence that we actually do have bodies.

That's what I think Descartes would say, anyway \-- thoughts about that line of reply?

Q: Practically, though, don’t we have to use induction?

Yeah, you've hit on an important methodological point about philosophy that I cannot stress enough:

Basically, all philosophers go about their philosopher-business on the assumption that the Problem of Induction fails. Not because we all **agree** about why it fails. (And, indeed, for many us, we actually don't have any solution in mind\!) But we assume it fails because we can't really do otherwise.

It's paralyzing to hold onto Inductive Skepticism\! Your life comes to a grinding halt. So we don't do that. We are aware of the Problem, but must operate under the assumption that it's soluble. (likewise, we're all aware of Descartes' arguments for external world skepticism\! But we continue to operate as though we can know things about the external world.)


And, of course, so do scientists. They keep doing science, keep assuming the uniformity of nature, continue operating on the inductive principle that the future will resemble the past.  And, it's been working\! Scientific progress continues to march onward.

In fact, I expect between now and the next time I teach the Problem of Induction to another batch of students that science will continue to function normally. Everyone expects that. We all expect the future to resemble the past.

BUT the point of the Problem of Induction is that it's... really surprisingly hard to explain in a non-circular way why we're *justified* in expecting that. 

We *do* expect it, and we do *think* we're justified in expecting it. But... damn, it's not easy to pin down why.

Q: Can’t we just say the same thing about the Problem of Induction that Hick says about religious experiences?

Ahh, interesting\!

The connection you're drawing between Hick's arguments about religious experiences and the Problem of Induction is clever. *Why can't we respond to the Induction Skeptic in the same way that Hick responds to the atheist? Why can't we say, "**I** have personal experiences that prove to me that induction is reliable." ?*

I'm not totally sure what the Inductive Skeptic would say about this, but here's my best guess:

Recall, Hick's argument absolutely relies on the distinction between **public evidence** and **private evidence.** Public evidence is evidence that can be shared, that multiple people can examine and access at different times and in the same way. (e.g. a newspaper can be shared like this.) Private evidence is evidence that cannot be shared like that. (e.g. the feeling of your head aching cannot be shared.)

Hick's view is that **insofar as a theist has compelling private evidence** for God's existence \-- think, like, a literal vision of an angel or a conversation with God \-- then that private evidence may make it rational for that particular person to believe in God. That person may count as having *sufficient evidence* to justify their beliefs.

But it's not obvious to me that a parallel situation is ever going to occur when it comes to the reliability of induction. The point of the Problem of Induction is that *no one has **any** non-circular justification for thinking inductive reasoning is reliable.* 

Merely observing the recurrence of patterns doesn't count. That's, at best, circular evidence.

("Why are you so confident that pattern X will continue into the future?"

"Because it has proven to be right so, so often in the past. This generalization is my truth."

"But why does the fact that it's happened in the past mean it will continue happening in the future?"

"Because I know that the future will resemble the past\!"

"Wait... how do you know that?"

"Because, in the past, the future has always resembled the past. That's a pattern\! I've observed that generalization being true so many times. I can't share that evidence with you, but that's what's justifying my beliefs."

"I don't see how that helps \-- again I ask: why do you think that *that* pattern will continue to hold in the future? What justification is there for assuming ANY pattern will persist into the future?"

"Because I know the future will resemble the past."

"You still haven't actually given me any justification for that claim. Your last answer was basically *It will because it will."*

Etc.)

All that's to say, I'm not sure I see how private evidence helps? The problem is that *all evidence* seems circular \-- whether sharable and public or not. All of our justifications for induction seem to presuppose that induction is already justified. 

I guess one way out might be to have private assurances from God that induction is justified. Like if God came to you this morning and say, "Hey, just to let you know: Induction is justified." Assuming you believe God to be omniscient and truthful, that would seem to justify a belief in induction\!

But that's not a very satisfying place to leave the Problem of Induction:

*It is rational to believe that inductive arguments are justified but only if an Omniscient God personally told you they are.*

 

That's my best guess at what the Inductive Skeptic would say, anyway. 

Any thoughts about that? Are there other avenues by which private evidence might give us some non-circular justification for thinking inductive reasoning is realiable?

Q: On solipsism

RE: Solipism

Yeah, that's a good question, too. *Solipsism* is the actually the name for a few different views.

The strongest version says: *Only* *I exist; there are no other beings with minds in the universe.*

The interaction between external world skepticism and that kind of solipsism is kinda complicated.

First, external world skepticism is the view that *we can't **know** whether the external world is real.* Thus, the external world skeptic is not a (strong) solipsist. At most, she will say that **she doesn't know** whether or not she's the only person in the universe.

Second, most of the skeptical hypotheses we considered actually don't entail solipsism\! Like, suppose you are being tricked by an evil demon. If so, there are actually 2 people in the universe: you and the demon. Hence, solipsism is false.  Similarly, suppose you are being tricked by a bunch of superneuroscientists. If so, there must be a few people in the universe: you and a bunch of scientists.

That said, there are other skeptical scenarios that would entail solipsism. Like, maybe you're a brain in a vat but the supercomputer that's running the simulation isn't conscious and there are no other conscious beings in that universe \-- just a big computer and a single brain. 

That scenario *does* seem to entail solipsism\! But, again, the external world skeptic isn't *confident* that she's a brain in a vat. So she'll never be confident that solipsism is true.

There are weaker forms of solipsism, too. And those *are* entailed by external world skepticism. 

One weaker version says: *One cannot know whether other minds exists.*

That version obviously does follow from external world skepticism. If I can't know whether *anything* outside of my own head exists, then I can't know whether *minds* outside my own head exist.

Does that answer your question?

Q: But wait – aren’t some inductive inferences better than others?

Yeah, that's super interesting\!

Right, so both of those inferences are inductive but they differ in how reliable they seem. (i.e. one of those inferences seems better than the other.) What’s up with that? Does that mean Hume is wrong?

Notice, in both cases we're projecting a regularity from observations we’ve already made onto as-yet-unobserved cases. In one case, the regularity is "\_". In the other case the regularity is, "\_". In both cases, our inference assumes that the unobserved instances will resemble the observed instances. That does mean, of course, that both inferences are unjustified if we can't solve the Problem of Induction.

(e.g. "Why do we know that \_?" "B/c \_." "How do we know that?"... )

 

As you point out, one of those inferences seems far more reliable than the other\! That's interesting\! How could some inductive inferences be better than others if they're all unjustified?

Well, they wouldn't. **If** induction is unjustified, then all inductive inferences are equally worthless.

But you and I and everyone we know don't **actually believe** that induction is unjustified. We use it all the time\! It works great\! (or, *has worked great so far*\!)

So what you're noticing is that *If we set aside the Problem of Induction and assume that induction works,* then some inductive inferences are clearly better than others. Your cases illustrate that point really nicely\! Setting aside the Problem of Induction, it's clearly true that the \_ inference is more reliable than the \_. (For lots of reasons\!) If this were a Logic course, we'd talk a lot about why that is. *Assuming induction is justified, what makes some inductive inferences better than others?* Turns out, it's pretty complicated\!

Q: For someone confused about skepticism, broadly

Great \- that's a really astute question\!

 

The grossly simplified version goes like this,

*The X Skeptic is someone who doesn't think we can have knowledge about X-things. So a **religion** skeptic doesn't think we can know things about God or the supernatural, including whether God actually exists. A **history** skeptic doesn't think we can know anything about the past. A **horse** skeptic doesn't think we can know anything about horses (including whether they even exist.)*

Obviously, some forms of skepticism are more plausible than others. No one is a horse skeptic; that's a silly position. But there are some religious skeptics\!

An **external world skeptic** is, thus, a person who doesn't think we can know things about the external world, including whether there even is such a thing.

Here's the more sophisticated explanation:

*Most people agree that **knowledge** requires **justification**. Suppose I just random pick a day (call it 'Z') from the calendar and then I form the belief that that's your birthday. And, let's suppose, I accidentally guess correctly. Do I **know** that Z is your birthday? Well, if you tell me "Hey\! You guessed correctly\!" then yes. I know it. But if you **don't** tell me, then surely not. Surely I don't **know** it\! I believe it, and my belief is accidentally true, but it's not knowledge.*

*But what is justification?*

*It's evidence, basically. Or, if you like, reasons. When I ask, "What's your justification for thinking that the earth is round?" I'm asking, "What evidence do you have?" or "What reasons led you to this belief?"*

*Justification comes from a bunch of different places. Our senses give us justification. The fact that **you saw** a buffalo on the grass, is a good reason to think there's a buffalo on the grass. Other people's testimony can give us justification. The fact that I **told you** that my favorite fruit is dates is a good reason to think that my favorite fruit is dates. Our memory can give us justification. Logic and reasoning can give us justification.*

*Here's why all that matters:*

*The External World Skeptic thinks that we cannot have knowledge **about some particular thing.** (that's what I was explaining above.) The External World Skeptic thinks that **no sources of justification can give us evidence about the external world.** A **religious skeptic** has a similar view about religious knowledge: no sources of justification can give us evidence about religion.*

*The Inductive Skeptic isn't like that. She doesn't think that we can have knowledge **that's justified using induction** (which is a form of reasoning.) The Inductive Skeptic thinks that **this particular source of justification (induction) is worthless and never gives us knowledge.** (Compare: a Memory Skeptic is someone who thinks that **memory is worthless and never gives us knowledge.** A **Testimony Skeptic** is someone who thinks that **other people's say-so is worthless and never gives us knowledge.**)*

 

If I had to guess, anyway, that's the source of the confusion: those two kinds of skeptics are *skeptical about very different sorts of things*. The External World Skeptic is skeptical that we can know **about** the external world. The Inductive Skeptic is skeptical that we can know **using** our inductive reasoning. 

Does that help?

 

#

Q: Can we accept the Post-truth Thesis and the Echo Chamber Thesis?

I certainly don't think these two explanations (the Post Truth Thesis and the Echo Chamber Thesis) are incompatible. It could well be the case that each is a partial explanation for the worsening of political disagreement. (Maybe *to some extent* people care less about truth than they used to. But people *are also increasingly* getting trapped in echo chambers \-- and, for those people, caring about truth doesn't help.) Nguyen doesn't give us any arguments against this possibility. He doesn't try to show that **only one explanation can be correct.** Nor does he try to show that **everyone likes truth and reasoning just as much as they used to.** In other words, he doesn't close off the possibility that the Post Truth Thesis is kinda true.

Charitably, we might interpret Nguyen's position as being that *the Echo Chamber Thesis is the more powerful explanation*, or the one that explains *more* of the data. (That's why he points out that when you talk to someone in an echo chamber, they mostly do really *seem* to care about argumentation and truth\! They will give you arguments, cite evidence, raise objections, etc. That observation, if correct, does seem to support the Post-Truth Thesis as the more important explanation.)

As for whether the Post Truth Thesis depicts a world that's easier to fix... I don't know? Maybe not\! 

Like, how do you persuade someone who doesn't care about truth that they *should* care about truth? (Won't they just say, "Maybe it's true that I should care about truth. But I don't *care* that that's true." ???) Similarly, how do you persuade a person that's indifferent to rationality, consistency, and reasoning to care about those things? My first instinct would be to... argue with them. Which presumably won't help. 

Hard question. Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that? About which Thesis, if true, would be easier to fix?

Q: Surely there’s a moderate view between Conciliationism and Steafastism\!

Right, so: there's an important sense in which Conciliationism and Steadfast(ism?) are very simple views. Namely, they don't have any exception clauses; they both say, *always do this with your credences when confronted with peer disagreement.*

Maybe neither view is successful *precisely because* we shouldn't always do one thing with our credence. Perhaps it's contextual\! In such-and-such kinds of cases, we should conciliate; in so-and-so other cases we should be steadfast.

If that's right, then it might be more helpful to think of Conciliationism and Steadfastism as starting points for the discussion, rather than final answers. Like, *Here are the extreme views. Clearly the correct answer rests somewhere in the middle. The trick is figuring out where\!*

Q: On the “no new evidence” argument for Steadfastism

Yeah, that's a nice suggestion\! That sounds like a common Steadfaster treatment of disagreement. (Which is to say: you're in good company\!)

Steadfasters often want to say:

*When you learn that an epistemic peer disagrees with you about X, you don't **at that moment** get any new evidence about X. (After all, we can pretty much guarantee that for every one of your beliefs, **someone** disagrees with you about it. So you don't really learn anything new when you locate that someone.) Since you don't get any new evidence, it would be irrational to change your confidence in X. **(Plausible Epistemic Principle:** one should only ever change one's beliefs or degrees of confidence in light of new evidence.)*

*That said, when you learn that an epistemic peer disagrees with you, you **do** now have an opportunity to ask them why. And it could be that their explanation **does** give you new evidence \-- some argument or consideration or reason you've not considered before. If that happens, then of course you should change your degree of confidence. But not otherwise.*

 

I think that's a pretty powerful argument for the Steadfast position\! 

Any thoughts (you or anyone else\!) about what the Conciliationist might want to say in response?

Q: Are echo chambers getting worse? Haven’t we always had them?

‘Yeah, that's a good point\!

It is absolutely essential for Nguyen's thesis that echo chambers are **getting worse** over time. (If that's not true, if echo chambers are just as common now as they used to be, then he doesn't have a great way to explain why disagreements are getting worse\!) But supposing that's right, that still leaves us with the question: **why?** Why are echo chambers more common now than ever before?

I'm not sure what Nguyen is going to want to say about that. Is it the result of recent political changes? Of recently technological changes? Of recent social upheavals? 

We'll read a paper next week that (indirectly) suggests that much of the blame can be laid at the feet of technological change. I think I agree with you that that's *probably* Nguyen's view, too. Maybe the same forces that are causing online epistemic bubbles (e.g. a shift from everyone reading the same news sources to everyone having their own personalized news streams) are also making us more vulnerable to echo chambers? Though, as you point out, it's not entirely obvious that epistemic bubbles *are getting worse.* The internet and social media really do provide us with access to all sorts of information that we might otherwise lack\!

I'm curious whether you (or anyone else\!) has any other suggestions / theories as for why echo chambers might be getting worse.

Q: Are echo chambers getting worse b/c of politicization?

You might be right that limiting politicization would help\! 

That said, I *think* what's going on there is that limiting politicization limits the *scope* of an echo chamber. (rather than limiting the *amount* of echo chambers or the *number of people* trapped in them.)

Like, suppose that i am trapped in a CrossFit echo chamber. I trust my CrossFit trainer **way more than I should.** That might not be that bad for me, though, because my CrossFit trainer mostly only talks to me about exercise. So I'm only going to have unreliable beliefs about that stuff. But now suppose my CrossFit trainer starts talking to me about how COVID is a hoax. If I continue to trust her, then my echo chamber is worse b/c it now has a broader **scope** (including both exercise facts and public health facts.)

I think that's what's going on with politicization. When Topic X becomes politicized, people in political echo chambers are going to start forming their beliefs about X in unreliable, echo-chambery ways.

That's my first reaction about politicization, anyway.

Thoughts about any of that? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: On the diff between the post-truth thesis and the echo chamber thesis

According to the Post-truth Thesis, many people have stopped caring about reasoning / about truth / about rationality all together. For these people, the decision about whether to believe something is fundamentally *emotional* rather than *rational.*

 

According to Nguyen's preferred explanation (what we might call 'the Echo Chamber Thesis'), people still care about reasoning / about truth / about rationality. What's going on is that some people are *radically* mistaken about how evidence should be assessed. Such people *really do* care about truth and rationality, they are just getting things wrong because they're starting from false assumptions.

Here's a concrete case \- maybe that will help.

Suppose Trevor is really into some new diet craze \-- maybe the Beans Only diet \-- and is a member of a bunch of online communities for Beans Only eaters like him. Suppose some new scientific study comes out that says, with some force, that the Beans Only diet is very unhealthy. How will Trevor respond to this new information? Assuming he's going to react *badly* (irrationally), the Post-truth Thesis and the Echo Chamber Thesis actually make different predictions:

(i) The Post-truth Thesis expects Trevor to *not care* about this new research. Trevor *feels* that the Beans Only diet is healthy, and that's enough. He doesn't need to see "evidence" or "arguments". It doesn't matter what "rationality" demands. That stuff doesn't matter. He's found his truth, and his truth is the Beans Only diet.

(ii) The Echo Chamber Thesis expects Trevor to *challenge* this new research. Trevor has been told that nutritionists are in the pocket of Big Agriculture and that their research is fake and spurious. He looks through the new article, and, it seems to him, it's more of the same. Bad science paid-for by industry hacks. So, **he reasons,** he should dismiss this evidence. The **reasonable thing to do**, he concludes, is to continue believing that the Beans Only diet is good.

(There's a third option for Trevor, of course. He might simply *reason correctly*:

(iii) Ideally, if Trevor is being a responsible and rational agent, he will carefully consider the new study and, in light of this new evidence from experts, he will lower his confidence that Beans Only diet is a good idea.)

The main difference between the two Theses is this:

The Post-Truth thesis says people have **given up on reasoning.** The Echo Chamber thesis says people **are still reasoning but doing it badly.**

Q: On how to tell whether one is already in an echo chamber

One thing we might say is this:

*In general,* one thing we should watch out for is a tendency in ourselves to dismiss people as unreliable *as soon as we learn what views they hold.* It's fine, of course, to dismiss people as unreliable when we've listened to what they have to say and decided that they are liars or that they have bad argument or that they have bad judgment. In contrast, it's a pretty good sign that you are echo chambered if you feel confident that someone else is unreliable **merely upon learning what their beliefs are.** 

That may be one helpful indicator but, unfortunately, it doesn't seem to be either a necessary nor sufficient condition for being echo chambered.

 

It's not sufficient because sometimes it probably **is** rational to dismiss people merely on learning that they have certain views. If, e.g., you learn that I think there's no such thing as truth or justification or rationality, then you can probably safely dismiss everything I have to say. Since I don't myself even **believe** in rationality, I presumably won't be giving *arguments* or *reasons* for agreeing with me. (B/c I don't believe in that stuff\!) So you can ignore me.

 

And it's not necessary because very often people trapped in echo chambers really **do** listen to other people\! They don't just ignore people who disagree with them\! They engage with their arguments and reasons. (Badly, of course, but they do really engage.) So you may be echo chambered even if you never make the mistake of dismissing someone merely for having certain views.

 

So, yeah. It's hard. You're right.

BUT it does seem to me that a person who regularly asks themselves "Am I trapped in an echo chamber?" is much more likely to not get trapped in one\!

Q: On distinsuighing peer disagreement cases from superior/inferior cases

Here's what I think we should say about those cases.

The question about whether to be **steadfast** or to **conciliate** only arises in situations where you yourself judge that the person you're disagreeing with is your epistemic peer. If you and I are disagreeing about cars, you should judge me your epistemic inferior. (I know nothing about cars.) Thus, that would not be a case *peer* disagreement. The problem doesn't arise. If you and I are disagreeing about Russian Table Tennis, you should judge me your epistemic superior. (Let's pretend that I am widely regarded as an expert on that topic.) Thus, that too would not be a case of *peer* disagreement. The problem doesn't arise.

 

As you note, there is a serious, unresolved challenge here: how are we supposed to tell whether the person who disagrees with us is our epistemic peer? 

It's hard\! It's messy\! There will be cases where it's simply unclear\!

I tried in lecture to gesture at a list of relevant factors, a list of criteria that people do in fact use to assess other people's relative epistemic standing (e.g. our relative experience, our relative bias, our relative time / attention.) But (a) that may not be the whole list and (b) it may be unclear in any particular case how two people stack up, along the listed dimensions.

 

The question of peer disagreement only arises *when* and *once* that issue has already been settled\! *Assuming that you've decided you and I are epistemic peers,* how should you respond to our disagreement?

Q: On the “aren’t steadfastness just assholes?” response

That's a rather kind way of putting a common complaint against the Steadfast View. 

The complaint is basically, *Aren't Steadfasters just kind of... assholes?*

 

Imagine my position if you and I disagree about X but **agree** that we are epistemic peers. If I am a Steadfaster, then I will simultaneously acknowledge that you are basically as competent as me, are basically as likely as me to be right about whether it is we're disagreeing about, AND still insist that I am correct, making absolutely zero concession to you or to the possibility that I might be wrong and you might be right. Essentially, I'll be saying something like, "It's true that you and I are equally like to be right about this. But there's an important difference between you and me: whereas I am me, you are not me. Your opinion can be dismissed (can be regarded as *no evidence*) precisely because it is not mine."

Which kinda sounds like something only an asshole would say\!

 

(Of course, that doesn't show that the Steadfast View is **false.** But it does present something of a rhetorical challenge, don't you think?)

Q: On whether we can solve the objections to our Views by claiming that the other person isn’t a peer

Maybe the Conciliationist can say something like this:

*The question about whether to be steadfast or to conciliate only arises in situations where you yourself judge that the person you're disagreeing **with is your epistemic peer**. If you're simply disagreeing with an inferior (as the PhD in Roman History is in the vomitorium case), then you should of course be Steadfast.  And, thus, maybe what's going on in the cases where the Steadfast View seems better is that **you are not actually judging the other person to be your peer.** If you tell me that 1 and 1 make 0, I'm not going to take you be roughly as good as me at arithmetic. I'm going to take you to be a crackpot \-- a non-peer, an epistemic inferior. And since I judge you to be an epistemic inferior, I should of course be steadfast.*

That's one very promising way to defend the Concilationism position\! Maybe we can simply argue that all the cases where the Steadfast View seems right are just cases in which the other person **is actually an epistemic inferior.**

 

But I wonder whether the same strategy might not be open to the Steadfaster. Like, maybe the Steadfaster could argue that all the cases where the Conciliation View seem right are just cases in which the other person **is actually an epistemic superior?**

That would explain why we feel the pull of conciliating\! If we just actually the think the other person is more reliable than us, then of course we should lower our own confidence. The Steadfaster can say: *all cases where we **actually** judge the other person to be our peer are cases where we should intuitively hold steadfast.*

But maybe that argumentative doesn't work as well for the Steadfaster? I don't know\! Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Q: On different versions of Conciliationism

Great question\!

Basically, you get **different versions of Conciliationism depending on how much conciliation you think is rational.** The most extreme version of Conciliationism is what we might call the 'Equal Weight View'. It says: *if you think X but then learn that a peer thinks not-X, you should give both views equal weight. (i.e. you should think it equally likely that X is true or that not-X is true.)*

That's the most extreme version\! Less extreme versions will tell you that you should lower your confidence in X less than that. (Maybe, you are 90% confident that X. You learn that a peer thinks not-X. So you should conciliate and be 75% confident that X.)

*Which version should we prefer \-- or which is **the most rational**?*

That's hard\! Here's one helpful rule of thumb:

Keep in mind that the whole point is that we're trying to **maximize the number of true and justified beliefs we have.** Thus, we might say, the best version of Conciliationism is the one that, when followed in the long run, maximizes the number of true and justified beliefs. (I don't know which one that is.)

Q: Are steadfastness or conciliators more vulnerable to echo chambers?

That's a provocative line of thought\! 

I didn't explicitly consider the connections between the Peer Disagreement conversation and the Echo Chamber conversation in lecture, but you're right to suggest that there **are some.**

We might wonder, for instance, *Are echo chambers a bigger problem for either of our Peer Disagreement views? Like, are Steadfasters more likely to get trapped in an echo chamber than Conciliationists?*

 

If I'm reading you right, you seem to be leaning toward *Yes, Steadfasters are more at risk*. 

That's plausible, I think. We might reason: Steadfasters are, by definition, people who refuse to change their minds in the face of disagreement. That's part of what's going on in an echo chamber\! A person trapped in an echo chamber may *hear* or *be exposed to* contrary viewpoints and disconfirming evidence, but they refuse to be swayed.

On the other hand, though, I think there's also something to be said for Conciliationists being especially vulnerable to echo chambers. Recall, Nguyen's view is that people get trapped in echo chambers when they trust someone else *too much,* when they regard in-group people as super-trustworthy and out-group people as super-untrustworthy. You might think: That sounds like a Conciliationist problem\! Conciliationism is all about trusting other people.

So: all that's to say, *I'm not sure whether Conciliationists or Steadfasters are more likely to get trapped in echo chambers.* A case can be made for either.

Then again, we might think that a person trapped in an echo chamber isn't really being a Concilliationist **or** a Steadfaster. A person trapped in an echo chamber is making the mistake of treating **everyone who disagrees with them as an epistemic inferior**. If everyone who disagrees with me is my epistemic inferior, then the question of whether to Conciliate or be Steadfast simply doesn't arise. When it comes to my inferiors, the rational thing to do is ignore them.

 

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that \- about the connections between echo chambers and our views on peer disagreement?

Q: A reply to someone who thinks Olberding is too sympathetic to incivility

Olberding’s ultimate position is, I think, that **incivility is mostly not great**. (She wants to say, of herself, *I often feel the pull of incivility, and can convince myself that it would be really great to be uncivil. But I realize that I shouldn't act on those feelings. I am likely to confuse my selfish desire to be rude with actual righteousness.*) She does thinks that there are, in principle, cases where incivility is called for. But those cases are rare. She worries that we are not very good at honestly determining when we ourselves are in such a case.

What makes this article interesting is that **Amy is actually *way more opposed to incivility* than most people** in our country right now seem to be. Her position is, in that sense, kinda radical.

 

It sounds like you think her position isn't quite radical enough, that she's still too sympathetic toward incivility. That's interesting\! 

It sounds like you think incivility is unproductive \-- or counterproductive \-- in many cases. Do you think that's true even in the sorts of easy cases Amy has in mind? (e.g. being disrespectful to pedophilia proponents.)

And are there any other sorts of reasons why you think incivility is mostly not a good idea?

Q: On whether incivility is ever persuasive

Good points\!

I think there are a few things Olberding might say here:

 

(i) First, we might doubt whether the suffragettes won *because they were persuasive.* That seems like an open question to me. For all that's been said, maybe the suffragettes won the vote by being *exhausting.* Maybe the men in their society thought it would just be easier to give them the vote than to continue putting up with their uncivil campaign for suffrage. (To be clear, I don't **endorse** that reaction on the part of the 1910's men. I am merely raising it as a historical possibility.) If that's right, then the suffragette case wouldn't count as a counter-example to the claim that *incivility is unpersuasive*. If that's right, they *weren't* persuasive. They didn't change hearts and minds; they just changed behavior.

(ii) Even if the suffragette case *is* a counter-example to the claim that *incivility is unpersuasive,* Olberding actually doesn't need that claim to be true. What matters, for her purposes, is that civility is in general **far more persuasive** than incivility. That's enough for her argument; that's enough to get her what she wants. She's mostly just trying to get us to accept that *being civil* should be our default manner of engaging with people who disagree with us. That weaker claim (i.e. that civility is much more persuasive than incivility) also seems to me to be quite plausibly true. (I, myself, am much more persuadable when the people who disagree with me are giving me arguments and reasons *respectfully* and *politely.* Indeed, I can't really think of a instance where someone has persuaded me of something while yelling at me or insulting me.)

(iii) Lastly, Olberding might say that *Incivility is never persuasive* is not actually her view, anyway. She never actually says, "Incivility is never persuasive." That was just **my** quick gloss to get the general idea across\! 

Probably I should have been more careful. (For precisely the reasons you've raised here\!)

Q: On Moderately Objectionable Means and righteous incivility

Olberding and I were lumping together under the banner "INCIVILITY" a bunch of different actions: yelling, cursing, threatening, punching, window-breaking.

But, you might think, *Hey\! Those actions are not all on-a-par, morally speaking\! Like, punching someone is morally much worse than yelling at them. Breaking their windows is much worse than cursing at them and calling them names.*

*So **even if** I agree that the suffragettes were justified in being uncivil (by yelling) I might still think they were **not** justified in being uncivil (by breaking windows.)*

 

That's a sharp point\! And one that Olberding and I glossed over in the interests of keeping things simple.

The question then is,

*If we **are** sometimes justified in being uncivil, what exactly are we justified in doing? (yelling? threatening? punching?) And how can we tell?*

I don't have any ready answers here. Do you (or anyone else) have any thoughts about that?

Q: Clarification: Rini thinks it’s prudentially a good idea to share fake news

One thing that's worth clarifying:

Rini's argument is that it makes prudential sense for the individual to share fake news. It's a benefit for that person. (In the same way: it makes prudential sense for me to pirate movies. It's good-for-me.) She isn't claiming either (a) that it's morally acceptable to share fake news or (b) that it's good-for-society for people to share fake news. (She doesn't claim those things, I think, because they aren't true\!)

Claim (a) is probably false \-- as you point out. It's probably not morally good to share fake news. One thing we might do here is, essentially, point back to Clifford: it's often morally bad to believe things on the basis of insufficient evidence, and, thus, it's morally bad to knowingly get other people to believe things on the basis of insufficient evidence.

Claim (b) is also probably false. 

I wonder if Rini might not consider this to be a tragedy of the commons case. For each individual news consumer, it is in his or her best interests to share fake news. (Because that is a good way to get your social media contacts to believe things that you want them to believe.) But it's a net bad for society if everyone shares fake news. Since I don't have any control over whether everyone shares fake news, my choice seems pretty straight forward. I can either share fake news or not. If I do, then I at least get the benefit of persuading some people some of the time, regardless of how much other people are sharing fake news. If I don't, then I still don't have any control over whether social media gets overrun by fake news, but I now don't get to reap the personal benefits of doing so myself.

Thus, fake news is individually sensible but collectively disastrous.

That, anyway, is my best guess at what Rini would want to say about this. Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts or reactions to that line of argument?

Q: What if people disagree about whether “sharing entails assertion”?

I think she'd say this about your point about different people have differing norms in mind:

*True, there may be cases where the person with whom you share a fake news article thinks that sharing entails assertion. In which case they may, by default, take you to be less credible. But,*

*First, if that's true only rarely, then my account can still explain why it's mostly rational for most people most of the time. If that's true only rarely, the fake news sharer may occasionally lose credibility, but it may still be rational for her to share. So, the question is: To what extent do people think that sharing entails assertion? (Someone's gonna have to do some science about that\!)*

*Second, the sharer may still be able to claim plausible deniability when they are able to have a conversation with the share-ee. If they can persuade the other person "I honestly didn't take myself to be asserting the content of that news article" that may considerably reduce their credibility loss. That said, the sharer won't then be able to get the benefits of asserting shared content with that particular person in the future \-- not that they've said that they don't mean to assert when they share.*

Q: Clarification: Rini’s view is more subtle than simply endorsing confirmation bias

I don't see her telling people to *seek out* news articles that align with their beliefs. She's imagining a person who has friends on both sides of the aisle and who sees articles shared from both sides. (Thus, the set up isn't supposed to involve a selection bias. I suspect Rini would say, *Insofar as you have good reason to think you're already trapped in an epistemic bubble, you should be worried that your news diet **whether mostly fake stuff or mostly legit** is habitually leading you astray, causing lots of false beliefs. But, then, the source of the irrationality in that case is the epistemic bubble, not the fake news articles per se.*

Nor does she tell people to *believe* the content of articles *only because* the people sharing the article are co-partisans. That would be irrational\!

The thought is more subtle. She wants to say, *Knowing that friend X is a co-partisan gives me an extra reason to treat them as an epistemic peer on normative and political matters. That reason will come into play when they share an article with me. But the reason often won't be decisive; it will often be outweighed by other considerations.*

*If the article is **very suspicious**, it will be irrational to believe the article's content, despite the fact that the person is a co-partisan. If the article is **very reliable seeming,** it will probably be rational to believe the article, and would be **even if** the sharer wasn't a co-partisan. It's only when the article is **kinda suspicious** that the co-partisanship matters. That may tip the balance in edge cases, making it rational to believe articles that would otherwise not pass muster.*

You may ultimately be right that her view ends up endorsing some confirmation bias\! (And you'd be right that that's a point of concern\!) But it strikes me as maybe unfair to say that her position is **predicated** on confirmation bias.

(OR at the very least, to say that her view is predicated on confirmation bias would seem to imply some surprising things epistemic peerhood\!  Part of what we're assessing when we're assessing epistemic peerhood is how likely the other person is, on average, to get things right. But "getting things right" is necessarily relativized to what we already happen to think is true\! So a person who disagrees with me about everything is not going to be judged, by me, an epistemic peer.

Maybe that means that judging someone to be an epistemic peer is predicated on confirmation bias? That might be a defensible view\!)

Q: On whether Rini \+ Nguyen means Rini thinks echo chambers are rational

Yeah, good\! If I'm reading you right, you seem to be gesturing at a pretty important objection to Rini \-- one that comes to us from the Nguyen article we read last week.

The argument I have in mind is this:

According to Rini, it is sometimes reasonable for me to believe fake news articles because the fact that the articles are being shared by a co-partisan may generate enough extra justification to overcome whatever worries I may have about the article seeming suspicious. (That *sometimes* is important, of course. If the article is REALLY suspicious, it would still be irrational to believe it.)

So suppose I do that. I rationally believe some fake news articles shared with me by co-partisans. And, let's suppose, the upshot of those articles is that such-and-such people are super-trustworthy and so-and-so other people are super-untrustworthy.

That is to say: by believing those news articles, I start the process of getting caught in an echo chamber.

Since believing those news articles was rational, by Rini's lights, it must be the case that the result of those beliefs is also rational.

But... that can't be right, can it?

Surely it's never *rational* to get caught in an echo chamber? And since Rini's view says otherwise, surely that's a good reason to reject her view?

Does that capture (part of) what you were arguing?

Since I don't have any control over whether *everyone* shares fake news, my choice seems pretty straight forward. I can either share fake news or not. If I do, then I at least get the benefit of persuading some people some of the time, regardless of how much other people are sharing fake news. If I don't, then I still don't have any control over whether social media gets overrun by fake news, but I now don't get to reap the personal benefits of doing so myself.

Thus, fake news is *individually* sensible but *collectively* disastrous.
