Q: On Olberding’s racial injustice worries / kneeling for the anthem

This is interesting\! Let me offer a couple thoughts on Olberding's behalf:

(i) Regarding the passage you quoted,  ("Uncivil black men will not be taken as refreshingly 'real', but instead as threatening or dangerous"), I take it that Olberding's point is this:

*For many of us, being socially admired for being uncivil is a kind of privilege, a benefit we receive merely because of our demographic characteristics. When I am uncivil as a white male (or in Amy's case, as a white woman), my friends and acquaintances \-- and even strangers \-- may admire me for keeping it real, for speaking truth to power, for being brave and independent in thought and deed.*

*However, not everyone is likely to be met with that reception. A black man who is uncivil, for instance, may not be interpreted as **brave** and **independent** but as threatening or dangerous. (Psychology research has consistently demonstrated that Americans tend to have implicit tendencies to interpret black men in those ways.)*

*That's not good\! That's a subtle form of unfairness. When \*I\* am uncivil, I am admired. When \*others\* are uncivil, they are not. So by being uncivil I may be contributing to that unfairness.*

 

So this is a extra, small argument in defense of civility. (By being uncivil we may contribute to ongoing and unjust racial disparities. We should avoid actions that do that. Therefore etc.)

 

(ii) The kneeling-during-the-national-anthem case is a really interesting one because it's controversial whether it counts as civil or as uncivil. Many people think that kneeling during the anthem is a **civil** way of expressing disagreement; it is non-threatening and thoughtful and not obviously intolerant and, in some respects at least, is respectful of other people's view points. Other people think that kneeling during the anthem is **uncivil** because they think it clearly expresses disrespect (toward the flag; toward the country; toward the troops; toward other Americans.)

So it's a hard case precisely because people disagree about whether or not it counts as a case of incivility\!

I don't know what Olberding would say about that case. Are we permitted to engage in acts whose civility is in dispute? If so, why? If not, why not?

Do you (or anyone else) have any thoughts about that?

Q: What counts as civil?

So for Olberding, **a behavior counts as *civil* when (and only when) it symbolically demonstrates, performs, or displays pro-social values like respect, consideration, and toleration**. 

That definition tells us that "what counts as civil?" is going to differ culture-to-culture, context-to-context. She herself is fond of making comparisons between civility and politeness. When it comes to both politeness and civility, she thinks, it's just a matter of *social convention.* Is it polite to kiss a person on lips upon meeting them? Depends on what the social conventions are 'round here. Is it civil to make eye contact while a person is talking to you? Depends on what the social conventions are 'round here. (i.e. *How will the people here interpret that action? What symbolic meaning does it carry in the local body-language lexicon?*)

So: How do you convey civility to someone? You should try to do those things with you body / voice / language that you expect the other person will interpret as *civil.* Similar remarks apply regarding **in**civility. You can convey incivility by performing whichever actions you expect the other person will interpret as *uncivil.*

(Of course, if you're interacting with a stranger from a strange land, you won't know what sorts of behaviors they are likely to interpret as civil or uncivil. But... that's not really a problem for Olberding's view. That's just a language barrier. She can say, "Try to be civil as best you can, and if you mess up, that'll be a case of *accidental* incivility.")

Q: Is it ever bad to be civil in cases of ongoing social dispute?

Maybe\!

Suppose I'm an atheist, and suppose it is a matter of ongoing social dispute in my country whether atheists are worthy of moral consideration. (Some people think, *people who deny the existence of God don't have rights.)* If I find myself in conversation with one of those people, should I be civil? It *is* a matter of ongoing social dispute. But, we might reason, surely it's not morally good for me to demonstrate *respect* and *consideration* for the view that I do not have rights. (Like, "I disagree with you, sir. I think I that I actually do have some human rights. But, please, tell me your reasons for thinking that I am morally on-a-par with fish. I will consider what you have to say." How could that be morally righteous?\!)

^ This is hypothetical, obviously. But there are probably isomorphic situations that real people actually do find themselves in.

I don't know \- is that satisfying? Thoughts about that case? Did you have any other sorts of cases in mind, as possible situations where civility would be bad? (despite the issue being a matter of ongoing social dispute)

Q: On expert disagreement

Those are good questions\!

 

This is an important ongoing issue in epistemology: 

*When (and to what extent) should we trust experts \-- especially in the sciences? And what should we do when different experts disagree?*

As you point out, part of what makes these questions so hard is that *experts themselves* are not politically neutral, epistemically perfect agents. They have their own biases, prejudices, and irrational habits of thought.

 

I don't have any worked-out answers here, but I might be able to outline what directions we might want to take to make progress. It might help to start by looking at cases of expertise where politics seems to be largely absent:

Here's a case. Suppose a Professor in Mathematics tells me that *we have finally proven the Honeycomb Conjecture.* (it says "any partition of the plane into regions of equal area has perimeter at least that of the regular hexagonal grid.") I am not equipped to understand the proof, having only minored in Math, so I can't assess the purported proof myself to decide if it succeeds.

*Should I trust this expert?* 

Seemingly, yes\! I don't have any reason to think he'd lie to me about this. Nor can I see how this conclusion of his might have been unduly influence by his politics. Math is pretty apolitical as STEM disciplines go.

*What if I learn that other mathematicians disagree about the soundness of the proof?*

That's harder. But I think we can point to relevant considerations. Seemingly, I should see *how many* other mathematicians disagree about this. (Is it just 1 or 2? Is it split 50/50?) The fewer that disagree, the more confident I should be.

I might also look at these peoples' track-records. Have some of them been more reliable in the past? If so, I should be more confident that they are right.

I might also see if any of the mathematicians have any arguments to offer (well, arguments that I can actually understand) in support of their position. If so, I should be more confident that the side with the better-seeming arguments are right.

 

It seems to me, that these are exactly the kinds of things I'll want to consider in other cases of expert disagreement \- even where there is a much higher likelihood of political bias.

In more political cases, I'll simply need to add to my list of considerations. I should try to work out whether any of the disagreeing parties have strong political reasons for holding their views, and try to adjust my confidence accordingly.

(This could also be something I should look for the math case\! There could be some Math Department politics going on in the background that would be relevant to my judgments about which side is more trustworthy. (Maybe the proof was discovered by an unlikeable person? Or maybe the proof was found by one university which is in a bitter rivalry with another?) If so, I should lower my confidence in accordance with how much bias I think each side is likely to have.)

This is all very complicated and a lot of work, but it seems to me like the right direction to go in to answer your questions.

 

Does that seem right to you (or to other people\!) ?

Q: A summary of my own conflicted feelings and the complexities of the situation

I myself am just really conflicted on this issue. Like, I'm pretty clear on the Big Picture objectives: obviously our number one priority should be protecting sex workers. But I go back-and-forth about how best to get there. I **do** genuinely want people to be able to do work that they like and find satisfying \-- and for some that may include sex work\! But I **don't** want people in desperate, low-income situations to have to turn to sex work when they'd really rather not. I **do** want sex workers to feel like normal, un-stigmatized members of society\! But I **don't** want us to start thinking about sex as something transactional and causal that's just another part of doing business \-- lest that undermine the broader importance of sexual consent, sexual freedom, and enjoyment. (A world where everything thinks of sex, "It's like graphic design, but with your genitals\!" seems ungreat? Seems like a world where we'd continue to have the same problems with rape and unjust sex norms.) I **do** want there to be occupational protections for sex workers, to prevent them from getting abused by clients and to give them legal options if they are \-- and I think those protections need to be in place *regardless* of the legal status of sex work. Etc.

I don't know what the best way to accomplish all that is, though.

Q: On Anderson’s explanation that we recoil B/C we value sexual autonomy

I think this is a very important objection to Anderson's argument. (In fact, I tend to think this is **the** weakest part of his position\!)

 

Anderson is really successful at getting us to have the right sorts of intuitions about a world where sex work is treated like other personal services. Pretty much everyone I've ever talked to about this paper recoils from his hypothetical world. So, that part of his argument seems pretty solid\!

But then the question is:

***Why** do we recoil? What explains our intense negative reactions to this world where sex world is just another personal service?*

There are basically two competing explanations on the table:

(\#1) We recoil because sex *really is* different and special.

(\#2) We recoil because *we have been culturally conditioned to **think*** sex is different and special. (Even though it's not, really.) We all carry around these deeply-ingrained, old-fashioned prudish attitudes, and it's those attitudes that are causing us to recoil.

 

Anderson obviously wants us to accept Explanation \#1. But, if I understand your post correctly, you are suggesting that maybe Explanation \#2 is better. 

And, yeah \- maybe\! Anderson doesn't say enough, I don't think, about why we should reject \#2. 

But then our question is this: *How do we decide? How do we tell whether our revulsion is caused by sex **actually being** different or by our deep cultural prejudices?*

 

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that? (that is, do you have any thoughts about how we might sort out which of the explanations is the correct one?)

Q: On whether we can agree w/ Anderson and still support legal sex work

Suppose we think that Anderson has successfully shown that we do actually think that sex work is different from other personal services *and* has convinced us that the difference amounts to our placing a very high value on sexual autonomy. We can still ask, *Is there not some way of organizing society so that the people who are willing to do sex work can be justly compensated and protected? Isn't there some way to ensure that people receive rewards commensurate to the value of their sexual autonomy?*

 

Maybe\! I think one suggestion earlier in this discussion thread is one promising suggestion. (Something like, *Maybe, e.g., sex workers could all be federal government employees with exacting training requirements, job protections, robust pensions and health insurance.*)

Would that be sufficient for us to count sex workers as being adequately compensated for surrendering their sexual autonomy? I don't know\! But, even if not, there are surely other sorts of employment schemes we could devise\!

Q: Clearing up the confusion about the conditional: IF sex work is just like other personal services…

The important point about P3 is that it's a conditional. It says **IF** sex work is just like other personal services **THEN** the best way to protect sex workers is by legalizing and normalizing. 

That claim is *not* saying that sex work actually is like other personal services. It's saying that *if* it is (maybe, maybe not\!), then this other thing follows.

Anderson seems to accept that P3 is true. He just happens to think the antecedent (the statement immediately after the IF) is false. 

 

Here's a different example that might help. Suppose I assert the following,

"If I have a million dollars when I turn 40, I will buy a big ol house boat."

That claim is *not* a prediction that I'll have a million dollars when I turn 40\. It's saying that *if* I do (maybe, maybe not\!), then this other thing follows. (I'll buy a house boat\!)

 

So that's what P3 is claiming. As for why P3 is *true*, the thought is this:

Imagine a world where it's genuinely and clearly the case that the only real difference between sex workers and massage therapists is that people feel icky about sex work. How do we protect sex workers in that world? What's the best way? Seemingly, the best way is to legalize sex work (just as we've legalized massage therapy), to regulate sex work and give workers legal protections, and then try really hard to get people stop feeling so icky about it.

That's what P3 is saying. **IF** sex work is just like other personal services, **THEN,** surely, the best way to help sex workers is to give them legal protections and try to feel less icky about sex work. So, Option 1, basically.

 

Does that help? Did that answer your question, or have I just been talking past you?

Q: On whether sex workers would still be better have A job rather than none

I think this a really lovely way of articulating (one reason) why this topic is so hard\!

On the one hand, sex work is very bad for sex workers. And, for the reasons Anderson offers, we might be worried that these bad outcomes fall disproportionately on those least able to bear them. On the other hand, things might be worse for sex workers if they couldn't engage in sex work. For many of them, sex work is their only reliable source of income. That's... a really tough situation, and it's hard make sense of what we should do about it. (I mean: obviously, we should end poverty; that's what we should do. But in the unjust meantime it's hard to know what we should do\!)

I tend to think this basic problem crops up all over the place, in lots of other markets. We'll talk about one next week: the market for organ sales. A not too dissimilar set-up.

I've also heard analogous concerns about sweatshop labor in developing nations. (*On the one hand, these jobs are objectively very bad; on the other hand, there aren't really any backup jobs for most of these exploited workers...*)

I'll be curious to hear if you (or other people\!) feel any differently about this sex work topic after next week. We'll encounter some arguments that have rather obvious bearing here.

Q: On why we maybe can’t eliminate prostitution

I suspect that Anderson (and the so-called "radical feminists" whose position he's defending) would ultimately be sympathetic with your suggestion. Mostly what they care about is protecting the interests of those who've been forced into sex work. If it turns out the best way to do that is to offer free job training to those individuals, to help them secure employment in other industries, I think they'd be totally on board. 

 

However, one might anticipate the following worry:

*Prostitution is often called "the world's oldest profession" and not without reason. There has always been a strong demand for sex, more sex than is available through non-transactional, consensual human relationships. For as long as there's been people, it seems, there's been people willing to pay others for sex. But if that's right, then... surely that will continue to be true in the future. There will continue to be people willing to pay others for sex. And if we know anything about economics, we know that whenever there's a high demand for a good, there will inevitably be people who try to figure out how to supply it. Thus, we can never get rid of sex work. Since there will always been some demand, there will always be people who, for the right amount of money, will be willing to supply.*

I suspect that something like this argument is what has lead the radical feminists to the "criminalize and prosecute" position. We can't *eliminate* sex work; the best we can do is create laws to disincentivize it.

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that line of argument?

**For persistent confusions about Anderson’s argument**

Anderson is responding to an important argument from the Legalize / Normalize crowd. 

One of the premises of that argument is, *Sex work is in principle no different from other kinds of personal services.* Call this claim 'S'.

Anderson thinks that claim is false. **More precisely,** he thinks that *you* (or, *most people*) not actually believe S to be true. His argument is intended to pump your intuitions that S is false.

To do that he asks us to imagine a hypothetical world where sex work is legalized and where sex work is treated exactly the same as other personal services \-- is subject to the same regulations, laws, and norms. I went through 7 of those in lecture, trying to explain what such a world would be like. 

Anderson argues: If I really believed that S were true \-- that sex work were no different from other personal services \-- I would not be bothered by that hypothetical world. But most people *are* bothered by that hypothetical world. Thus, most people do not actually think S is true.

The mistake that lots of people are making is in thinking that Anderson is **making a prediction** about what would happen in our world if we legalized sex work. 

It worth re-emphasizing: ***He is not**; Anderson **is not making predictions** about what would happen if we legalized sex work. That is not the point.*

Maybe none of his hypotheticals would ever actually happen. Maybe the hypothetical world he imagines *could never* be our actual world.  That's fine. That fact (if it is a fact) is not a problem for his argument. All he needs for his argument to work is for most people to **dislike** his hypothetical worlds. His point is simply this, "Most people do not actually, intuitively judge that sex work is just like other personal services. Most people intuitively want to treat sex work differently; they **dislike** a world where it is treated exactly the same."

Thus, pointing out that Anderson's world would never happen *does not undermine his argument. (*Since this is straightforwardly a question about what Anderson's argument *is,* this is the sort of thing I might ask about on the exam\! \*waggles eyebrows suggestively\*)

To be clear, there *are* plenty of good reasons why we might be skeptical of Anderson's arguments\! I don't mean to suggest Anderson is *correct* about the Legalize/Normalize argument. The point is that "his hypothetical world is implausible" is not among those reasons.

Q: On one person’s modus tollens being another person’s modus ponens

If I'm following your line of thought, you've suggested the following conditional is true,

If a person's valid consent is sufficient for waiving their right to bodily integrity, then assisted suicide (w/ consent) must be permissible (and, perhaps, should be legal, too.)

 

I think lots of people will agree with that conditional claim\! That is, lots of people will probably agree that the permissibility of assisted suicide (with consent) really does follow, if we accept that that valid consent is sufficient waiving the right to bodily integrity.

 

Here's an interesting Logic Fact\! 

This conditional actually gives rise to two different arguments, depending on whether you think the antecedent (i.e. the IF... bit) is obviously true or the consequent (i.e. the THEN... bit) is obviously false.

Arg \#1:

P1) If consent is sufficient for waiving my right to bodily integrity, then assisted suicide (w/ consent) must be permissible.

P2\*) Consent clearly is sufficient for waiving my right to bodily integrity

C\*) Assisted suicide must be permissible 

 

Arg \#2

P1) If consent is sufficient for waiving my right to bodily integrity, then assisted suicide (w/ consent) must be permissible.

P2^) Assisted suicide (even w/ consent) is clearly NOT permissible

C^) Consent is NOT sufficient for waving my right to bodily integrity

 

Those are both valid arguments. And both appeal to the truth of P1. The question is simply this: 

is P2\* true or is P2^ true? Pick one\! 

You seem to endorse Arg \#2\! Presumably, because you feel confident that P2^ is true. 

However, given public opinion polling on this issue (see here (Links to an external site.) for instance), I suspect many other people will endorse Arg \#1 instead\! They feel confident that P2\* is true. 

 This is true of all conditionals. In principle, we may agree that a particular conditional is true, but still arrive at very different conclusions because we disagree about the truth value of the parts of the conditional. (One of us thinks the antecedent is obviously true; from which it follows that the consequent is true. The other of us thinks the consequent is obviously false; from which follows that the antecedent is false.)

Q: On the limits of “give the poor more choices” arguments

Yeah, nice\! I think that's an excellent way of putting RR's point. (Like, *poverty is bad* and part of why poverty is bad is that people in poverty have to make some tough choices, make some major sacrifices, just to survive. Maybe it's even appropriate to say that poverty *coerces* people into making those tough choices\! But look: we don't do the poor any favors by preventing them from making those hard choices. If the choice is between *survival* and *avoiding a costly sacrifice,* we should at least let the poor person choose survival.)

What's interesting about that line of argument is that most people only accept it up to a point. There are some activities that people think of as being *so bad* that they favor banning them, even though keeping them legal might give the poor an extra way to survive. Child labor, for example. Most people are in favor of laws that ban children under a certain age from working for pay (with some exceptions for agriculture and entertainment industries). But: consider a desperately poor family consisting of two parents working full time and a 12 year old. Everyone in that family might reasonably prefer that the 12 year old go out and get a job. That's a major sacrifice for the 12 year old, of course, but that sacrifice may be preferable to not-starving. Nevertheless, most people seem to agree that it's best to keep child labor illegal \-- to, in effect, deny that family the option of sending their child to work.

 

So that's interesting\! That seems to demand some sort of explanation. How do we explain why (most people's) intuitions about those two cases diverge? That is, why does the argument you've given seem pretty good in the one case and not-that good in the other?

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Q: An obj to “Isn’t the black market worse?”

One major objection to this argument is that there already are black markets for kidneys. Thus, it's not quite true that legalizing kidney sales will create a new noxious market. It already exists\! And since it already exists, one might reason, we might as well legalize kidney sales and try to minimize the harm done to sellers.

I think that's a cogent objection\! 

But I think there's a plausible reply in the offing here too. So I wanted to try to explain that reply, to see what you thought about it.

 

The reply actually came up at the beginning of our conversation about Anderson.

So recall, Anderson points out that we have, effectively, two choices when it comes to sex work: Option 1: Legalize and Normalize or Option 2: Criminalize and Prosecute. It's not obvious what the correct answer is.

We noted in lecture: this same choice comes up in other contexts, too. But in those other contexts, sometimes the correct answer is obvious. 

I suggested as an example, black markets in child slavery. This practice is really awful for the children, obviously, and we might wonder: What's the best way to protect the enslaved children: Legalize/Normalize child slavery or Criminalize/Prosecute?

Obviously, the answer is Criminalize/Prosecute. 

But, notice: the dialectic here looks very similar\!

One person says: A market in kidneys will be noxious. We should keep it illegal.

Another points out: That black market basically already exists. Wouldn't it be better to legalize?

 

You might be able to answer:

Not necessarily\! It's equally true that a legal market in enslaved children would be noxious, and equally true that a black market already exists. But I am not inclined to say we should just legalize child slavery\! So can't we say the same thing about kidney sales? The black market is awful, but that's not a reason, by itself, to legalize? The government shouldn't be endorsing these practices\!

What do you (or other people\!) think about that reply?

Q: Another counterexample to the H.O.P. (minimum wage)

Yeah, that seems plausible to me, too. I think that *if* the U.S. were to legalize kidney sales, then we'd be likely to enact protections to prevent the sorts of *debt collateral* concerns that Satz is raising.

But \-- here's the interesting bit \-- supposing that's true, that doesn't mean Satz is wrong about the HOP. The HOP, recall, is a really strong claim:

**HOP \--** *It never does or could make someone worse off to give them one more choice.*    \<= (that's supposed to apply always in every possible case\!)

Satz' example still seems sufficient for showing that that claim is false \-- adding the option to selling a kidney could make someone worse off, if kidneys were then considered debt collateral. 

Here's a more interesting counter-example that Satz briefly mentions in the article, but develops more fully elsewhere:

Satz suggests that minimum wage laws might also count as a counterexample to the Harmless Options Principle.

A person who accepts the H.O.P. might reason like this: Suppose we abolished the minimum wage laws. People could now work for 1 dollar an hour. And this is harmless\! We've just given people one more option than they had before\! As always, having one extra option is not bad for you. If you don't want to work for 1 dollar an hour, just ignore your new option.

Satz worries that this new option could, in principle, affect the decision-making of other agents, elsewhere in the economy and end up being a net-harm for people. She's particularly concerned about effects on the public school system.

In one sense, the whole point of the public school system, from the perspective of the Federal Govt, is to educate and train citizens well enough for them to become contributing members of society. So understood, the minimum wage laws actually represent a meaningful constraint on the public education system: Since no citizen can legally work for less than $7.50, we must educate our citizens at least well enough for them to be able to produce $7.50 worth of value / hour. If we fail at that, our citizens will be unemployable and our school system will have failed in its single, overriding aim.

If the minimum wage laws were abolished, Satz thinks, this constraint would no longer be in place. In principle, if people could work for $2 / hr, then the state could get away with educating people only far enough for them to hit that level of marginal productivity. Put differently, Satz suggests that minimum wage laws are a kind of commitment that the state is making to educational standards. The state is effectively saying, "We regard it as unacceptable for our citizens to have a level of education below this threshold – the $7.25 / hour level of marginal productivity – and we are committed to ensuring that all citizens are skilled enough and educated enough to qualify for jobs paying above that minimum wage." That commitment undergirds the education system and has an influence on all sorts of educational policy-making\!

When we abolish the minimum wage, we are just giving people more choices than they had before. (“Now you have access to lower-paying jobs\! You couldn't choose those before, but now you can\!") But, Satz says, that isn’t by definition harmless. There could be harmful effects where other agents change their behavior, in light of the knowledge that people have this new option. (in this case: the other agents she has in mind are the people in charge of public schooling.)

 

Of course, even if we decide that the HOP is false, we should still ask, Is there some weaker version of the HOP that avoids these counter-examples and that we should accept? Like, maybe: *In general, giving X one more option does not limit X’s freedom nor make X worse off than she was before \-- unless the addition of that option undermines other choices.* ?

That weaker principle may be enough to defend the legalization of kidney sales, so long as we put in place the kinds of protections you mentioned. (Where the point of those protections is to make sure that the extra choice to sell a kidney do not undermine any other choices.

Any thoughts about any of that? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: On whether legalizing sales would decrease kidney supplies

Obviously, Satz ultimately doesn't come down one way or the other on this issue. (i.e. she doesn't say, "So legalizing kidney sales will result in fewer available kidney's" nor the opposite of that), but it's a really interesting concern, and I'm glad you've brought it up\!

 

And, I think you're argument is pretty plausible\! 2 out of 3 kidneys come from dead people and since, as you point out, there's no reason to think people would stop being organ donors merely because kidney sales are legalized, there actually aren't that many kidney donations to be lost\! (Only the 1/3 donated by the living.) That seems right to me.

 

One other thought I had about this:

In many cases, the living people who donate kidneys are donating to a relative or acquaintance. That's a special sort of case, one where the altruistic motive is specifically directed at a particular person. And I wonder: Would people really be less willing to donate a kidney to their beloved aunt or neighbor merely because kidney sales are legal?

I kinda doubt it\!

If that's right, then there are even fewer kidney donations that might realistically be threatened, be crowded out by a legal buying/selling market. Which, again, may lead us to conclude that legalizing kidney sales really will result in more kidneys for transplant.

Q: On how we should respond to weak agency concerns

I like this\! This is really interesting and thoughtful. 

 Weak agency really does seem to be a serious problem. (It's also a problem for sex workers. We didn't talk about that explicitly last week, but it bears mentioning.) In selecting social policies, we should want to adopt ones that minimize weak agency, that minimize the extent to which vulnerable people are pressured into making decisions that they would not reflectively endorse for themselves. (that's not the only relevant criterion for selecting policies, of course, but it's an important one.)

And you've correctly picked out that the question about kidney sales is one that's non-obvious to answer:

Can we better protect those with weak agency by keeping this practice illegal (and, thus, reducing the number of opportunities for persons with weak agency to be pressured into these decisions) or can we better protect people by legalizing this practice (and, thus, giving the government some ability to step in and regulate the practice, to protect those with weak agency?

Hard question. My first instinct is to say, The correct answer may differ society-to-society. We should ask, for instance: how many people in society X are in a position of weak agency? How effective and competent is the government of X \-- at stamping out black markets? at regulating medical procedures? at ensuring its populace is not living in poverty? at providing people with weak agency access to legal protections, in general? (some societies, for instance, are still pretty unjust when it comes to giving equal legal protections to women and men.)

That's my first-pass reaction anyway. Do you have any thoughts about that?

Q: On the parallels between sex work and kidney sales

I'm glad you picked up on the parallels between the sex work and kidney sales arguments\! I didn't have a ton of time in lecture to draw those similarities out explicitly, unfortunately, so I'm happy that you noticed them anyway \- and spent some time thinking about it.

As you point out, the kidney sale case is, in some sense, just a reweighted version of the sex work case. The arguments for legalizing kidney sales are way more forceful (thousands of lives could be saved\!) than the arguments for legalizing sex work. While the arguments against are, if anything, a little weaker. (protecting people from one bad surgery and, maybe, some future bad health outcomes seems less pressing than protecting sex workers from just all the awful stuff that sex workers have to deal with.)

And, it's worth pointing out, one could also wield the Harmless Option Principle is support of legalizing sex work. ("Letting people work as sex workers just gives them one more option than they had before. Giving X one more option than she had before does not...") And, accordingly, one could interpret Anderson as giving arguments along the same lines as Satz: pointing out certain subtle respects in which the H.O.P. may sometimes fail. (We could interpret him that way, but probably shouldn't. It's pretty clear that he intends for his arguments to be undermining the claim that sex work is in principle no different than other personal services \-- and not to be undermining the H.O.P.)

We might be able to generate an argument in support of organ sales parallel to the original argument in support of sex work:  
Selling a kidney is, in principle, no different from selling other body parts (e.g. semen, hair, plasma) except with extra stigma and more risk of long-term poor health. If that's right, then the best way to protect kidney sellers is to legalize the practice, offer sellers robust healthcare, and normalize. 

Then the question is: is that argument persuasive? (As before, the interest bit is the claim about kidney selling being "no different")  
Any thoughts about that?

Q: On consent issues and “consenting adults” args in organ sales and sex work

I wonder if what's going on in each of these different issues is that there's a deep disconnect between the simplified, easy cases of consent that we imagine in our heads and the actual, messy kinds of cases that crop up in the real world.

Like, it's easy for me to imagine a couple of hyper-rational, free and autonomous adults (maybe standing together in the middle of nowhere, unattached to any particular society or social context) and think, *Surely these two consent adults can do anything they want together, so long as both enthusiastically consent\!* 

(Do they want to try some weird sex thing where one of them is the prisoner of the other for a year? Go ahead\! You're both consenting adults\!

Do they want to try eating human flesh and enthusiastically agree to taste-test on one of their left feet? Go ahead\! You're both consenting adults\!

Do they want to practice plastic surgery on each other? Go ahead\! You're both consenting adults\!

etc.)

While I have those judgments and find them pretty forceful, I think I'm often too quick to use those judgments (which are, ultimately, judgments about abstract, idealized cases\!) as reasons to endorse particular laws or social policies. Like, "Well, if it's ok for two hypothetical, consenting adults do X, so surely X should be legal, right?"

But, for the reasons you're pointing to, real world cases of X are often really messy. People mostly aren't hyper-rational, free and autonomous adults. People are all tangled up in social obligations, contorted by competing social pressures, experiencing stress and poverty and anxiety, subject to moments of despair or weakness.

And when we make X-ing legal, we are making it possible for *people like that* to X (to sell a kidney, to do sex work, to request assisted suicide, etc) and not just making it possible for our hypothetical ideal adults. And we need to keep that in mind. We need to think about whether we want *actual people with messy lives and non-ideal circumstances* to be able to X.

Anyway, that's my hot take about what might be going on with our intuitions in these cases.

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Q: Two objections to Dahaner

Good yeah\! You seem to me raise a couple important objections to Dahaner.

As a first-pass reply, I think Dahaner would say this: people who prefer to work in the ETU will still be able to do work-like activities even if they don't strictly speaking have jobs. So, e.g., if you really liked car repair, you could do that\! You could buy up old cars and fix them, or offer to help your neighbors with their car troubles. You just wouldn't have a paid job as a mechanic, since all those jobs are done by robots.

But you've raised, it seems to me, two important objections to that account:

(i) What about people who want to do tasks in industries that can't be done on-the-side and for free? What about someone who, say, really wants to be a surgeon? If robots are performing all the surgeries (and doing a much better job than humans ever could), then what is the human surgeon to do? It would be unethical to let her do surgery on people. (B/c those same operations could be done more safely by a robot) Isn't the would-be surgeon worse off in the ETU? 

(ii) What about people who specifically want **a job** \-- i.e. who want to be paid for their labor, to work a 9-5 like their grandparents used to. (Even though, let's suppose, they don't need the money. They just really like the idea of having a job.) If there aren't any jobs in the ETU, then aren't they worse off? Isn't it bad for them that they can't get a paying job?

Q: Responding to concerns about the ETU leading to poor health / stupidity

Interesting\! 

I should think, if anything, people's physical health would be likely to improve in the ETU. At least, many people \*say\* that they aren't getting enough exercise because they're too busy. So we might think that the ETU would be a place where people are physically more fit. (Of course, people might be lying about their reasons for not exercising or, more charitably, they may be overly optimistic about how much exercise they'd actually get in a world where they have lots more leisure time. But still: that's at least a reason to be optimistic about physical health in the ETU. Also: health insurance. By stipulation, people will have enough money to actually pay for their own healthcare, which is not true in the U.S. today. That too is a reason to be optimistic about physical and mental health in the ETU.)

There's also pretty strong research suggesting that chronic stress is a major component of bad health. Since chronic stress is strongly associated both with poverty and with poor work-life balance, this is one more reason we might be optimistic about health outcomes in the ETU. (We might also note, in support of this observation, people with high socio-economic status consistently both get more exercise and have better health outcomes than people with low socio-economic status. So maybe a future where everyone's socio-economic status is kinda high is one were people act more like today's rich than like today's poor?)

But you may well be right about people's mental health / mental acuity declining in the ETU. I guess I have two thoughts about that:   
(i) We might look to retirees to see how well people handle idleness. It seems to me that lots of people do pretty well. Studies suggest retirees are on average happier than they were while working. (One study I came across said that \~50% liked their post-retirement lives better while only \~18% liked it less.) Of course this comparison is complicated by a couple factors: First, retirees have worked for most of their lives. Maybe people who have worked do better in idleness than people who haven't? (The opposite could be true, too.) Second, these numbers might under-estimate the happiness of retirees b/c it's hard to disentangle the effects of retirement from the effects of poor health (which is both associated with advanced age and in some cases, a cause of retirement.)

(ii) I'm not sure whether I think a loss of mental acuity per se is bad for people. (Certainly, depression is bad for people\! But is it bad simply to become less sharp, less intelligent overall?) This is true especially when I imagine a person who's greatest dream in life is to be a couch potato: it's not obvious to me that it's bad for that person to lose some mental acuity \- at least, not when I assume they don't mind. (if it's distressing to them that they've lost acuity, then that's bad for them. But then... my explanation for why it's bad is that it's bad to feel distressed about stuff.)

Q: On whether our work-is-virtuous intuitions are unreliable

But let me pitch a different argument in support of Dahaner:

It seems very plausible to me that our intuitions about the ETU are muddied by some strong social beliefs in the U.S. surrounding the importance of work, productivity, and efficiency \-- attitudes which date back at least to the Puritans. But I wonder, (a) would we hold onto those attitudes in the ETU and, if we didn't, would we thereby be better able to enjoy lives of leisure? and (b) to what extent are these Puritan Work Ethic beliefs defensible?

Let me give an example of a Puritan Work Ethic belief that's not (I don't think) defensible:

 

A lot of people have the intuition that people who get up early in the morning are more virtuous than people who get up late. (I also have this intuition.) This is a pretty old cultural belief, one that seems to date back, again, at least to the Puritans.

But is this belief defensible? I doubt it. Suppose Person X gets up at 5 am, works for 8 hours, then goes to bed by 9pm while Person Y gets up at 11am, works for 8 hours then goes to bed by 3am. 

While part of me really does want to say that Y is a lazy oaf, that reaction doesn't actually seem defensible. He doesn't actually do less work.  He just gets up later than X and then goes to bed later, too. But that difference should be irrelevant to his industriousness. The conclusion I want to draw is that my intuitions about early-rising being a virtue is a confused Puritan leftover that I'd be better off without. (Maybe it was true of farmers that late-risers didn't get their work done before the sun went down. But it's not true of data analysts or computer programmers or whatever. That heuristic (early-rising \= virtue) isn't a good rule of thumb anymore.)

 

So maybe our beliefs about the importance of work are like that? Leftover prejudices, instilled in us by a work-obsessed culture, that are ultimately not very defensible? Beliefs that we could safely jettison in the ETU and, thereby, help ourselves to happy lives of leisure?  
Admittedly, that argument needs more work than I've given it. But it's a start of a possible reply anyway\!

Thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

**On loneliness concerns in the ETU**  
In particular, I think your concerns about solitude and isolation in the ETU bears some thinking about. If I'm understanding you correctly, the thought is this:

Dahaner points out a number of critical goods that workers in today's society can only really attain by having a job. He's argued that we would be able to attain those goods in alternative ways in the ETU. But perhaps there's another critical good that he's overlooked: social interaction.

There's some good psychology research suggesting that humans who don't interact regularly with other humans have poor health outcomes. And, of course, one of the primary places that most people socialize with others is at work. Thus, here's one more critical good that we attain through our jobs.

The obvious question is: Would people still get the same level of socializing in the ETU? 

 

I'm genuinely uncertain how to answer that question. On the one hand, we might think that people in the ETU would have a lot of leisure time and would naturally desire to spend some of that leisure time with others \-- joining clubs, sports, social groups, etc. Being with other people is, for the most part, enjoyable. So we should expect that the ETU will be full of people seeking out this enjoyable activity. On the other hand, the recent history of humanity seems to be one in which people are increasingly replacing old forms of socialization with inferior new forms \-- that is, "inferior" in the sense that the new forms seem to be less-good for us, seem not to be as effective at staving off loneliness. (e.g. texting instead of talking on the phone; commenting on YouTube rather than joining a book club; playing Madden rather than playing in an actual football game.) We might expect that trend to continue in the ETU.

If so, this may be one respect in which the ETU would be bad for people. Whereas today most people get some social interaction through their jobs, in the ETU they won't. (b/c no jobs.) And if people don't seek out replacement social activities, everyone will be a little more lonely and isolated.

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that? Feelings or arguments about whether we should be concerned about loneliness in the ETU?

Q: On Dahaner’s assumption that the ETU is possible

Yeah, that's interesting\! As you point out: Dahaner's whole project is premised on this major assumption: we could eventually find ourselves in a world where poverty has been eliminated and the govt adequately provides for everyone.  
And... wow. That's a big assumption. And I think you're right to wonder about it. (Is that plausible? Could we ever arrive at that world? Could there be such a trustworthy and reliable govt?)  
   
I think it's worth asking: Is there any reason to care about Dahaner's project if we don't think the ETU could ever happen? 

And the answer, I think, is *probably yes*\!

There are a couple things that I think Dahaner might want to say here:

First, he might argue that the ETU is a future worth considering simply because... people are notoriously really awful at correctly predicting the future \-- especially the future course of technology. Like, *really* terrible. So when it comes to trying to imagine the future 100 or 200 years out from now, maybe all we can do is shrug and ask "What if?" questions. (c.f. *the entire genre of science fiction*). Some of those "What If"s may come to pass; most won't. But given how awful we are at predicting which are which, we can't afford to be picky about which futures we allow ourselves to contemplate.

Second, sometimes those "What if?" questions may tell us interesting and useful things about \*ourselves right now\*. Like, *Fahrenheit 451* is a big What If? book. But it's a worthwhile exercise in speculation because it tells us something interesting and important about the dangers of censorship, the value of education and literature, and the risks of authoritarianism. The fact that *Fahrenheit 451* will never happen is not a reason to stop reading or thinking about it\! It has value outside of it's predictive accuracy.

I suspect Dahaner would want to say something similar about the "What If?" exercise that he's engaged in. The fact that the ETU will never happen (if that is a fact\!) is not a reason to stop thinking about it\! It has value outside of its predictive accuracy. He might say,

*The Era of Technological Employment is a useful thought-experiment, even if it's not something we could ever realize in the real world, because it allows us to think about **the value of working** in isolation from **the value of having an income**. It gives us a helpful way to think about **why** we work and whether work is something worth doing for its own sake. If we find ourselves dreading the ETU, that strongly suggests that we think work is intrinsically beneficial. If we find ourselves yearning for the ETU, that strongly suggests that we think work is only beneficial insofar as it helps us materially provide for ourselves. And, either way, the reasons we have for liking/disliking the ETU will help us make sense of our feelings about work. That is, this What If? can be helpful even if it never comes to be.*

(Though, Dahaner would surely also grant: his project is more interesting and relevant if we think the ETU is a plausible future outcome\!)

 

Any thoughts about either of those replies? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: How does punishment work in the ETU? And surely prejudice and discrimination will still be a problem? How will they rear their ugly heads?

I take it the thought is something like this:

There are a bunch things about our current society that are bad. Here's an incomplete list: (1) Poverty and income inequality; (2) Interpersonal violence (e.g. domestic abuse) ; (3) Identity-group inequalities (e.g. racism.)   
Dahaner asks us to imagine a future version of the world where bad thing \#1 has been eliminated, because jobs have been eliminated. He asks, "Would that world be good for us?"  
You're pointing out, It's not quite that simple because bad thing \#1 is entangled in complex ways with bad thing \#2 and bad thing \#3. Like the ETU is presumably still a place with domestic abuse. But how does punishment work in a world where no one has a job and everyone has an adequate income? Maybe the ETU is undesirable in the sense that it makes it harder for us to punish people who mistreat others. Also: the ETU is presumably still a place with discrimination and prejudice\! People will still be classist, racist, sexist, ableist. But... if everyone has the same income and the same employment situation, where will discrimination and prejudice rear their ugly heads? Will violence get worse? Will other forms of structural inequality get worse? 

I have no idea what Dahaner is going to say about those questions\! They're really good questions, though.  
Part of me thinks this is just an important and under-appreciated objection to D's project: it doesn't even exactly make sense to try to imagine the ETU as "Everything the same but no poverty or jobs."  You can't pluck poverty out of our society without tugging on everything else\! (e.g. violence, racism, and maybe sexism? I think that's implicated here, too. Think about how much would change if women and men all had the same job prospects and the same income. It would change dating\! And parenting\! And divorce\!)

To answer your question, though: I guess I'm tentatively optimistic? Disrupting poverty would have all sorts of downstream effects, both positive and negative. But I'm attracted to the thought that it would be more blessing than curse, a chance to do better, to fix or mitigate some of the more persistent and awful injustices in our society?

Q: On the alternative explanations for why we haven’t destroyed ourselves yet

I think you’re raising an important point about Bostrum's position. He thinks the best explanation for why we haven't destroyed ourselves is that we've been lucky. You seem to favor a different explanation.

**He** looks at the history of technological development and thinks,

*We haven't destroyed ourselves yet; tech has been mostly good for us. But surely we cannot simply assume that the future will resemble the past. There seem to be so many ways for tech to go wrong\! Surely we've merely been lucky.*

 

**You** sound like you want to draw the opposite conclusion,

*We haven't destroyed ourselves yet; tech has been mostly good for us. Thus, doesn't all our evidence support optimism about the future? Surely, the correct conclusion is that tech will continue to be mostly good for us.*

 

Leaving aside the Problem of Induction, I'm not sure how to adjudicate between those two lines of reasoning. It's true that tech development has been fine so far. But what explains that observation? Is it that we've been lucky (H1) or is it that there are no black ball technologies (H2)?

Maybe we need to look for more evidence to help us decide between those two hypotheses?

But if so, what would that evidence look like?

Q: On Bostrum’s thesis being wishy-washy

I think he's basically trying his best to be honest with us about what he's doing: **he's trying to predict the future** and **that's a hard thing to do.** 

Especially when it comes to *technological* predictions, people are notoriously bad at guessing what the future will be like. Bostrum is sensitive to that problem, I think, and that's why he states his thesis weakly. He says, *I don't know if the Vulnerable World Hypothesis is true, but I don't think it's obviously false.*

Part of the problem is, as you suggest, that it's not even clear what solid proof would look like for this kind of future-prediction claim\! How could you ever possibly **prove** that the future will be such-and-such a way? So he doesn't try. He just wants us to think that it's **sensible** to be worried about humanity inventing a black ball technology. 

He could be mistaken about that\! Maybe the VWH **is** obviously false?

Or, he could be mistaken in not trying to prove the truth of the VWH\! Maybe he gave up to easily and should have looked for a better argument?

Q: Reply to Bostrum: Just put technologists in charge of everything

Yeah, that’s interesting.

Insofar as we can successfully install people with technological know-how (and people who are reflective and thoughtful) into policy-making positions, that might indeed help us prevent some of the black balls that Bostrum is concerned about. It often feels like our policy-makers are 5-10 years out of step with technology, constantly playing catch-up and whack-a-mole with emerging technological problems \-- and, even then, with only middling success. So it does seem plausible that better-informed policy makers would be able to do a bit better at preventing devastation and disaster.

Though, that still leaves the global problem to grapple with. Even if we, the American public, manage to get wise technologists into positions of power in our govt, there are new technologies being developed and implemented all over the world that we'll still have no great way to guide or regulate. That, again, may suggest a need for some sort of global oversight? I don't know\!

Q: Maybe there’s a reason why nukes are hard to make\!

We have this observation about our world: *Nuclear weapons are really hard to make. Engineering one from scratch requires (a) a PhD-level understanding of physics and (b) millions of dollars and (c) hard-to-obtain materials.* 

How do we explain that fact?

Bostrum's explanation: *Nuclear weapons are hard to make... by accident, basically. There was no guiding intelligence; no one ever **decided** that nukes would be hard to make.*

Your proposed explanation (assuming I'm understanding you correctly): *Nuclear weapons are hard to make **by design.** Someone **decided** that nukes would be hard to make in order to keep our world safe.*

On your explanation, it seems we have a way to reject the VWH. If **someone is actively protecting us from black balls,** then we don't have to be worried about technological progress ultimately leading to societal devastation.

I like that reply\! That's a subtle argument against Bostrum.

I'm not totally clear that I'm following your train of thought about *who* is protecting us from black balls though. Are you thinking it's human governments? Or a diving being? Something else?

If it's the government protecting us from black balls, then we might wonder whether that story is adequate to explain all the facts about nuclear weapons. Like, one reason why it's hard to make nukes is that uranium is really hard to enrich. That process is just really demanding. I'm not a nuclear scientist, of course, but it does seem a bit odd to say that *the government* decided that uranium would be hard to enrich. (Relatedly, it sounds weird to say that *the government* decided that nuclear physics would be hard for the human brain to comprehend?) It kinda seems like uranium is hard to enrich... naturally and not as a result of anyone's decision-making? 

Then again, that story is a lot easier to tell if we are imagining God as protecting us from black balls\! The U.S. govt doesn't get to decide the laws of physics but maybe God does\! And maybe God has engineered the universe in such a way that we won't ever invent a black ball.

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that? What's the plausible story for us to tell about who is protecting us / how we're being protected from black balls?

Q: On the weirdness of the ontological argument arg

The argument's method is \-- and I cannot stress this enough \-- genuinely, bafflingly strange.

Anselm asks us to think really hard about the concept of God. By doing this, he claims, we will simply see (using pure logic\!) that our concept of God applies to something that really, actually exists in the world.

But.... WHAT?\!

That just seems so obviously confused as a methodology. Surely you can't figure out which things exist in the world just by sitting around with your eyes closed and thinking really hard?\! ("Are there any dodo birds left in the world?" is not a question you can answer by just thinking really hard on it.)

Of course, that doesn't tell us why or where the argument goes wrong. But it does seem to make the argument unpersuasive. It's too weird to work\!

Q: On the assumption that every being belongs in exactly 1 category

Good question. Let me take a crack at answering it.

There's a background assumption at play here which is that *every being is in exactly one of our three categories. **Every** being: from bananas to pokemon to round squares \- each particular kind of being is either Cat 1 (and NOT in 2 or 3\) or in Cat 2 (and NOT in 1 or 3\) or in Cat 3 (and NOT in 1 or 2).* This assumption hopefully seems pretty intuitive\! A being that exists (i.e. is in Cat 1\) can't *also* be a being that doesn't exist; a being that is impossible (i.e. is in Cat 3\) can't *also* be a being that's possible. 

Thus, Anselm's God also belongs in exactly one of the categories. That's why we can't place God in *both* 1 and 2\. No beings are in two categories at once.

Q: On conceivability and possibility

When we try to actually sort various beings into our 3-Categories, we ultimately confront the question, *How do we tell whether a being is impossible or not? What distinguishes the impossible beings from all the rest?*

In lecture, I relied pretty heavily on the metric of *conceivability.* If you can **conceive** of a being, probably that means it's possible. And if you can't even conceive of it, probably that means it's impossible. But, as you point out, *conceivability* is an imperfect proxy for possibility\! Some of the things in the universe may be *inconceivable by human minds* and yet still exist. (e.g. the dimensions posited by string theory) And yet other things may *seem conceivable* while actually being impossible. 

One famous example: *the barber who shaves everyone who does not shave themselves:*

Suppose there's a town, and in that town is a barber. The barber has a policy: for any man in town, the barber shaves that man if and only if that man does not shave himself (like, at home or whatever.) But wait: the barber lives in town, so does the barber shave himself? Well, if the barber DOESN'T shave himself, then, by his own policy, he must shave himself. (He shaves all the men who don't shave themselves\!) And, on the other hand, if he DOES shave himself, then by his own policy, he doesn't shave himself. Either way, we've run ourselves into a logical contradiction: the man both does and does not shave himself. The description of our barber appears incoherent.

But then... if we can't rely on our ability to **conceive,** how are supposed to tell which beings are impossible? And, moreover, doesn't that undermine P2 of the Ontological Argument? ("God is not in Cat 1 b/c we can imagine God.") Apparently, the fact that we can imagine something may not actually tell us all that much about whether that sort of being can exist\!

Q: On how Rowe is using the word ‘impossible’

Part of what's going on is that there are these concepts of *possibility* and *necessity* and *impossibility* that are already present in our everyday language. Like, normal people really do say things like, "It's impossible to go faster than the speed of light" and "it's impossible to be angry at you for more than a few minutes" or "It's impossible to multiply a number by two and have the result be odd."

Pretty clearly the notion of *impossibility* being used in those three sentences is very different\! What Rowe is trying to do is take one of those meanings (the third one) and try to be a bit more precise about what is being claimed. He's suggesting: *when we say it's impossible to multiply by two and get an add number, we're saying something not just about OUR abilities but about what can be done **anywhere** by **anyone.** Noone can multiply by two and get an odd number\!*

That suggests that it might be useful to think about *other hypothetical universes* when we consider which thinks are impossible. Like, imagine a universe with really different physical laws than ours. Can the people in *that* universe divide by two and get an odd number? Seemingly not\! If that's right, then it seems like this rule of ours ("It's impossible to divide by two and get an odd number") really is true **everywhere \--** and not just, like, around here.

Q: Is Anselm allowed to make up his own def of God?

I think that last point your raise is a particularly interesting an important concern about the Ontological Argument. To many people, it feels a bit illicit for Anselm to introduce his own definition of 'God' and then argue that *that* being exists. Like, what about people who don't think of God as *the greatest possible being*? Isn't there room for reasonable disagreement about what God is like?

One thing we can say in Anselm's defense is this,

*Strictly speaking,* the Ontological Argument doesn't ever need to use the word 'God' to work. Anselm could have used another name instead: **Let's define 'George' as the greatest possible being.** In that case, what his argument would show is that George exists. Really, then, the implication of the Ontological Argument is that there is in existence in our universe an entity who meets the description 'the greatest possible being'. Anselm wants to call that entity 'God', but you don't have to, if you don't want\! He's content if you accept that George exists, where 'George' is the name of the greatest possible being.

Q: Another explanation of contingency, necessity, and possibility

One helpful way of thinking about these concepts (*necessity, impossibility, possibility)* is to imagine an all-powerful God creating a bunch of different universes and filling them with different things. Not for any particular reason, just playing around with it. Using this metaphor, we can describe beings as being *necessary* or *contingent* or *impossible* according to whether God has a choice in placing them in each universe.

Most beings are **contingent** \-- God **can decide** whether to include them in each universe. Centaurs? Maybe\! Some universes may include them and others don't. ATMs? Maybe\! Some universes include them and others don't. (Some of the contingent beings are **real,** meaning they actually exist in our universe. Others are **not-real but possible.** They don't actually exist in our universe, but an all-powerful God *could have* included them no problem.)

Some beings are **impossible** \-- God **never** gets to include them in his universes. (examples might include a round square. Round squares don't make sense, are incoherent, so God can't create them and can't insert them into any universes.)

Finally, some beings are **necessary** \-- God **always** has to include them in each universe. (examples might include **oneness** \-- i.e. the number one, not the arabic numeral '1'. Can God create a universe and not have the number one be there? Surely not\! After all, the universe itself is **one thing.**)

So in lecture and in the reading, we were just asking of different things, "Which category does this belong in? Is this something that God **can't** create? Or does God get to choose?" 

Will Smith and Will Smith's evil twin are both contingent. Our hypothetical God can decide whether to include them or not. Dogs-that-are-taller-than-themselves are impossible. Our hypothetical God never gets to include those b/c they are incoherent.

Q: The ketchup case is a category-mistake

We'll probably need to refine our definition of 'Great-making Property' to exclude the possibility for category mistakes \-- which, I think, is what's going on in the cases you're pointing to.  
A **category mistake** occurs when we incoherently attribute a property to a being which can't have properties of that sort. A classic example:  
   
*I am giving you a guided tour of CU's campus.*   
*"Look, that's the library\!" I say. "And there's the Philosophy building and that over there is the campus gym."*  
*"But..." you ask, "where on campus is the school spirit? You haven't pointed it out yet."*  
   
We call that a **category mistake** b/c *CU's school spirit* isn't the sort thing that has a physical location. It's not the right *category* of being; it's confused to ask after the physical address of an abstract intangible like school spirit.  
So probably we'll want to reformulate our definition of 'great-making property' to make it clear that category mistakes don't count as counter-examples. Maybe: *a great-making-property is one such that, invariably, adding the property to a being which lacks involves either a category mistake or makes that being greater.*  
Then when some asks about kind ketchup, we will simply say: That's a category mistake\! Ketchup isn't the sort of thing that can be kind or mean, so we don't learn anything about the great-making nature of *kindness* from talking about kind ketchup. (Likewise if someone asks about *strong* ketchup or *happy* ketchup.)

Q: On the wisdom example – is it a great-making property?

It's not *super* clear to me what precisely **wisdom** is supposed to be, but it's probably in the neighborhood of *good judgment.* But then... what does a serial killer with good judgement look like? How does that even work?

Here's my first, knee-jerk response.

We might notice something curious about the English adjective '**good**'. Suppose I tell you that my friend Dave is *a good chess player and a good liar but not a good person.* Obvs, you have no trouble understanding what I mean by that: I mean that Dave *is talented at chess* (does the chess-thing well) and *is talented at lying* (does the lying-thing well) and *is morally wicked* (does the being-a-decent-person-thing badly.)

Compare: Suppose I tell you that Dave is *a very tall chess player and a very short liar.* **This time** you probably don't understand what I mean. If Dave is a very tall chess player, he must also be a tall liar. That's just how tallness work. People can't be tall-at-chess but short-at-lying. A person is either tall or not. 

I suspect that our confusion about the serial killer with good judgment really just stems from our uncertainty about what it means to have **good judgment.** What does it mean to be talented at making judgments? What does it mean to do the judgement-thing well? Presumably, a person who makes excellent judgments is a person who makes **correct** judgments. But... what kinds of judgments does the *wise* person excel at? Moral judgments? Self-interested judgments? Legal judgments? *All judgments*? Is a wise person a person who makes correct judgments about everything?

If so, our serial killer can't be wise\! Since he's **killing people,** he is clearly not making good moral judgments. (He apparently judges that killing people for fun is okay thing to do. Which is incorrect\!) So if *wisdom* entails *good at all kinds of judgment,* there can be no such thing as a wise serial killer. As soon as we imagine adding *wisdom* to our serial killer, he ceases to be a killer.

On the other hand, if wisdom entails excellence at some other sort of judgment, then maybe a serial killer *can* be wise and continue to kill*.*

 

Any thoughts about that? Does that help at all?

Q: ‘Better’ in what sense?

For *existence* to be a great-making quality, it really must be true that every single case we can think of where we add *existence* to a being which lacks it results in a *better* (or *greater*) being. And the cases you're pointing to suggest maybe that's not true.

Your objection leads me to wonder,

*How exactly should we be understanding betterness in this context?* Like, better in what sense? Morally better? More impressive? More praiseworthy? Better at survival/fitness (i.e. evolutionarily better)?

Those are genuinely different metrics\! Adding a property to a being might make it better in one of those senses and not in another. (e.g. a being that's *extremely altruistic* might be morally better but evolutionarily worse.) It seems like we need to be able to pin down what exactly 'better' is supposed to mean before we can properly assess which properties count as *great-making*.

The fact that Anselm ultimately wants to say that *God has all the great-making properties* does help us somewhat, I think. We can rule-out, e.g., the *evolutionarily better* interpretation since Anselm surely isn't trying to say that God is the being with maximal fitness. But I'm less certain about the other options. If a great-making property is one that makes beings morally better, then Mecha-Hitler cases (super-evil entities who exist only in our imaginations) would seem to show that existence is not a great-making property. On the other hand, if a great-making property is one that makes a being more impressive, it's less clear that Mecha Hitler cases count as counter-examples. (Though, now we probably need to try to be more specific about what 'impressive' means. \*sigh\*)

Any thoughts about that \- about what the most plausible/charitable way to understand betterness is in our definition of 'great-making property'? (You or anyone else\!)

P2) If the universe is a simulation, then the universe had a beginning. (That's just how simulations work\! First, someone decides to run a simulation. Then they start simulating.)  
P3) If the universe is not a simulation, then the universe had a beginning. (That's what our best physics tells us, anyway. And if we're NOT in a simulation, we should trust our best physics.)  
C) So, either way, the universe had a beginning.
