Q: On saying “God exists for you but not for me”

Interesting questions\! I'll take my best shot at explaining what I \*think\* Craig takes himself to be doing. (and how other philosophers interpret what Craig is doing\!)

Mostly, when philosophers say that something "exists", they mean that it's **real** or **actual** or **a part of reality.** Indeed, one of the sub-areas of philosophy, Metaphysics, just is the study of what exists. That is to say, Metaphysics is all about figuring out which things *exist* or *are real*.

As an analogy, imagine a small basket sitting in my closet and full of my stuff. We could make a list of all the things in the basket (maybe, e.g, a hairbrush, a couple of pennies, a baseball, a dead cricket, a lockbox, and 3 textbooks.) Maybe we work really hard to make sure our list is perfectly accurate and complete: everything in the basket is on the list and there is nothing on the list that isn't in the basket. Now let's suppose that the lockbox in the basket is sealed shut and you and I disagree about what's inside. You think it's a bunch of Pokemon cards while *I* think it's a bunch of old medical documents. Which is to say: we disagree about what belongs on the List of Things in the Basket. *You* think the list should include Pokemon cards; *I* think it should include Old Documents.

In this case, I think, it clearly doesn't make much sense for us to say something like, "Maybe we each get our own list? Maybe what's in the basket differs from person to person?" That just sounds confused. After all, the *whole point of the list* is **be accurate,** to **correctly describe the contents of the basket.** It's true that we *don't know* what actually belongs on the list (Pokemon cards or old documents), but there's still a fact of the matter. Either there are Pokemon cards in there or there aren't. If so, 'Pokemon cards' belongs on the list. If not, they don't. It doesn't really make sense to say that the Pokemon cards "exist-for-you-in-your-basket" but "don't-exist-for-me-in-my-basket." **There's only one basket\!** We are looking at the same, single basket, and we are disagreeing about what's inside.

 

Now imagine a similar list for the universe, a list of all the things that are in the universe: I'm on the list because I'm in the universe. You're on the list because you are too. Grizzly bears, coffee cups, anchors, and icebergs are all on the list. On the other hand, unicorns, time machines, earth's 2nd moon, and Sherlock Holmes are all **not** on the list. Those things aren't actually in the universe.

When Craig says "God exists", what he means is, *God is on the list of things in the universe.* And when an atheist says, "There is no God," what she means is, *God is **not** on the list of things in the universe.* And as in the basket case, the background assumption is **there is only one universe.** Craig and the atheist are looking at the same, single universe, and they are disagreeing about whether God is a part of it.

Thus, Craig takes himself to be giving us reasons for believing that God really does belong on the List of All Things in the Universe. (Translating the argument into our list metaphor, it says something like: "Every list needs a list-writer\! Thus, the List of All Things must include the list-writer Herself\!") 

As for people who refuse to believe in the existence of things that others take to be obvious (e.g. a man who insists that "Whales aren't real, bro\!"), I think it makes more sense to say that those people are *ignoring* the proof (or, better, ignoring the *evidence*) rather than to say that *there is no proof.* 

(In general, I think that talk about "proof" is kinda confusing for complicated linguistic reasons. I think it's clearer to talk about "evidence." We can say, e.g., that there is *decisive evidence* that whales are real. And yet, there could still be people who ignore that evidence \-- people to whom the existence of whales is "unproven." Those people are just wrong about the existence of whales, though. We shouldn't think that their wrongheaded skepticism somehow undermines the evidence for whales\!)

Does that help at all? Was that even an answer to anything that you were asking?

Q: Is nothingness not itself a something?

OBJ: *Doesn't "nothing" count as a thing? If so, even something that comes to be "from nothing" has actually come from something\!*

There's actually a small sub-discipline in philosophy about this issue: *what is the metaphysical status of **absences?** Are absences **real things,** equal-status members of reality alongside you and me and giraffes?*

Like, suppose we created a list off all the things that are real and that actually exist. (the List of Real Things) You and I would be on that list. So would dogs, H20, neutrons, EM fields, etc. 

But what about absences? Like: do shadows belong on the LRT? holes? silences? darkness? pure void? (maybe some of those things but not all? If so, which things and why?) When I talk about a "hole" is there a real entity that my word refers to, or is the word just a convenient way of talking about a place where there is a lack of entities?

 

It's a controversy\! I'm not well-versed in that area of philosophy, but my impression is that there are smart people with good reasons on both sides of the issue.

Your point, I take it, is that **IF** we think that absences are real things, then Craig's principle is vacuous. If nothingness *is* something, is a real entity, then a thing that pops into existence from the void *doesn't* violate Craig's principle. ("the tiger DID come from something\! It came from this bit of void here \-- and, keep in mind, voids are *bona fide* parts of reality\!") And, thus, a universe that pops into existence from nothing and for no reason would be also be coming to be *from something,* and would not violate Craig's principle.

I suspect Craig is not going to be moved by this objection b/c, I'd hazard, he would not think that absences are real; he would not include them on the LRT next to dogs and quarks. 

But for anyone who does think that absences are as-real-as you or me, this objection may have some teeth\!

Thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: What about giving a material interpretation to “nothing comes from nothing”?

think there's some ambiguity in Craig's principle that *something can never come to be from nothing.* He might be thinking that's true in the sense that everything comes to be *for a reason* **OR** he might be thinking that that's true in the sense that everything comes to be *out of pre-existing material.* (Or, Craig might be thinking that both are true?)

If I'm understanding you correctly, it sounds like you want to press on that second interpretation?

The material interpretation of the claim *does* seem very plausible\! 

A wooden statue can never come from **brand new** matter; it must come from some pre-existing stuff \-- viz. the matter of a tree. And of course the tree's matter comes from pre-existing matter too, all the matter that a tree takes-in to run its metabolism. (e.g. water, CO2, nitrogen from the soil.) And *those* things used to be other things, which used to be yet other things. And so on, back through time. The same is true of every other material object: when it comes into existence, all of its matter / energy is derived from other things that existed before.

You seem to be asking: *What about the universe? Where did all the matter in the universe come from?*

That's a good question\!

I think Craig is going have to say either **(a)** from God. Or **(b)** from nothing.

On (a), the view is that God literally took a part of Herself and used it to generate all the matter of the universe (and since matter/energy are convertible, all the energy of the universe too.) On (b), the view is that God has the power to create matter/energy from nothing at all. That is to say, on (b), Craig is denying the principle that *all material things come from other material things.* God, at least, is able to violate that principle and create matter *ex nihilo.*

Now, you might think both of those options are going to run Craig into trouble.

Suppose Craig answers your question about the material source of the universe by saying (a). "All of the matter/energy that was used to create our universe pre-existed the universe. That matter/energy used to be part of God."

If that's the story, then it seems we should ask the same question about God. *Where did God's material come from?* By claiming that the universe's matter used to be God's matter, Craig would seem to be endorsing the principle that *all material things come from other material things \--* not even the universe is an exception\! But then... whence God's matter? Has it just... always been? If so, are to we to assume that God has diminished Herself by making the universe from Her material substance? If not \-- if God is just as large post-Universe-Creation as before but also God really did remove some of Herself to make the universe \-- then that entails that God is actually infinite in material scope. (Only with a thing of infinite size can you substract a large amount and end up with the thing being the same size as before.) But, we're told by Craig, actually infinite things are impossible. This is troubling. So either Craig accepts that God is made of matter/energy **and** God diminished Herself when She made the universe OR Craig goes with (b).

But (b) (i.e. the creation of matter *ex nihilo*) seems to create other problems\! Craig is a big fan of treating natural regularities as exception-less rules, and here's a natural regularity that we've only ever seen obeyed and never violated: *matter/energy is always conserved.* Since that's always been true in the past, then by Craig's reasoning, that's surely true of God and the universe, too.  So... (b) doesn't work either. The universe must also have been created from conserved matter/energy.

Thoughts about any of that? (you or anyone else\!)

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
Q: Hume teaches us that there is no causation. Checkmate Craig.

Right, so, Hume was famously skeptical of causation. His view was that we can't know whether our commonsense notion of \***causation**\*, as a relationship that obtains between events whenever one event precedes, explains, mechanistically gives rise to the other, corresponds to any real relationship that actually exists in the universe. (i.e. he was skeptical about whether causation is a real thing.) And he was skeptical for the reasons you've outlined: our *evidence* that causation is real is just our observations that certain events consistently happen before/after other certain events. From those correlations, we've *inferred* that causation is a real thing. But... we could be wrong. All our evidence is equally compatible with causation **not** being real. It could be, instead, that the universe is no more than one thing happening after another.

Your objection, I take it:  
If we can't *know* that causation is real, then *a fortiori* we can't know that everything that begins to exist has a cause. So we can't know whether P1 is true. (Notice: that doesn't mean P1 is *false*\! Rather, the Humean about causation would withhold judgment about the truth of P1 \-- and, thus, about the success of the C.A.)

As it happens, [not that many philosophers are Humeans about causation](https://survey2020.philpeople.org/survey/results/4998)   
[Links to an external site.](https://survey2020.philpeople.org/survey/results/4998)  
, so this line of reasoning may not end up convincing very many people. But it's an interesting objection\! 

I do wonder:  
Is there a way to re-write P1 of the Cosmological Argument that would make the same point but in a way that's agreeable to Humeans? I'm not 100% sure what that would look like, but my instinct is that it's possible.  
Maybe,   
**P1:** Every *coming to be* event is preceded by some other event of a particular type. (the *new-thing-preceding* type.)

Since the universe came to be (P2), Craig could then infer that there was some other event that happened before the universe came to be, and that that event was of the same sort that we see constantly conjoined to other coming-to-be events. Since there was nothing else before the universe except entities that transcend beyond the universe, that earlier event must have involved one of those transcendent beings (viz. God.)

Thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

#

Q: On whether the universe could still support life with diff constants

That's really interesting\! Could a universe still support some form of life, with different physical laws and constants?

Our best physics suggests, No.

That's not merely because such a universe couldn't support human life. The universes with different laws are mostly places where stable matter cannot exist. The physicists are thinking, I imagine, that if there aren't any atoms at all, then surely there's no conscious life. Which \-- seems like a pretty plausible inference, I think.

Obviously, I'm not a physicist\! (I have a Physics minor, but that's not nearly enough.) I'm not sufficiently informed about cutting-edge cosmology to argue with them on these points. They might be wrong about fine-tuning, but I'm not equipped to say so.

That said, there is one relevant possibility that your questions suggest.

Imagine one of those universes that doesn't even support stable matter; it's just a soup of high-energy electrons and neutrinos and stuff. Is it possible that in some of those electrons-only universes, that some of the electrons themselves are conscious and have minds?

Maybe?\! I don't know. But, if so, that would seem like an important objection to the Fine-tuning Argument.

Q: On whether the universe is badly designed, though life-supporting

I think Collins is going to want to carefully separate the following conclusions:

(a) The universe has a creator

(b) The universe has a creator and he/she did a good job.

The Fine-tuning argument supports (a) but it does not support (b) \- nor does it claim to support (b). Which is to say, the Fine-tuning Argument can be true AND it also be true that the universe is a botched creation. The Fine-tuning Argument simply doesn't give us much information about what God is like. (Which is not, I don't think, a reason to reject the argument\! It's a reason to think the argument is *modest* in what it's trying to show. The argument, if it succeeds, doesn't tell us all that we might want to know about God.)

That said, next week we'll look one very powerful argument against (a particular kind of) God that can be found in precisely the sorts of observations you're making about the deficiencies of our universe. Hence, your concerns are very much apropos to our investigation into the existence of God.

Q: On what we can infer about God

You might be right that we can infer more things about God than I suggested in lecture. (about, e.g., Her power and knowledge) To create the universe, She certainly needed \*lots\* of power and \*lots\* of knowledge. That much seems exactly right.

However, I'm not sure it follows that she needed \*all of\* the power and knowledge. Suppose, e.g., that at the dawn of time, God didn't have the power to play guitar and didn't know how many jelly beans can fit into a soup can. Could She still create the universe, despite those limitations? Seemingly so\! Thus, from the fact that She \*did\* create the universe, if that is a fact, it doesn't follow that God must have known and had the power to do \*everything\*. 

I wonder whether we can infer anything about God's character *merely from* Her decision to create the universe \- e.g. Is She kind? Does She care about us and our lives? Do you have any thoughts about that?

Q: A re-explanation of the argument

You might think of the Fine-tuning Argument like this:

*Physicists report that the laws of universe are set **just right** for our universe to be a place where stable atoms exist. That's weird, huh? Why is that?*

*It seems like there are two possible explanations: (1) the laws are that way by accident, (2) the laws are that way on purpose.*

*It could be by accident, I guess. But WOW, what an unlikely accident\! That'd be like accidentally spilling a jar of sprinkles in your kitchen and then finding that they've randomly fallen into a shape that spells out "Don't be so clumsy" on the floor. Not **impossible** but... really really unlikely.*

*So probably the laws are that way because someone made them on purpose.*

Q: On the multiverse hypothesis – and god of the gaps objections

I think you're right to point out that the multiverse is a live option in physics. (Not the only live option, but one of them for sure\!) In that sense, the success of the fine-tuning argument may ultimately be hostage to our best physics. If future physicists determine that we almost certainly do live in a multiverse, then the fine-tuning argument will thereby be much less persuasive. If they determine that we don't, it will look more persuasive.

Something similar may be said of the points you're making about how the individual universes are generated in the multiverse. Presumably, that's a question that physicists may ultimately be able to answer, and if so, then again the success of the argument may be hostage to future physics developments.

Here's a natural follow-up question:

Does that mean that the Fine-tuning Argument really is a "god of the gaps" argument? (After all, what's characteristic of those arguments is that they are hostage to future scientists not being able to explain particular natural phenomena)

Thoughts about that? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: On a “maybe the universe is a designed computer simulation” reply

One other option that didn't come up in lecture (but that you may have encountered elsewhere) is that our universe is actually part of a computer simulation. For all we know it could be that there's some race of super-intelligent humans with super-powerful computers who decided to simulate an entire universe to further their own science, and, again for all we know, our universe is that simulated universe. You and I and everyone we know exist as subroutines or whatever in that giant simulation.  
If that's true, then, of course, the fine-tuning argument is not evidence for God's existence. Rather, if we are in a simulation, the fine-tuning of the universe is evidence for the simulation. ("Of course the universe is carefully designed to support life\! The aliens weren't interested in running a simulation on an empty, lifeless universe\!")

If that's right, then I think this is a very peculiar objection to the Fine-tuning Argument.

On the one hand, that story basically affirms the Fine-tuning claim. Our universe very much was designed to support life\! That fact is evidence for a (or a bunch of) designers. So the argument succeeds.  
But, of course, the 'Designer God' we find on such a story doesn't look very God-like at all. It's just a bunch of filthy humans (or aliens or whatever)\! How disappointing for the theist.

I imagine that the theist might want to argue against this possibility as mere speculation. But, still, we may ultimately reach a point where we have the capacity to simulate a universe like our own. If so, the simulation theory would start to look pretty good\!

Here's the question, I think:

If future scientific progress could potentially support this simulation theory as feasible, does that mean that the Fine-tuning Argument really is a "god of the gaps" argument?  
(After all, what's characteristic of those arguments is that they are hostage to future scientists not being able to explain particular natural phenomena \-- but maybe future scientists can explain how simulated universes are designed\!)

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about this alternative reply to the Fine Tuning Argument?

Q: Stating the pocketwatch analogy in terms of entropy / microstates

I wonder if Collins might not respond by offering an alternative account of how we spot design. In particular, I wonder if he might not appeal to statistical mechanics-ish reasoning. Something like,

Suppose you come across a deck of playing cards arranged into a 4-story house. Nearby, is a table and an empty card box, giving the impression that the cards may of simply fallen off the table. But, of course, that's almost certainly not the story. Why not? You reason, There are lots and lots of different ways for the cards to be arranged after falling off the table. Call each one of those arrangements a 'microstate'. And there are, basically, two outcomes for the cards after they fall off the table: either they form a flattened pile of cards, or they form an upright structure of some sort. Call each of those outcomes a 'macrostate'.  One observes an important difference between the two macrostates: There are only a few tens of thousands of microstates that form an upright structure (i.e. only a few tens of thousands of ways to arrange the cards such that a stable structure results), and yet there are a billions of microstates that form a flattened pile. On the assumption that each microstate is roughly equally likely to occur, following the cards tumble off the table, we must conclude that the upright strucutre macrostate is overwhelmingly unlikely to occur as a result of the cards tumbling off the table. Thus, the fact that the cards are right now in that macrostate is strong evidence that the cards were arranged that way by someone \-- as opposed to simply falling into that shape at random.

Surely humans are capable of engaging in this same sort of reasoning about all sorts of things they might encounter in the world. And, indeed, it's plausible that something like this is often used to spot design \-- to distinguish designed entities from randomly formed entities. We can infer that a building must have had a builder because given some concrete and rebar subject to random forces, the macrostate pile of rubble is realized by billions upon billions of microstates while the macrostate upright building is realized by a relative few. Therefore, etc.

 

That's my charitable attempt to respond on Collins' behalf anyway. (Given his deference to our best science, I thought him especially likely to be attracted to statistical mechanics-y and entropy-style reasoning about design.)

Any thoughts about that? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: ‘Surely w/ the universe so big, life is unsurprising’ is confused

Interesting\! I think you're right that given the size of our universe, and the incredible number of galaxies and planets, it's not surprising that there's a planet (viz. ours\!) that supports life. In fact, it kinda seems like there oughta be **lots** of life-supporting planets. But we should want to carefully distinguish the following two facts:

(i) Our universe has **life potential** (i.e. is the sort of place where life could happen b/c it has, e.g., stable matter)

(ii) Our life-supporting universe has **actual life in it** (as opposed to having life potential and kajillions of planets but no life.)

As best I can tell, Collins is only arguing that the first fact is surprising, not the second. The first fact, he thinks, provides strong evidence for the existence of God b/c surely the universe wouldn't have any life potential, if all the physical laws and constants had been fixed at random. 

It seems like you're pointing out that the second fact is **un**surprising? (Am I reading you right?) If so, I think Collins would simply agree with you. Given that we *do* live in a universe with life potential and given that our universe has kajillions of planets, it does indeed seem unsurprising that at least one of them actually developed life. (For exactly all the reason you're pointing to\!) That fact does not need any explanation and certainly isn't evidence for God.

 

That said, you could imagine a different version of the Fine-tuning Argument being deployed in a universe much smaller than ours. Like, imagine that we'd discovered our universe is just our solar system and maybe 2 or 3 others. In *that* universe, it really would seem surprising that our planet has life on it\! If there were only 30 planets in a universe with life-potential, it would be unlikely that any of them would actually have life. And so, the people in that universe would surely be rational for taking the existence of their own life-supporting planet as strong evidence for the existence of God.

That's obviously not *our* situation, though, b/c (as you point out) there are **so many planets** in our universe\!

Q: On minds in a universe w/o stable matter

Good, yeah \- that's really interesting\! Could a universe still support some form of life, with different physical laws and constants? Maybe just really different life?

Collins argues that No, it couldn't \-- and I think his reasons are rather plausible\! (Though that's not to say, correct.) His point isn't merely that a universe w/ different laws & constants couldn't support human life. It's that universes with different laws & constants are mostly places where stable matter cannot exist. Collins and the physicists are thinking, I imagine, if there aren't any atoms at all, then surely there's no conscious life.

Which \-- seems like a decent inference?

One thing that I've heard argued  is that there could be some psychophysical laws operating in our universe too, laws which govern the connection between matter and conscious life, and perhaps those laws could also have been different. The basic picture is something like this: (I'm adapting this from my recollections of a paper by the philosopher Neil Sinhababu)

In our universe, there seem to be some lawlike regularities concerning the type of material structures that support consciousness. Like, rocks and brains are both made of matter but only the latter seems to support mental processes; only the latter as the right sort of complexity and form to give rise to thoughts. Why is that? Well, we don't know. Indeed, the exact nature of consciousness is itself a matter of ongoing dispute \-- as is the nature of the connection between consciousness and the matter that gives rise to it.

But maybe the matter/mind connection is governed by physical laws\! Maybe those laws are not unlike the ones governing the strength of the electromagnetic force or the neutron/proton mass ratio.

So imagine one of those universes where there is no stable atomic matter. Maybe it's just leptons and quarks zooming around, never coalescing into anything larger. If the psychophysical laws of that universe were different from ours, might it not be the case that the electrons are themselves capable of conscious thought? In our universe that's not possible, of course, but this universe is clearly a very different sort of place.

If thinking electrons are possible, then that would mean that there's apparently more than one way for the laws of universe to support (mental) life. And then the fact that our universe supports life should seem less strange, is more likely to have happened merely by chance.  
 

That's one way I've seen your line of thought fleshed out, anyway. Any thoughts/comments/responses to that? (you or anyone else\!)

#

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
Q: On torture theodicies

You may be right that some torture counts as necessary suffering\! And perhaps for the very reasons you've given.  
But that's not enough (for the theist.) We should distinguish these two claims:  
(1) In every single case in the history of the universe in which someone was tortured, the torture was necessary to prevent a greater evil (or provide a greater good)  
(2) In some cases in which someone was tortured, the torture was necessary to prevent a greater evil (or provide a greater good)  
You've given us an argument that (2) is true. But the theist needs to argue that (1) is true. (Which will be pretty hard\!)  
We might note, for instance, that serial killers, tyrants, and terrorists have all been known to commit acts of torture. The theist, who believes in the 3-O God, must argue that each and every one of those acts was a necessary evil. The theists must argue that if God had stopped any of those acts of torture, She would have made our world a worse place.

Q: On defining omnipotence and omnibenevolence

We define 'omnipotence' as 'the capacity to bring about any logically possible state of affairs' in order to preclude certain (stupid) objections to the 3-O God.

Suppose we simply defined 'omnipotence' as 'the capacity to do anything'. Then someone could argue,  
The 3-O God does not exist because it's not in God's power to make a rock so big She can't lift it. Thus, God is not Omnipotent.

It's probably correct that God can't do that (i.e. make such a rock) but that's only because the action itself is contradictory. Can God make logical contradictions true? Probably not. Thus, we restrict our definition of 'omnipotence' accordingly. What God can do is everything else \-- all the non-contradictory stuff. 

As for maximal moral perfection... I don't know that I have anything very helpful to say. God has a perfectly good will? God has all the moral virtues? God always makes the morally best choice in every situation? Probably all those are true of an omnibenevolent being.

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
Q: On the God’s plan theodicy

I wonder what you (or others\!) think of the following argument, in reply:

Consider this partition on logical space \-- i.e. consider this exhaustive list of all the ways the world could be:  
Option 1a: The world does not contain unnecessary evil. And God has given humans the intellect they need to see that this is true. (i.e. a smart person thinking carefully can tell that there's no unnecessary evil, can tell that the Problem of Evil argument fails.)  
Option 1b: The world does not contain unnecessary evil. And God has not given humans the intellect they need to see that this is true (i.e. a smart person thinking carefully cannot tell that all evil is necessary. Some evil will always appear to be unnecessary no matter how wise and smart you are.)  
Option 2: The world contains unnecessary evil.  
If I understand your argument correctly, you are arguing that Option 1b is true.  
If so, the natural next question is this:  
Isn't our inability to understand the necessity of evil itself an instance of unnecessary evil?  
(e.g. many wise and smart theists are disturbed and upset by the Problem of Evil \- and their inability to respond to it. Many wise and smart atheists fail to find God because they find the Problem of Evil argument so persuasive.)  
Surely God in Her Omnipotence could have given us intellects sufficient for understanding the necessity of evil; surely in her Omniscience She anticipated the problem; surely in Her Omnibenevolence, she wanted to fix it.  
So... how do we explain the necessity of this source of suffering?

And, of course, it won't do to just say "Sadly, we're too stupid to understand why she did that"\! That's circular \- that's the very thing at issue\!  
(WHY are we too stupid to understand? Why did God make that call?)

Q: On whether my in-class examples of necessary evils are actually nec.

This is a really important question\! (And one I papered-over in the lecture, in the interests of time.) The thought is: even if we can point to some greater evil that's prevented or some greater good that's secured, we can often still ask, But couldn't God have tinkered with the world a little to reduce the sheer badness of this feeling or reduce/eliminate the need for it? Sometimes, a theodicy seems only to push the question back a level and not truly answer it.

A lot of health examples seem to fit this same basic pattern:

We might say that exercise is necessary suffering because it's needed to promote good health. But... Couldn't exercise have promoted good health and also felt less-bad? There's nothing stopping God from tinkering with the feeling of exercise to make it less painful, more joyful. So... doesn't the fact that sitting feels better than running count as unnecessary suffering?

We might say that eating vegetables is necessary because it's needed to secure basic nutritional needs. But... couldn't God have made broccoli taste more like ice cream cake and less like ass? There's nothing stopping God from swapping the taste of ice cream cake with the taste of broccoli. So... doesn't broccoli's unpalatability count as an example of unnecessary suffering? (Or, if you love broccoli: mutatis mutandis with some bad-for-us food that tastes amazing and some good-for-us food that tastes terrible.)

We might say that brushing your teeth is necessary to prevent cavities. But... Couldn't God have given us healthier and more resilient teeth? There's nothing stopping Her from tinkering with our mouth chemistry to make us less prone to dental problems. So... doesn't the fragility of our mouth health count as unnecessary suffering?

Q: On what we might believe if we accept the problem of evil

Yeah, this is an important line of inquiry for both theists and atheists\!

Suppose we accept that the Problem of Evil argument is sound. We can still believe in God (under a different description\!) We have options:

An Omnipotent & Omniscient God who doesn't care about us (or is actively wicked)  
An Omnipotent & Omnibenevolent God who's on vacation or something, and so doesn't know what's going on here on Earth  
An Omniscient and Omnibenevolent God who wants to help but is powerless to.  
An Omniscient God who knows about our suffering but doesn't care to help / couldn't help anyway  
An Omnibenevolent God who would want to help, but doesn't know we need it and doesn't have the power to anyway.  
An Omnipotent God who doesn't know about us or care. (maybe, She created the universe and then peaced out)  
 

We might note: Hey, some of these options look like they're compatible with the Fine-tuning Argument\! (where God is powerful enough to create the universe)  
Which is an interesting result\! It looks like we can coherently accept both the Problem of Evil argument and the Fine-tuning Argument without contradiction.

Q: On why the 3-O God

Basically, the major Abrahamic Western religions all posit the existence of a 3-O being (they attribute more than just those 3 properties to God, of course. But they do seem to agree about those three.) Since \~75% of the U.S. belongs to one of those religions, the 3-O God is the most popular God 'round these parts. So while we could look at arguments for and against the existence of Zeus and the Greek pantheon or the existence of the Zoroastrian dualistic gods, most of my students wouldn't be very invested in those arguments because few of them would take those religions seriously, as live options worth debating.

Of course, any students who don't care about the 3-O God may find themselves left out in the cold, forced to engage with arguments over an entity they find uninteresting. (Which isn't great\!) But, even still: lots of the people you know probably believe in the 3-O God\! So that's some indirect relevance at least

Q: Isn’t Goodness subjective? Thus, isn’t the 3-O God kinda whatever you think?

We’ll talk about ethics much more explicitly in a few weeks – it's a deep topic, and there's a lot to be said\!  
For now, though, I think the everyday notion of moral goodness is perfectly serviceable for our purposes. We understand what is meant, at least in general terms, when someone says something like, "She's a really good person. She's compassionate, thoughtful, and kind, and she truly cares about doing what's right." The 3-O God is good in that sense \- just moreso. And any being who isn't good in that sense, isn't the 3-O God.  
To give a concrete example: consider Zeus. One doesn't need to know exactly what's required for moral perfection in order to determine that Zeus is not morally perfect. He is canonically a jealous and vengeful god. He is also a serial rapist. Whatever Omnibenevolence amounts to, that cannot be it.  
It's for this reason that I tried to stick to relatively uncontroversial examples of evil in lecture. We may all have differing views about goodness, but we can agree, I trust, that torture and genocide are not-good. And, thus, we can agree that a morally good God would dislike those things too and would, where possible, do what She can to prevent them. Contrast: it wouldn't have worked for me to put 'swearing' on the list of unnecessary evils. People do disagree about whether foul language is not-good, and, thus, people would disagree about whether a 3-O God would dislike foul language and whether She'd care about preventing it.  
\[As an aside....  
I suppose, there could be a few students who are value nihilists, students who believe that nothing is ever good or bad. Those students would disagree that genocide is a bad thing. But, then, those students also wouldn't believe in a 3-O God. Because Omnibenevolence is, on their view, a fictional property that nothing posess. Those students can simply treat the Problem of Evil as the other half of a proof-by-cases argument against a view they know is popular. "There is either value in the world or not. If there isn't value, there is no 3-O God (b/c no Omnibenevolence). If there is value, there is no 3-O God (b/c the Problem of Evil). So there is no 3-O God."\]

Q: On rejecting an O from our 3-O God

You're right that it's very easy for the theist to wriggle out of the Problem of Evil\! They can just reject one of the O's and call it a day.

But they don't want to\! Most theists are very committed to each of the O's and so their response to the Problem of Evil isn't the quick dodge you're suggesting\! Instead, they try to argue that literally never has there been unnecessary evil.

You can tell how committed theists are to the 3-O God just from the fact that that is their typical response\! That is such a huge hill to climb, when they could just simply turn to a 2-O God instead.

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
Q: On “pain is helpful” theodicies

Good\! I like this theodicy \- and I think you've done an admirable job making it seem plausible.

In general, I think the theist can get a lot of mileage out of this kind of story.   
Why is there so much pain?   
Well, pain gives us important information about about the health of our bodies. If we didn't experience bodily pain, we wouldn't know when / how / whether to protect ourselves from our environments and we'd suffer a lot of unnecessary damage as a result. (That damage maybe wouldn't hurt, in a world without pain, but it could potentially cut our lives short\!)

Q: Previewing the Soul-making Theodicy

Yeah, nice\!

This spin on the Free Will Theodicy is starting to sound a lot like the Soul-making Theodicy. (Which we'll talk about in the next module.) It's a pretty plausible theodicy, all things considered\! 

The basic thought is this: take any evil you like, it will be true that God could stop that evil. Of course She could. She's Omnipotent and Omniscient. But that would be like God doing our math homework for us. Which is to say: it would rather defeat the point\! God *could* do our math homework; God is very good at maths. But the point of math *homework* is for us to do it ourselves, for us to *learn math* by doing it. My math homework is a necessary evil *that I have to deal with myself.* It is necessary for *me to struggle with my homework myself* in order to achieve the greater good of learning math. No struggle; no learning.

Similarly: God could stop evils; she is very good at doing that. But the whole point of there being evil at all is for us to *deal with it ourselves.* We should be the ones to stop evils b/c we are the ones who desperately need to mature and grow wise and compassionate. We should be the ones to make our world a better place because we, and not God, need the practice. Thus, the greater good that God secures by allowing suffering to occur is the maturation of us humans. No struggle; no maturation. And, on this view, the end goal of all this suffering is for all humans to be brought, in time, into communion with the 3-O God. But only once they've had a chance to mature. (to *make our souls better*, hence the name: the Soul-making Theodicy)

One important thing to notice about this theodicy, in contrast to the Free Will Theodicy is this:

on the Soul-making Theodicy, suffering is justified because it **promotes personal growth,** which is a very good thing. That is, on the Soul-making Theodicy **suffering *causes* good things** to happen. On the Free Will Theodicy, in contrast, suffering is the *effect* of good things \-- or, put differently, **good things *cause* suffering.** (Free choice is good, but free choices cause suffering. Since the goodness of freedom outweighs the badness of suffering, it's better to preserve freedom than to prevent suffering.)

Accordingly, I think your suggestion is akin to the Soul-making Theodicy b/c you're telling the same sort of story. You are explaining how **suffering might cause good things.** (which is backwards from the story we get from the FWT\!)

We'll talk about the SMT a lot more next week, but since you're pushing in that direction right now, I thought it worthwhile to go ahead and bring up.

As we'll see, the SMT is a powerful theodicy for the theist, but it does seem to have some strange implications.

For instance:

(i) What about people who die at a very young age? They don't get a chance to mature. That seems unfair. So… do they finish maturing in heaven? Or are they reincarnated, maybe?

(ii) What about sociopaths – i.e. people who appear incapable of moral maturation? Why does the 3-O God create people like that?

(iii) What about people who die while still very immature? They failed at the single task set for them by God. Do they get another chance to finish maturing? If not, how is that fair? (After all, the 3-O God surely foresaw their failure before She even created them\!) If so, where do they finish? In heaven? Are they reincarnated on earth?

(iv) Is moral goodness more valuable when it’s achieved through struggle, rather than simply present in a being from the start? If so, doesn't that imply that God’s moral perfection (maximally valuable as it is) must also have been achieved through struggle? So… God used to suck? But then She got better? Is that plausible?

Thought about any of that? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: On natural disasters / climate change as necessary suffering

Nice \- yeah, that's an interesting theodicy\! It seems like there are a couple subtly different suggestions here, and I think it might be worthwhile to pull them apart.  
   
(1) Natural disasters might count as necessary suffering b/c they are helpful and informative symptoms of our ailing planet. Thus, a series of heat waves may be necessary, despite killing a few people, because it provides us with important information about the health of our planet.   
(2) Natural disasters may be a way for the earth to purge/clear an ongoing threat \-- viz. the threat of overpopulation. Thus, a series of heat waves may be necessary precisely because it kills and purges a few humans. And the fewer humans there are, the less damage we'll collectively do to the planet.  
   
I suspect the proponent of the Problem of Evil is likely to say something like this...  
RE: (1)  
It seems plausible that this information is important, but I wonder, Is this the least-evil way to deliver this information? Like, surely there are other things God could do to convince us that climate change is real and a problem that doesn't kill so many people? (e.g. think of all the other ways that climate scientists gather information about climate change\! Or maybe God could just tell us?) The question is not simply, Is this information good to have? But also, Is this the morally best way to deliver this information?  
(complicating this discussion is the fact that millions of people are resistant to climate change information. They've already been presented with lots of evidence, but they still don't believe that human-caused climate change is real and a threat. Maybe the existence of climate change denialism is itself a kind of unnecessary evil? Why hasn't God already convinced them if, as you suggest, convincing people about the ills of climate change is super important?)  
RE: (2)   
Granting that human overpopulation is a serious threat to our planet, we should still ask, Is killing off humans in natural disasters the morally best way to fix overpopulation? Couldn't God fix the problem with less suffering by causing fewer babies to be born? (By killing off sperm or by causing infertility or by lowering people's sex drive or by increasing the popularity of oral sex or by... etc.) Plus, since it's largely the global poor who will bear the brunt of climate change and do the majority of the suffering and dying, this strategy of using natural disasters to fix overpopulation seems like it presents a rather serious fairness concern. Like... even if it's morally perfect for God to kill a bunch of humans with floods, surely it's not morally perfect for Her to mostly kill off the poor?  
RE: (1) and (2)  
Couldn't God have made our planet more resilient? Like, couldn't she have created natural mechanisms that reduce the impacts of climate change? Or maybe She could have originally made the world with less coal and oil? Or maybe She could literally just reach down right now and suck a bunch of CO2 out of the air? Why doesn't She do that?  
   
Any thoughts about any of that? Responses on behalf of your theodicy? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: On the decision procedure for a 3-O God minimizing suffering

Good questions\! I'll take a shot at answering them, as best I can, on behalf of the atheist.  
Since the 3-O God is by definition Omniscient, She presumably has knowledge about the future. She knows, for instance, what will happen if she prevents a particular car crash, and she knows what will happen if she doesn't prevent that car crash. Presumably, the 3-O God will decide what to do using the following procedure:  
(1) She will notice a bad thing that's going to happen soon \-- like a car crash.  
(2) She will look into the future to assess all of the consequences of this car crash. That is, how much good will done by this crash? How much bad? What is the net-value of this event, from now until the end of time?  
(3) She will look into the future to assess all of the consequences of Her stopping the car crash. How much good will be done? How much bad? What is the net-value of stopping the crash, from now until the end of time?  
(4) She will select the option with the highest net-value. (If stopping the crash results in a better world, She'll do that. If allowing the crash results in a better world, She'll do that.)  
Something like this procedure has to be the one that the 3-O God will follow. Her Omniscience lets her figure out which events are necessary for maximizing the good in the world, her Omnipotence lets her bring about those maximally-good events, and her Benevolence means She always wants to bring about the best possible world.  
That's why you will sometimes hear the Problem of Evil stated this way,  
P1) If the 3-O God exists, then ours is the best of all possible worlds.   
P2) Our world is not the best of all possible world.  
C) The 3-O God does not exist.  
And the atheist will support P2 by pointing out all the ways that our world could be better. (e.g. it could contain fewer genocides, it might have not included Alzheimer's Disease, it could contain less child abuse and domestic violence, etc.)  
   
Does that help at all? Any thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: On Omniscience and foreknowledge

I take it that the core concern is this: Does God's Omniscience include knowledge of the future?   
Recall, we defined 'Omniscience' as having knowledge of all knowable facts, meaning God must have knowledge of the future if the future is knowable.  (If the future is un-knowable, then God presumably doesn't know the future, despite Her Omniscience.) As you point out, the whole story of Job from the Old Testament is really only interesting if God doesn't know the future. It's not cool and impressive for God to "test" Job if God knew all along that he'd pass. That defeats the point. A test is all about information-gathering\! I test you on course materials to find out what you've learned. If I had perfect knowledge of the future, I wouldn't need to give tests. Surely the same applies to God.  
   
One reason this question is so important is that it presents the theist with a dilemma,  
Option \#1: The 3-O God knows the future.  
This is troubling for the reasons you point out. AND, it also seems to create problems for our understanding of free will. If God has knowledge of the future, then She already knew before you were even born ever single thing that you would ever choose to do. For any particular decision you make (e.g. the decision to eat toast for breakfast rather than eggs), God has known since the dawn of time that you'd do that. But then... is there any meaningful sense in which your decisions are ever free? Like, you chose toast for breakfast, just as God has always known you would. Could you have chosen eggs? Were you free to choose eggs? Seemingly not. For, choosing eggs would surprise God, would break Her Omniscience. And surely it's not in your power to break God's Omniscience.  
Thus, if God knows the future, we should be concerned that human choices are never truly free.  
Option \#2:  The 3-O God does not know the future  
This is troubling b/c it makes the Problem of Evil way worse. Like, recall the God's Plan Theodicy. The theist says, Every bad thing that's ever happened has happened according to God's morally perfect plan. God lets natural disasters, wars, and genocides happen because each of those things is necessary for making our world the best-possible-place. That's not a very satisfying Theodicy, but that story does have to be true, if the 3-O God exists. If there's a 3-O God, then everything that happens is in accordance with her morally perfect will.  
Only, if God doesn't know the future, then that story is probably false. God's Perfect Plan only makes sense if God has knowledge of the future; you need to know the future in order to perfectly plan for it. Like, consider a genocide. God sees the genocide about to happen and decides not to stop it. Why? Well, not because She knows that the future goes best if the genocide happens. Because God doesn't know the future\! Likewise for many other instances of suffering. If God doesn't know the future, then God doesn't know which instances of suffering are in-the-long-run necessary and which are in-the-long-run unnecessary. Which is a big problem\!  
   
Someone who believes in the 3-O God seemingly must choose one or the other of these options, but it looks like both have very serious problems\!  
Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about which Option is more plausible / a better choice for the theist?

#

Q: On animal suffering

I've heard a number of different responses from students over the years about your animal suffering questions, though. Let me try to articulate some of them, and then we can think about which of them are the most plausible.

Let's stick with Rowe's fawn as our illustrative example of animal suffering. (There's a young deer and a forest fire. The deer is burned in the fire and gets trapped under a fallen tree. It dies of exposure, immobile in that spot, over the course of 3 days.) Can the theist explain the necessity of the deer's suffering? 

Here are some of the responses I've heard:

(1) Deer have perfectable souls. The fawn suffers for its own good.

(2a) The fawn doesn't suffer b/c humans are the only beings in the world that God created with *inner mental lives.* A deer, like every other non-human animal, is just a really fancy Roomba. (And while it might *look* like animals have inner mental lives and can suffer \-- and while our best scientists might even insist that they do and can \-- that appearance is misleading.)

(2b) The fawn doesn't suffer b/c God performs a miracle to shut down its brain and prevent it from feeling pain. God does this *precisely because* there is no greater good to be gained by allowing the deer to suffer for 3 days alone in the woods. (and God **always** performs this miracle on animals, where appropriate.)

(3) The fawn suffers *for us.* After all, we're talking about it right now\! The greater good that's achieved is that we can think about the fawn's sad fate and thereby further our understanding of God and of the natural world. 

 

Solution (1) has all the problems that you are pointing to.

Solution (3) isn't robust. Although it can explain the suffering of one single deer, the one lucky enough to be used in Rowe's example, it doesn't explain the trillions of other examples of animal suffering that have occurred in the history of our planet, unwitnessed by humans and never used as a learning-tool.

Solutions (2a) and (2b), on the other hand, both seem robust enough to explain animal suffering, in general, and don't raise the new problems that come along with giving animals perfectable souls. Only... they're both **really radical.** One tells us that animals are all mindless automata (\!\!\!) and the other tells us that God is constantly performing sneaky miracles on animals. (\!\!\!)

There are surely other solutions that might be offered here, of course\! But those 4 are the ones I recall hearing from students most often.

Any thoughts about those? (you or anyone else\!) Any of them stand out to you as most-plausible? Other suggestions for what the theist might say here?

Q: Maybe it’s IMPOSSIBLE for god to be more present in our lives?

Yeah, that's really interesting\! I haven't really entertained that possible response before. Let me work through some initial thoughts:

*Is it impossible for God to be more present in our lives a lot? Is that why the 3-O God seems hidden?*

The first thing I'd want to know is this: *What's the sense in which it's impossible for God to be present in our lives?* There seem to be at least a couple different things we might mean,

(i) Sometimes when we say something is impossible, we mean that it's **logically** impossible \-- that the thing is self-contradictory. (e.g. a round square, a married bachelor, a dog that's taller than itself.)

(ii) Othertimes when we say something is impossible, we mean that it's merely **nomologically** impossible \-- that the thing would violate a law of nature. (e.g. a spoon going faster than the speed of light, a whale that appears out of thin air for no reason, a closed system that decreases in entropy \-- all of those are nomologically impossible.)

To put the difference another way: a **logically** impossible thing is one that's so impossible it *can't even be imagined,* while a **nomologically** impossible thing can be imagined but *could only exist as a result of a miracle or literal magic.* 

 

If the claim is that God being present is only **nomologically** impossible, then that doesn't actually seem to help much. Surely, *God can violate the laws of nature.* Surely, *God can perform miracles*. If that's all that holding Her back, it's still very puzzling that She's not more present in our lives. ("I wanted to comfort you, dear theist, but I just couldn't bear to perform any more miracles.")

So probably we need to claim that God being more present is **logically** impossible. If *that's* true, then that does seem like a plausible reason why God is absent. God wants to be more present, but doing so would require creating a true contradiction, which not even God can do. (As I pointed out in my response above to Sanjeev and Alison, it's not entirely uncontroversial to say that God is restricted to doing only the logically possible. But it's at least plausible.)

But then the question is: is there any good reason to think that it's *literally contradictory* for God to be more present in the world?

It not obvious to me why that would be true. **Especially** if we grant that God is *sometimes* present. Like, it would be one thing to argue that *God is, by definition, absent from the world.* I can see how that might not be too hard to defend. But it's harder task to argue that it's *sometimes logically coherent for God to be present and other times logically incoherent for God to be present.* What a strange claim\! I'm not sure how one might argue for that?

That's my initial reaction to the suggestion, anyway. Any thoughts/comments/objections to any of that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: On Schellenberg’s calling God ‘infinitely resourceful’

I'm really glad you're raising that point from Schellenberg. It seems to me that there are at least two immediate issues worth raising about God's "infinite resourcefulness". The first is, *Why should we think that a 3-O God would be super-resourceful?* The second is, *How does God's resourcefulness impact our understanding of the Problems of Evil and of Divine Hiddenness?* 

 

(1) Would a 3-O God be super resourceful?

You might think that, strictly speaking, *Omniresourceful* is a 4th 'O' property that is not literally entailed by any of the other 3 divine attributes. I think that's probably right. It seems like we can imagine a God who (a) is morally perfect, (b) knows every knowable fact, and (c) has the power to do any do-able thing but who **isn't particularly clever.** That is, we can imagine a 3-O God who *isn't* Omniresourceful. 

But, I don't think that's a very plausible God. First, because hardly any theists believe in an un-clever 3-O God. Pretty much everyone I've met who accepts a 3-O God *also* accepts that that God is super resourceful. (And, in fact, most theodicies directly appeal to God's cleverness to make sense of the suffering in the world.) The second reason why the un-clever 3-O God isn't very plausible is that it's hard to imagine God *intentionally* restricting Her own cleverness. Like, if God is Omnipotent, then it's in her power to be super-resourceful. And if God is Omnibenevolent, then surely she'll *want* to be super-resourceful b/c that will help her help us. Therefore, etc. If God was ever un-clever, She surely fixed that lack a long time ago.

It seems that we can safely infer that the 3-O God is very, very resourceful and clever. 

 

(2) What impact does God's resourcefulness have on our understanding of the Problems of Evil/Hiddenness?

I think I essentially agree with you about this. Once we're on board with a Omniresourceful God, we'll need to keep in mind that whatever problems God runs into, She'll always be able to cook up lots of clever strategies and solutions. (and will know which one is best\!) If She needs to comfort a sad theist, She'll be able to think up thousands upon thousands of different strategies. (e.g. a fortune cookie message\! a significantly-shaped cloud\! a radio that plays an appropriate series of songs\! an email from a mysterious stranger\! etc. etc. etc. (*I'm not as clever as God, so my list is way worse than Hers\!*)) 

I think this fact is a double-edged sword for the theist.

On the one hand, it gives him a bit more leeway to make use of the "God's Plan" theodicy. ("I'm not 100% sure why this flood is necessary for preventing some greater suffering. Maybe for soul-making reasons? But *keep in mind,* God is super-clever\! So the long-term benefits may be really subtle and complicated\!")

On the other hand, it actually undermines some of the standard theodicy strategies and makes even the stock examples of necessary suffering  (nausea, exercise, dentists) look a bit dubious.

The thought is: even if we can point to some greater evil that's prevented or some greater good that's secured, we can often still ask, *couldn't an Omniresourceful God have tinkered with the world a little and found a better, smarter solution to this problem?*

A lot of health examples look like this:

We might say that exercise is necessary suffering because it's needed to promote good health. But... Couldn't exercise have promoted good health and also felt less-bad? There's nothing stopping a resourceful God from tinkering with the feeling of exercise to make it less painful, more joyful. That might require some pretty fancy brain-chemistry manipulation but I bet an Omniresourceful God is up to the challenge

We might say that eating vegetables is necessary because it's needed to secure basic nutritional needs. But... couldn't a clever God have made broccoli taste more like ice cream cake and less like ass? There's nothing stopping God from swapping the taste of ice cream cake with the taste of broccoli. (again, that sounds pretty biochemically convoluted but.. Come on\! This is the maximally clever God we're talking about\!)

Etc.

 

Those are my first-pass thoughts about God's "infinite resourcefulness" anyway. Any reaction/comments/objections to any of that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: Comparison between soul-making and other religions

Yeah, interesting \- I think your comparison between the soul-making theodicy and other religious traditions is helpful and important. 

The soul-making theodicy asks us to take a particular view about what salvation means. It says that (a) everyone is saved in the fullness of time and (b) the thing that we are saved from is our own immaturity, imperfection, and short-sightedness. That sounds quite a bit like the view of salvation we hear from other, Eastern religions\! That's interesting and, perhaps, a bit of evidence in favor of the soul-making theodicy.

It's worth pointing out: that picture of salvation is not the traditional one you get from Christianity, at least. The traditional Christian views has it that (a) only those who accept Christ as their savior are saved and (b) the thing they are saved from is the badness of life on earth and the torment of an eternal life in hell.

For Christians, then, the soul-making theodicy is actually pretty revisionist\! I probably didn't stress that enough in lecture \- it certainly bears emphasizing.

 One other thought:  
I really like your final suggestion. I think that's a smart reply for the proponent of the soul-making theodicy to make in response to fairness concerns. (Why is it that some people seem to mature more quickly than others? Why is it that some people seem to be born with a bad moral character while others appear to be naturally more virtuous? Isn't that unfair?)

Your suggestion, I take it, is that the theist can simply say,  
"Those mature people are actually on their 2nd or 3rd reincarnation cycle\! They've been alive longer \-- though in a different body \-- and that's why they appear to be so much more mature."

At least, if one is will to get on board with reincarnation, that reply seems pretty good to me\! Pretty satisfying.

Q: A re-explanation of why hell doesn’t work on the soul-making theodicy

(i) One problem with Hell, in particular, is that it is unfair by definition. Bundy did a lot of really awful stuff. But he didn't do *infinite* bad things. So he cannot deserve *infinite* punishment. Insofar as Hell is supposed to be an eternal resting place for the badly-behaved, the 3-O God would presumably not be willing to sentence the finitely wicked to infinite suffering. The 3-O God will, at most, want to punish the finitely wicked with finite suffering. How will She accomplish that? Certainly, one option is Hell \-- a **finite** duration in damnation. But it seems to me more parsimonious to just assume that God sentences those people to more time earth, via reincarnation. Earth sucks\! We suffer here\! So maybe Bundy gets what he deserves by spending an extra 500 years maturing and suffering on earth while the rest of us go straight to heaven.  
(ii) The other problem with people like Bundy and Stalin is more profound: *nothing forced God to make those people. God, in her complete knowledge of all things, surely foresaw all of Bundy's bad behavior before she even made him. She **could** have re-designed his brain a little to make him more compassionate and less murder-y. But She didn't. Conversely, She could have made a good person like Mister Rogers more murder-y and less compassionate. But She didn't. In that sense, there is a **fairness concern** here. How is it fair for God to make Bundy so naturally murder-y and making Mister Rogers so naturally compassionate? Especially if the end result of that decision is that Bundy has to spend 600 years on earth and Mister Rogers only has to spend 80?*

#

Q: Maybe the alien would choose the religion that maximizes the chance of salvation?

That's interesting \-- and probably right, from a game-theoretic perspective. 

Consider this toy example,

There are 3 world religions (A, B, and C) that offer salvation. But they differ in how widely they offer it. Religion A says that only A-believers are saved. Religion B says that both A and B believers are saved. And Religion C says that A and B and C believers are saved. All agree that non-believers go un-saved, and all 3 religions can freely be joined by anyone. Which religion should you join, if you want to maximize your chances of salvation?

 

If your only goal is to maximize your chances of salvation, the answer is clear: *you should join Religion A.* That's because everyone agrees that A-believers are saved\! (Whereas C-believers only get saved if Religion C is true. Bad odds; don't pick that one\!)

That's just a toy example, but the upshot is clear enough: *if your goal is maximize your chances of salvation, you should join whichever religion has the highest number of votes as a "legitimate path to salvation."*

 

That's kinda strange... what are we to make of that? 

Well, we might note, first, that that implication only arises for people whose only decision criteria is *trying to maximize their own chance of salvation.* That's probably no one \-- or next to one. At least, I've never met anyone w/ that sort of baldly pragmatic approach to religious belief. And, second, we might point out that that's not how beliefs work most of the time. Just because a certain belief would *benefit me* doesn't mean I can adopt that belief at will. If, e.g., there were some evidence that Flat Earthers tend to be much happier than Round Earthers, I doubt I would be able to simply *will myself* into earnestly believing that the earth is flat \-- even though that would be good for me. Similarly, then, even if I can see that Religion X is the one that's most likely to lead to salvation, I might not be able to actually adopt all the beliefs that are required for counting as an X-er. 

 

All of this is skirting pretty close to Pascal's Wager. He famously argued that we should believe in God for pragmatic reasons: if we believe, we either go to heaven or waste a bit of our time. But if we don't believe, then we either go to hell or save a bit of time. Given the stakes, then, it makes far more sense to believe. 

That argument is complicated by the existence of multiple religions, and for exactly the reasons you pointed out\! (The different religions have complex beliefs about which other religions get saved\!)

Thoughts about any of that? (You or anyone else\!)

Q: Why do so many religions agree AT ALL about the big picture stuff?

Interesting\!

We have this fact, *the* *world religions disagree about a bunch of stuff **and also** agree about a bunch of stuff. Both of those facts are philosophically interesting.* Zagzebski mostly focuses on the disagreements, of course.

But I share your sense that something is being missed if we don't say anything about all the religious agreement\!

We should ask, *Why do so many of the world's religions (many of which were formed independently of the others\!) agree about the basic religious facts \-- e.g. that we will be saved?*

Theists, obviously, want to say that it's because **God is real**.  (Which, yeah \- maybe\!)

 

But what I actually find more interesting (as you seem to\!) are the alternative explanations for those religious commonalities. Here are a couple different ones:

(1) Evolution. Early hominids who believed in a transcendental reality had a fitness advantage over those who don't. Thus, we evolved to be open to the sorts of ideas that most religions share.

(2) Social cohesion. In ancient civilizations, shared belief in a transcendental reality helped hold society together. Cultures w/o these believes quickly fell apart.

 

Both of those explanations seem to raise a bunch of further questions. Like, *what exactly is the fitness advantage enjoyed by religious humans?* And, *Why couldn't the social-cohesion benefit be achieved by other sorts of social arrangements? What's so special about religious beliefs in particular?* 

Here are a couple other explanations one might give for religious commonalities:

(3) Viral Ideas. There used to be lots of other religions that *didn't* believe in salvation (e.g. ancient Greek religion), but those religions didn't spread as well as the salvific religions. People are more likely to convert to a new religion when it has something rather than nothing to offer (i.e. salvation.) Nowaday, the un-viral religions are mostly gone, having been outcompeted in the marketplace of ideas.

(4) Intolerance and Conquest. Most of the current world religions emphasize the importance of spreading the religion to others. Historically, not all religions were like that. (again, think about the Greeks and Romans.) Through a long history of conquest and intolerance, the religions that emphasize growth have dominated and stamped out the passive, tolerant, non-salvific religions.

 

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any inclinations toward / against any of these explanations? Or other suggestions for how a non-theist might explain the commonalities among world religions?

Q: Are there any ideas that are so bad that we should be intolerant, despite Lewis’ arguments?

Good question\! *Are there any ideas/views so bad that it's ill-advised to tolerate them?*

 

Maybe\! A view that demands *violence* or *violent* *intolerance* from those who hold it might fit that description. Here's a case:

Like, imagine some extremist position according to which killing children is the morally best thing that can be done while nurturing children is maximally evil. (Indeed, suppose that the view says that killing children is also the *prudentially* best thing one can do, that child killers receive unimaginable rewards in heaven or whatever.)

People who hold that view would, of course, have very strong incentives to kill children. While we could create disincentives, could make the punishments for child killing really severe and dreadful, that could turn out not to be enough. It could be that there's no way to stop the child-killers from killing, except jailing or killing them. Indeed, it could be that in the long run, the only way to stop the child killers is to suppress the view altogether.

 

A view like that seems to me like a good candidate for one that's honestly better to not-tolerate. It seems plausible that we'd be justified as a society in taking rather drastic steps to stop that view from spreading\!

That's not a *real view* that *real people have* (thankfully\!), of course, but it still can serve a proof-of-concept\! Seemingly, there can be views that are so bad that it's better to not-tolerate them. And, it seems to me, what's going on in this case is that there's a **really big difference** between my values and the child-killers values. (I think children's lives are very very valuable; they think that children's lives are very, very disvaluable.) And that value gap is so big that I am inclined to refuse to live in the same society with those people. (that is to say, I'm not ok w/ a 'live-and-let-live' treaty of mutual toleration, w/ me left to do my thing and them left to do theirs.)

 

The obvious next question is: *are there any **actual** views like that \-- i.e. views so dangerous and prone to violence that the only way to protect people is to suppress it?*

I don't know\! Probably the best place to look for a view like that is among the world's various extremisms. (e.g. religious extremist views, political extremist views) That's where you'd expect to find people with value judgements **so radically different** from the average person's as to be unacceptable.

 

Any thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!) Did that even answer the question you meant to be asking?

Q: On most issues, isn’t one side (e.g. environmentalists) more motivated to stop the other? (e.g. climate skeptics)

There does seem to be such a gap. But I'm not sure if that creates a problem for Lewis?

If I'm understanding you correctly, the disagreement you have in mind is over *how great the stakes are* in the dispute between the atheist and the theist. The atheist thinks, *If i'm right, then it's not a big deal for the theist to continue believing in God.* Whereas the theist thinks, *If I'm right, then it's **utterly disastrous** for the atheist to continuing non-believing in God. (*Put differently, the theist thinks atheism is *very very bad* while the atheist thinks that theism is mildly bad.)

The upshot is that the theist is **more motivated** to win the argument b/c, by his lights, more is at stake. Thus, the theist finds intolerance much more attractive than does the atheist.

So... why would the atheist even go to war at all? And shouldn't the theist win? Isn't that a problem for Lewis?

I don't think so. Rather, I think Lewis will say that the atheist will go to war, not to win, but to *defend himself and society from violent radicals.* While the atheist doesn't much care about whether people believe in God, he *does* care about religious radicals whose belief and value systems tell them to stamp out disagreement at all cost. The atheist might say, "It's a very, very bad thing for there to be people in our society who earnestly believe that the morally best thing is to kill those who disagree with them. Yikes\! Can't let those people run rampant\!" 

I think similar remarks apply in other cases with gaps, with asymmetric motivations. (e.g. climate change skeptics and climate activists. Probably the activists think more is at stake than the skeptics.) Lewis can say: while the less-motivated party doesn't care much about changing people's minds, they **do** care about intolerance. If the more motivated party starts getting violent, the less-motivated party will have a reason to retaliate \-- viz. to stop the spread of violent extremism. 

 

Does that answer any of the questions you were asking / is that response from Lewis at all satisfying?
