Q: What counts as “sufficient”?

Yeah, good\!

I think that is THE big question for Clifford's principle. He says we may only believe on the basis of "sufficient" evidence, but we're not told what counts as sufficient\! What standards are we to use? How much evidence must we gather? Surely we aren't supposed to dedicate like, *literally our whole lives,* to the gathering of evidence just to avoid the immorality of having ungrounded beliefs\!

In some sense, that question *(What counts as* *sufficient*?\!) is way, way, way too big for Clifford to tackle in a short essay. One could write multiple books trying to answer that question. (People *have* written multiple books\!) But the point remains: we need to know what counts as sufficient evidence if we're to know what we're morally allowed to believe or know which people we can condemn as believing wrongly\! Should we be condemning children for believing in the tooth fairy? Should we be condemning our ancestors for believing in lightening gods?

 

This is, like, a classic hard problem in philosophy. We have an important idea where we can all agree about the extremes, the easy cases. (e.g. We all seem to agree that you *do* have sufficient evidence for believing in whales. We all seem to agree that the shipowner did **not** have sufficient evidence for believing in the soundness of his ship and that he is culpable for that.) And then there are a bunch of hard cases in the middle where we aren't sure what to think. (e.g. Are children required to meet the same standards? How are we supposed to tell which facts count as junk facts?)

In cases like these, usually philosophers will try to do some theorizing to generate some plausible account that gets all the easy cases right and has plausible, helpful things to say about the hard cases. I share your intuition that Clifford's principle is too strong, but there's also something right about it, it seems\! So... we need some weaker, more nuanced account of sufficient evidence.

We can already roughly see what that account must look like, I think. We probably will want to count testimony (i.e. the things that other people say or report) as sufficient in a lot of cases. Otherwise, it's immoral to have beliefs about history or about any science you aren't an expert. (Like, what evidence do you personally have that Genghis Khan existed? Only the say-so of teachers and the like. But... surely it's not immoral for you to believe in Genghis Khan. So probably *the say-so of other people* should count as sufficient evidence.) We'll also want to think about the quality / importance of sensory evidence. (*When should we trust our senses and believe thing on the basis of our sensory evidence? When **shouldn't** we trust our senses? etc.)*

 

So... yeah. It's a huge, complicated topic. You are in good company in being stumped here\!

Q: On what Clifford would say about the ethics of doing science

This is interesting\!

I wonder if we might make sense of scientific inquiry (while preserving Clifford's principle) by appealing to other sorts of belief-adjacent mental states. What attitude does the scientist working on quantum entanglement take toward the phenomenon? It seems like we have some options\! One thing (s)he might be doing is *suspending belief \--* i.e. not taking a stance one way or the other. That seems compatible with Clifford's principle. He doesn't say it's wrong to *have no belief* on a matter about which there's insufficient evidence. (Nor does he say, as best I can tell, that it's wrong to *have no belief* about a matter for which there actually *is* sufficient evidence.)  
Which fact, I think, helps us make sense of what the scientist is doing. We might describe the scientist as *entertaining* (but not endorsing) various propositions about quantum entanglement. To *entertain* a proposition is to take it seriously as a possibility, without firmly believing it to be true or false. Seemingly, a scientist who entertains a certain view, theory, or hypothesis can continue to do science about it, can continue to investigate the truth of that view\! And, again seemingly, a scientist can entertain a new hypothesis even when there is already a large body of evidence that the hypothesis is false.

So all of that's to say: I'm not sure that Clifford's principle must necessarily condemn scientists for doing science\! (It seems like scientists might be able to investigate a hypothesis without taking anything "on faith" in Clifford's sense.)  
Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that possible line of reply on Clifford's behalf?

Q: On whether Clifford thinks it’s wrong to believe anything

This is an interesting objection\!

If I'm understanding you correctly, the thought is basically:  
If Clifford's principle is correct, then it seems like it's positively wrong for us to have beliefs about most things \- since we mostly don't have sufficient evidence. But surely it's not wrong to have beliefs about most things. Thus, Clifford's principle cannot be correct.

Clifford really has two options in responding here:  
(i) He can argue that we do have sufficient evidence in a lot of cases \- at least, in far more cases than you seem to think.

(ii) He can accept the implication and acknowledge that it really is objectionable for us to have as many beliefs as we do.

I actually don't have a sense for which way he's likely to go. Maybe a bit of both?  
Maybe he could argue that we should, e.g., count reliable testimony as sufficient evidence most of the time. (For instance, when I tell you that Aristotle believed there were 4 basic elements, Clifford might say that that's enough evidence for you to believe that's true. Because I'm in a position to know that, and you're in a position to trust me.) That will help him justify a lot of beliefs.  
But, then, he might also accept that we really are doing something objectionable a lot of the time when we adopt beliefs \-- even in cases where we unreflectively wouldn't think we're behaving badly.  
   
Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Q: An attempt to re-explain that Clifford doesn’t care about outcomes – about whether any bad actually happens on account of your wrongful belief

I'm not sure that's quite what Clifford wants to say about the ethics of belief, but let me see if I can articulate his position.

Consider the shipowner case again. In that case, Clifford wants to say that the shipowner has done something morally very bad by sending his ship to sea. But what precisely did he do wrong? 

Clifford thinks that the shipowners primary mistake was choosing to believe that his ship was safe, despite the evidence to the contrary. And, in fact, Clifford takes pains to tell us that he thinks that that would still count as a serious moral mistake even if the shipowner's ship had made the voyage safely. In that case, Clifford writes, "The man would not have been innocent, he would only have been not found out."

 

In short, Clifford thinks that it's immoral to believe on the basis of insufficient evidence irrespective of whether the outcome of that decision is positive or negative. The outcome is irrelevant; the choice to belief is immoral either way.

Let me offer a different pair of cases to illustrate what Clifford has in mind here. So imagine X and Y have both been drinking at a bar and are very, very intoxicated. Both need to get home. Both will themselves to believe that they are sober enough to drive safely, despite all evidence to the contrary. On the way home, X almost hits and kills a small child but, luckily, swerves out the way at the last moment. On Y's way home, Y also encounters a small child but he is unlucky and doesn't swerve fast enough. The child is killed.

One of these men has literally killed a child while the other has not. So, you might think, one of them has behaved very badly and the other has not.   
But that's not what Clifford wants to say here. Clifford thinks both men are equally guilty; both made the same bad decision to believe themselves safe to drive. The only difference between them is that X got lucky and Y got unlucky. But getting lucky doesn't make one less guilty. It just makes one's guilt less obvious to others.

Does that makes sense?   
And, if it does, do you think that's plausible? Or are you inclined to think that Clifford is wrong about this and that outcomes do matter when it comes to assessing guilt for bad-beliefs?

Q: On whether early humans were immoral for having beliefs about the natural world (i.e. b/c they believed w/o all the evidence)

That's a really interesting point.

I'm not sure what Clifford might say about that. But **I personally** really feel the force of the concern.

Surely we don't want to say, "You and I have lots of justified beliefs about, say, the hard sciences. We can read peer-reviewed journals and talk to experts to learn about our world. But our early ancestors didn't have access to those kinds of rich information sources. Thus, apparently, **they were behaving immorally whenever they formed beliefs about the physical world they lived in** (about physics, chemistry, basic biology). Our ancestors were just very bad people, given all the irresponsible believing they did."

That seems confused. Our ancestors weren't monsters just because they didn't have access to universities and textbooks. They were doing they best they could\!

 

I also think your solution is probably moving in the right direction. Surely the difference between a 2000 BCE human who believes in magical fire ghosts and a 2000 CE human who believes in magical fire ghosts is that the modern human **should know better.** The modern human should realize that his understanding of fire is based on less-than-all of the important, available evidence. But, of course, the same can't be said for the 2000 BCE human. It's not the case that he should know better\!

So maybe the correct standard is one, like yours, which says, *It's wrong to believe X when one knows (or **ought to** know) that one's belief in X is unjustified, is founded in less-than-all of the available evidence or is derived from some serious error in judgment*.

Q: On a self-defeat objection to Clifford’s principle

That's interesting\! Let me see if I'm understanding you correctly,

Consider this proposition,

It's always, everywhere, for anyone wrong to believe anything on the basis of insufficient evidence.

Is it ok to believe that proposition?

Well, according to this principle, you may only believe this principle if you have sufficient evidence for it.

But we don't have sufficient evidence for the truth of this principle. (Why not? ... I'm actually not sure I was able to follow this part of your argument. Something about Clifford's intuitions about the shipowner case not being "purely objective"?)

Thus, the principle is self-defeating. If we accept Clifford's principle, the principle tells us to reject itself \-- because we don't have sufficient evidence for it.

That's a really interesting argument\!  
I guess I have a couple thoughts,

(i) That argument obviously hinges on our not having sufficient evidence for Clifford's principle. So maybe the implication for Clifford is that he needs to work a lot harder to show us that his principle is true; he needs to gather a lot more evidence for it.

(ii) I wonder whether a weaker version of Clifford's principle would run us into the same problem? Lots of people’s reaction to Clifford’s principle is that it’s too strong and that we need to weaken it to specify that only under certain circumstances is it immoral to believe on the basis of insufficient evidence. Maybe once we've spelled out what the circumstances look like it will turn out that the resulting weaker-principle is no longer self-undermining?

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Q: On whether ‘junk fact’ is ill-defined

we'll want to say something more precise about what we mean by 'junk facts'. More precisely, we want to know *is there any way of defining 'junk fact' such that the Junk Fact Objection is plausible*? (Which goes: P1) It's morally permissible to believe on insufficient evidence when your belief concerns a junk fact. P2) Facts about God's existence are junk facts. C) So it's not immoral to believe in God on the basis of insufficient evidence.)

Here are some options:

(i) Maybe a *junk fact* is a fact that most people don't care about.

(ii) Maybe a *junk fact* is a fact that won't ever influence anyone's behavior

(iii) Maybe a *junk fact* is a fact that seems not to have any bearing on your behavior (and, thus, a fact will *cease* being a junk fact if it becomes clear that it does bear on my behavior)

Clearly, (i) is no good. Most people don't care about when my birthday is. Thus, on this def, my birth date is a junk fact. But it would be immoral for *my spouse* to believe that such-and-such is my birthdate on insufficient evidence. Thus, on this def., P1 is false.)

(ii) won't work either, though for different reasons. Simply put, on this definition we can't tell which facts are junk facts \-- and, again, for the reasons you've given. Take any fact you like. We can construct a hypothetical case where my beliefs about that fact influence my behavior. (e.g. *I get kidnapped by a Saw-type villain. He says, "I have imprisoned 2 other people. If you believe X about chubby turtle speeds, open the left door. If you believe not-X open the right door. If you open the wrong door, I'll kill both people.*) 

(iii) strikes me as much more promising, but it does require us to modify the original objection. Now P1 says, "It's morally permissible to believe on insufficient evidence when your belief concerns a junk fact \-- but **as soon as** you realize that fact might influence your own behavior, it ceases to be junk fact and it's no longer the case that you're permitted to believe whatever you want about it."

That implies that it's permissible to believe in God (or to not believe in God) only if it's clear to you that your belief in God will have no influence on how you behave. 

Maybe that's true of some people\! Maybe some people really don't change how they behave based on whether they believe in God\!

But... I've never met anyone like that. Theists mostly *do* act differently based on their belief in God. (they, e.g., pray or go to church.) Likewise, atheists.

Thus \-- assuming all that reasoning I just did was sound \-- it seems like we can defend only a **very** limited kind of theism (or atheism): the *frivolous* kind. 

Any thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: Quick Def of evidence

"Evidence" is being used here to mean something like, "an observation that seems to count in favor of (or justify) a particular belief." Public evidence consists in observations that we can share (e.g. photos, videos, arguments.) Private evidence consists in observations that we can't share. (e.g. the feeling of my head aching \-- i can \*tell\* you about it, but i can't share the feeling.)

Why should we think there's such a thing as private evidence? The basic thought is: sometimes we really do experience things that genuinely seem to justify our having certain beliefs (e.g. an experience of seeing an angel descend from the heavens to speak to me, a headache), even though we cannot share those experiences with others. The fact that we cannot share those observations doesn't seem to render them *evidentially worthless*, in other words.

Q: Responding to the ‘how can 1 religious experience make it rational?’ objection

One thing we might say in defense of the theist is this:

What counts as reasonable when it comes to assessing one's religious experiences partly depends on your own personal priors \-- that is to say, **your existing degree of confidence that God exists.** A person with very low confidence will need more evidence than a person whose confidence starts somewhat higher.

Compare two people:

I'm, let's say, 50/50 on God existing. Then I have a religious experience.

You're, let's say, 1/99 on God existing. Then you have a religious experience. (i.e. you were 99% confident God doesn't exist before the experience.)

How should the two of us respond to our experience?

It seems very plausible to me to say that **we both should *entertain* the possibility that our religious experiences came from God**. Whether or not that is the most likely explanation, it does seem to legitimately be on the table. It would, I think, be an error in judgment for either of us to entirely dismiss that possibility.

But by taking that possibility seriously, we will both thereby need to increase our levels of confidence a little \-- maybe, a 20% bump from our prior:

So I'm now 60/40 that God exists

You're now 1.2/98.8 that God exists (i.e. you are 98.8% confident God doesn't exist.)

This is, basically, the Bayesian story about what the rational response to religious private evidence is.

And on this story, at least, you can see how someone who starts out on the fence about God might arrive at theism as a (seemingly reasonable\!) response to a few religious experiences. And, at the same time, we can see that for someone who starts out as a staunch atheist, it is equally reasonable **not** to become a theist on the basis of just a few religious experiences. They need a lot more than that. They need angels busting through windows left and right.

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Q: On the objection, ‘But people’s religious experiences point to incompatible views’

As you point out, different religious experiences seem to point to different religions as correct. **But** different religions disagree about lots of stuff. Either the universe is infinitely old or it isn't. Either we reincarnate when we die or we don't. Etc. So if it's rational for Person A to believe in religion X on the basis of her religious experiences **and** rational for Person B to believe in religion Y on the basis of his religious experiences, isn't that a problem? Only one of them can be right\!

There are two responses that I think Hick should give.

(1) We might point out: a lot of religious experiences don't actually have enough content to indicate **which** religion is the correct one. If I have a religious experience while looking at a mountain, that experience doesn't help me decide between Buddhism and Islam. So, Hick might say, in many cases your private evidence only supports a rational belief *that some religion or other must be true.* If you try to conclude more than that \-- if, e.g., you try to conclude that Jesus Christ is your lord and savior \-- you are doing something irrational. You are believing something on the basis of insufficient evidence.

(2) Here's a better response. (We'll talk about **peer disagreement** in few weeks, and all of this will make a bit more sense then, I think. But I'll try my best to say something quickly now.)

Sometimes it can be rational for me to believe X and rational for you to believe not-X, given the differences in the evidence we each have. (Which is different from saying, I may sometimes be **correct** in believing X, while you are simultaneously **correct** in believing not-X. That's contradictory nonsense. But that's not what Hick is saying. He's **not** saying that all religious experiences are accurate and veridical; he's saying they all count as evidence. Which leaves it open that some of that evidence is ultimately misleading.)

Here's a case:

Imagine a love triangle between A, B, and C.

A and B each believe they are in a long-term monogamous relationship with C, but C is playing them both. 

C tells A, *You are my one true love. I want to spend the rest of my life with you and you alone.* All of A's evidence supports the believe that he is C's one true love.

C tells B, *You are my one true love. I want to spend the rest of my life with you and you alone.* All of B's evidence supports the believe that he is C's one true love.

In this case, I think, it seems rational for A to believe, *C loves A most of all.* It also seems rational for B to believe, *C loves B most of all.* Of course, they can't both be right. (And they might both be wrong\!) But it is nevertheless rational for them to believe as they do, given the evidence they've seen. **So: two people can rationally disagree, even if it's clear that one (or both\!) of them are wrong.**

I think we should be able to say something similar about different people's incompatible religious experiences. We can say that each person counts as **rational** in his/her religious beliefs, given the evidence each has seen. And yet, obviously, most of them are wrong. 

Which leaves us with this question:

*If the 3-O God exists, why would She let that happen? Why would She allow people to rationally believe false things about her?*

Do you (or anyone else\!) have any thoughts about that?

Yeah, good points\! There's something quite appealing about Hick's solution. He tells us that it's good and rational to take our friends and loved ones seriously when they report having religious experiences. Q: On whether Hick’s argument is overly dismissive of testimonial evidence

These are really good questions\! (A quick terminological note: this kind of evidence is typically called 'testimony' by philosophers. 'Testimony' is any evidence grounded in what other people say, assert, or report to you \-- whether literally with their voices or in writing or other communication medium.)

 

Right, so I gather your question is something like this: *In lecture, it seemed like we were treating testimony as being a not-very-good source of evidence. But... is that right? Surely testimony is pretty valuable in a lot of circumstances, including our knowledge of history.*

Yeah\! I think that's exactly right. Nearly everything I believe about, say, Napoleon, I believe on the basis of what other people (historians, teachers, Wikipedia editors) have told me. (I haven't *seen* Napoleon or *talked to him* myself. So I'm really just going off of other peoples' say-so.) And... surely I have *knowledge* of certain facts about Napoleon? Isn't the testimony of historians sufficient for belief in Napoleon? 

I think so \- most philosophers think so too\! Testimony is considered to be a good, generally reliable, and important source of evidence. Lots of our beliefs *really are* justified on the basis of other people's say-so.

The average person actually seems to have a *really sophisticated* system for assessing testimony and judging whether it counts as strong or weak evidence. Our judgments are affected by,

* How reliable the other person has been in the past (e.g. imagine the same story coming from a friend who lies a lot vs. a friend who doesn't)  
* The social context of the testimony and how that context lends itself to reliability (e.g. imagine a story told at open mic night vs. a story told under oath in a courtroom)  
* How much expertise the testifier has in the topic at hand (e.g. an economist telling you that inflation is getting worse vs. a guy standing in line at the gas station)  
* How well the testimony fits in with our existing beliefs about how the world works (e.g. imagine me telling you that my doctor hit me with a rainstick vs. me telling you my doctor hit me with one of the reflex-tester widgets)

That last thing is part of what's going on in the Bill Murrary case. Most of us don't think that celebrities hang out in dumpsters. So are more skeptically disposed to testimony that claims otherwise.

So:

I think you’re right that testimony can often be really good evidence. I think Hick would acknowledge that too\! 

Hick's point is, I think, that in general we take \*seeing something our own eyes\* to be much more powerful evidence than merely hearing about it from someone else. (That is, *perceptual evidence* is all-else-equal **better than** *testimonial evidence*.) That gap, he thinks, can explain why some people's belief in God may count as rational for them, depending on what they've seen.

Does that help answer your question at all?

Q: For a student who wonders about the *prevalence* of religious experiences and whether that counts as public evidence

Suppose I have a clear and distinct experience of talking with a ghost. This experience may be sufficient evidence **for me** to believe in ghosts. But when I tell you about my ghost encounter, **you** do not have sufficient evidence to believe in ghosts. (Whereas I saw the ghost and spoke with it, you only talked to me about what I saw.)

 

We might then wonder, *What if lots of people have religious experiences? At some point, does that start to count as sufficient evidence?*

Maybe\!

Think about the ghost case again. When it's only \*me\* telling you about my ghost encounter, you should probably remain skeptical of ghosts. But what if it's not just me? What it's lots of people? What if it's everyone you know? At some point, I expect, you'd start to think that maybe ghosts are real after all. *Everyone else* seems to think so\! What are the chances that literally everyone you know is wrong?

Probably something similar would be true if everyone you knew reported seeing angels or talking with God.

But suppose 1 million people have had ghost encounters this month in Chicago. Each one claims, in public, to have seen a ghost. You haven't seen any ghosts, but you might reason, *Given all these people claiming to have seen ghosts, it's starting to look sorta plausible that there are ghosts living in Chicago. Maybe I'm starting to believe that.*

Obviously, this argument is, at root, based on a bunch of people's experiences, their private evidence. **However,** strictly speaking, their experiences aren’t *your* evidence. Your evidence is other people's *testimony.* You believe in ghosts, in this case,  b/c other people *say* that they have seen ghosts. That testimony is public evidence\! (If I record a podcast telling my ghost story, for instance, that podcast public evidence.) A belief in ghosts is directly based on public testimonial evidence \-- and indirectly based on the testifiers’ private experiences.

Q: Maybe God exists OUTSIDE OF TIME

Yeah, good\!  
It's an interesting suggestion. It's also a suggestion that I'm completely at a loss to know how to assess.

*Maybe God exists outside of time.*

Maybe\! But... man I don't know if that makes much sense to me.

We are presumably trying to say, something like, "There is no particular point in time at which God exists, but also God does exist, timelessly." But when I pick at that claim a bit, I start to lose the sense of it.

First, we need to say, (i) *at each particular time, t, it is not the case that God exists at that particular time.* On the "Outside-of-Time" view, when I ask, "Does God exist right now?", the theist should answer, "That question doesn't actually make sense \-- it has a presupposition error. God doesn't exist at ANY particular time, so God does not exist right now." But obviously we can't leave it there b/c that sounds like we're saying God isn't real, full stop.

So we **also** need to say, (ii) *at each particular time, t, it is true at t that God exists.* (Why? Again, imagine asking a theist: *Does God exist?* They should say, *Yes.* They should say that regardless of what the time is \-- at *every* time, they should say *yes.* Thus, it is true at every moment that God exists.)

But those two claims together make my brain hurt: *at every particular moment, t, it is false that God exists at t, and it is true at t that God exists.*

Which... What? Is that coherent? It doesn't sound coherent

Set that aside though. Maybe we can resolve that. Maybe that actually does make sense. That *still* leaves us with another problem\! How are we to understand God's timelessness while at the same time insisting that God is capable of acting *inside* of time?

Like, suppose God does a miracle next Tuesday at 9:00AM MDT; God is the *cause* of some event at that time. (*The world is going along as normal, then ZZZT at the precise moment of 9:00 AM MDT, there is a physics violation; suddenly a rock is pushed up a hill by a divine force, a force not reducible to or explainable in terms of any other natural things. At that moment, there is in existence a divine force, a push from God.)* But... surely that means God exists at that particular time, right? At least, it doesn't strike me as particularly coherent to insist that an agent can act at a time while literally not existing at that time.  
(And obviously, it won't do to simply say, "Well, God exists that *all* times." That's the claim we're trying to *avoid* asserting\! If God exists at all times, then God exists at t1, which entails "If Jones..." therefore, etc.)

We can always retreat to the backstop of *"God is mysterious and beyond human comprehension"*, of course. But that's just admitting defeat. ("My view, in fact, cannot be expressed in a way that is logically coherent.") That's never a good place to rest one's case\!

Q: On whether the multiverse can give us free will and omniscience

Ahh, that's a really interesting suggestion\!

I have a couple thoughts. A clarificatory question and then a more critical reply (though, one that depends on how the question is answered\!)

(i)

I think I get the gist of the view you're suggesting. Like, suppose you're ordering a latte. You can choose a small, medium, large, or cancel your order. Those are all the possible outcomes that God foresees. Each of these represents a different possible world (and God knows all the facts about each world.) Your choice is free b/c God only foresees the possible outcomes, not the choice itself.

So here's a question:

*Is it your view that **each possible world is real**? Like, there are 4 worlds \-- each equally real \-- branching off of your coffee order? (the small latte world, the medium latte world, etc.) **Or** is that your decision just is a decision about which of those possible worlds will because the actual / real world (and the other possibilities are eliminated.)?*

 

(ii) 

And here's the worry: it seems like both of those options cause problems.

Suppose that there's only one actual, real world, and that your free choice *decides* which of the possible futures becomes our actual future. (with the rest being discarded.) This just sounds like a view on which God isn't Omniscient. Like, there's one real world, and in that real world you have a free choices about which size of coffee to order. God **does** know what your options are, but God **doesn't** know what you'll choose. When you select a medium latte, God **learns something** that She didn't know before. Thus, God is not Omniscient.

On the other hand, suppose that your choice causes the universe to branch: each of your options leads to a distinct and real universe. (the small latte universe, etc.) If so, that seems perfectly compatible with God's Omniscience. God knows, in advance, what all of your options are. **And** She knows that every single one of those options will be chosen by you. She doesn't learn anything when you make your selection. However, it's not clear to me on this view that you have **free will.** Part of what it means to freely choose, it seems, is to select one particular option over the others while having the ability to choose have selected a different option instead of the one. But on the view under consideration, that never happens. Every human choice results in every selection being made. That's just how God designed the universe. And... that doesn't sound like a universe with freedom? Freedom surely involve the power not just to choose but to refuse\! And we don't seem to have that power in a multiverse where everyone always chooses everything.

Does that make sense? Any thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: Does it violate our free will for God to answer petitionary prayers?

That's an interesting argument\! ("Does it not violate our free will for God to answer our petitions? If so, surely God doesn't answer our petitions.")

I \*think\* I know what a traditional theist is going to want to say about this? Let me offer that reply, and we'll see if you find that satisfying at all.

Obviously, petitionary prayers can take all sorts of different forms, can arise in lots of different situations, so, it's not easy to say something about *all* of them at once. But let's look at a concrete example, and see what lessons we can draw. 

Imagine that your car gets stuck in ditch late a night in a rural area, w/ no cell service. You try to get it out for hours. Pushing and pulling. Slamming the accelerator. Reversing. You have 3 options at this point: (i) keep trying on your own, (ii) give up, (iii) ask someone for help. Suppose you ask for help. You walk to the nearest farm house, and the farmer agrees to help. He tows you out of the ditch. 

Surely the farmer has not violated your free will. You freely chose to ask for help, and the farmer didn't *undermine* you or your choice.

Now imagine, instead, that you ask for help from God, rather than from the farmer. God sends an Angel Truck down to earth to pull the car out of the ditch. Or, God firms up the ground under your wheels so that you can get purchase. Or, God sends a message to the devout farmer, to go help you.

Surely God has not violated your free will \-- no more than the farmer did in the other version of the case. You freely chose to ask for help, and God didn't undermine you or your choice. 

Maybe the general lesson is this:

 Surely, one can be **free** without being **entirely self-sufficient.** It's okay to ask for help sometimes; that doesn't make you unfree. Indeed, *deciding to ask for help* is a meaningful use of our freedom\! 

 

Is that satisfying at all? Or, do you \-- or anyone else\! \-- have any other suggestions for what the theist might say here?

Q: Other good things that petitionary prayers might get us

Yeah, I think that's a nice suggestion\!

This response seems like a close-relative of the "Devotional Prayers" response I mentioned in lecture. I think there's probably a wider family of responses here: 

*Maybe petitionary prayers are important because they lead to \<**a good thing**\> that isn't divine intervention.*

In lecture, I suggested the good thing might be a re-affirmation of our devotion to God. One might think, instead, that another good thing is moral improvement. (maybe we become better people by practicing *surrender to God.)* Or, the good thing might be comfort.

This response is still rather revisionary, of course\! Like, most theists who engage in petitionary prayer *really do* seem to take themselves be sensibly asking for God's help. And this response tells us that that's confused. Petitionary prayer is great for a lot of reasons, but *having an effect on God* is not among them.

Q: Maybe God just helps us *learn* the facts about morality?

Nice\! That's a really novel and provocative response.

 

Ok, so, I think the view you're proposing is, strictly speaking, **not Divine Command Theory.** The moral facts aren't *merely* God's unguided preferences. They are independent of God. That means, the view youre proposing is one where is God is **not** directly responsible for the moral facts. God's will isn't the *immediate source* of moral facts.

**BUT** \-- assuming i'm understanding you correctly \-- your view does suggest a neat alternative: where God is still *truly* *essential* for morality. God isn't the author of morality but is, instead, the one and only *interpreter* of morality. God is the one who can *perceive* the moral facts, which makes the rest of us utterly reliant on Her for moral guidance. (It's like we're all trying to walk through a deadly, pitch-dark cave. God is the only one with night vision, so She's doing Her best to steer us away from dangerous and get us through safe.)

I think that's a cool compromise view\!

The theist gets to say **both** of the things that are important to her: (a) God is really important to morality and (b) there are objective moral facts. 

I suspect, though, that this view is going to strike people like Craig as kinda unattractive b/c *they aren't going to like the part where humans are completely clueless about ethics*. Most DCT-ists think that humans have some sort of innate, intuitive understanding of morality. Our intuitive moral judgments help us know what's right and wrong, even in cases where we aren't 100% sure what God's preferences are. (e.g. *What does God think about pirating software?\! She's never said. It's not in the Bible or anything\! But I **intuitively** judge piracy to be wrong, and my moral intuitions are reliable...*) Recall, Craig's argument for **moral realism** basically just amounted to this thought: *we all already have clear moral beliefs. We all already know that there are moral facts and what the moral facts are.* 

That suggests he's not going to be thrilled about your view, where we humans are all moral dummies. 

Does that seem right to you? Thoughts about that?

Q: On DCT, how do we *learn* about the moral facts?

Ahh, that's super interesting\!

You ask, *How are we able to know what actions are considered to be moral commands by God?*

I don't think there's any one, consensus view about this, but I think I can anticipate how Craig is likely to answer that question. (At least, given what he says in the article I assigned for this module.) Here's my take on Craig's answer:

God has given us two sources of moral knowledge, two ways to find out which things are right and which things are wrong. The first is divine revelation. We have this book, the Bible, that was dictated to humans by God and that includes information about God's preferences. \[Craig is a Christian, and I think an exclusivist about religion? But other religions could make the same point about their texts, presumably.\] The Bible tells us, for instance, that God dislikes killing and adultery and theft. It's right there in the Ten Commandments\! God *told* Moses that he hates those things. Thus, we can be sure that those things are wrong.

The second source of moral knowledge is moral intuition. We each are born with a God-given moral compass, a natural and instinctive understanding of right and wrong. This compass reliably tracks God's preferences, and so we can count on our intuitive moral judgments to be correct. This is important because God didn't have time in the Bible to spell out literally all of Her likes and dislikes. She left some unspoken. But we can fill in the gaps with our moral intuition. The Ten Commandments don't say, "Thou shall not post revenge porn online" but we all judge that doing so is wrong. And we're right\! Our judgments about revenge porn reliably track God's dislike for revenge porn.

As for all those wicked people who claim to be acting for God (e.g. Nazis): that's not really a problem. The rest of us easily see through that lie; we correctly intuit that their atrocities are not God-loved.

That's my best guess about what Craig is going to say, anyway.

Does that answer the question you were asking?

(Which is not the same as asking, *Do you find that answer satisfying?*  Though, that's also worth thinking about\! **Do** you find that answer satisfying?\!)

Q: Aren’t scientific laws kinda similar to moral laws?

Nice, yeah\!

I'm glad you noticed that parallel between moral laws and scientific laws. That's going to come up in a big way next week, when we talk about non-religious accounts of moral realism. From an atheist's perspective, there *does* seem to be a tight analogy between the rules we find in ethics and the rules we find in science. (*Here are some laws, but no lawmaker. Here are some abstract principles that just exist and not for any obvious reason, rules that seemingly just popped into existence one day.)* We might call this the 

**Analogy Claim:** for an atheist, ethical laws are analogous to scientific laws. (Which is to say, *these two things are so similar that the atheist needs to treat them the same, draw the same conclusion about both.*)

Notice, that claim *doesn't* tell the atheist what to think about these laws, what conclusions to draw. It just says the atheist should *be consistent* \-- whatever they decide about the scientific laws, they should decide the same thing about the moral laws.

There are a couple ways to go here; a couple different arguments we might endorse from this **Claim**.

**Argument \#1:**

(1) Clearly, there can be scientific laws w/o a lawmaker. The law of gravity exists and is universally binding, even though no one *authored* that law. Atheists can believe in universal scientific laws, no problem.

(2) **Analogy Claim**: scientific laws are basically the same as ethical laws 

(C) Therefore, the atheist can believe in universal moral laws, no problem.

**Argument \#2:**

(1') Clearly, there **can't** be moral laws without a lawmaker. That's nonsensible. Atheism is incompatible with a belief in objective, universal moral laws.

(2) **Analogy Claim**: scientific laws are basically the same as ethical laws 

(C') Therefore, atheism is incompatible with a believe in objective, universal scientific laws.

 

Many theists will simply take your point about the analogy between scientific and moral laws and use it to endorse Argument \#2.

We'll talk about this a bit more in lecture next week. Briefly, though: I don't think this is a promising line of argument for the theist. Pretty much every atheist I've ever met is **very certain** that there are universal, scientific laws. The atheist is **very committed** to claim (1). That's a fixed position; no theistic argument is going to budge them from that.

Thus, the choice before the atheist is just this: *Do I also accept the Analogy Claim and so agree with Argument \#1 that moral laws can be objective OR do I reject the Analogy Claim?*

That's my take on the dialectic, anyway.

Thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: On whether DCT is subjectivism or relativism

I've often heard the complaint that DCT amounts to **subjectivism.** What's characteristic of subjective truths is that they're dependent on / fixed-by personal preferences, and on DCT, the moral truths are all dependent on / fixed-by God's preferences. True: unlike other subjective domains, the subjective truths on DCT are all indexed to one person, God. But that doesn't make them *non-subjective;* it just means we've privileged a particular subjective perspective.

But that's not your worry. Your worry is that DCT amounts to a kind of **relativism.** Each religious community has different beliefs about what God's preferences are, and so each community has a different set of moral codes. That's relativism\! The facts are fixed by one's social group. 

My initial thinking about that:

this issue seems to arise only for DCT proponents who are inclusivists about religious disagreement. If I think that each religious has a legit claim to being true, then I will be inclined to think that each religion's moral code is true-for-them; the moral facts are dependent on your culture's religious consensus. On the other hand, an **exclusivist** DCT-ist would presumably say that other religious groups are just wrong. Like, if I'm an exclusivist about religion **and** my religion says that God hates it when people eat pork but yours doesn't, I'll simply say that your religious group is confused about the moral facts / the what-God-likes facts. It just **is** wrong to eat pork; God **just does** really hate pork-eating. And your community is confused.

That's my initial thought, anyway. Does that seem right to you? Reactions / objections / follow-up thoughts?

Q: What does Omnibenevolence mean on DCT?

Good, yeah\! I think this is a *really* important complication. (In fact, one that I originally had in my slides but deleted for lack of time\!)

Our 3-O God is, by definition, **morally perfect.** And part of what we were talking about this week is what that means \-- *What are we saying about God when we say that She is morally perfect?*  One easy gloss is this:

**God is Omnibenevolent** \=\> God lives up to *every moral standard,* always and without fail. God is, in deed and thought, *perfectly compliant with the moral rules.*

If I'm understanding you correctly, one of the things you're asking is what Omnibenevolence looks like when DCT is true, when we suppose that morality is objective in the way that Craig says it is.

That's a good question\! And I think the answer is actually rather surprising\!

Recall, on DCT the moral rules are just *God's will and preferences.* God *likes* kindness, so it's a moral rule that being kind is morally correct; God *dislikes* murder, so it's a moral rule that murdering people is morally incorrect. 

On that picture, when we say that God is **morally perfect,** we're saying that God *always acts in accordance with Her own preferences.* She always does the stuff She likes (e.g. being kind) and never does the stuff She dislikes (e.g. murdering.) And since on DCT, God's preferences are what **make** morality (rather than being *responses to* pre-existing moral facts), **God's Omnibenevolence seems to amount to non-hypocrisy.** God is perfectly in sync with her own arbitrary preferences. If She tells us that She's decided such-and-such is bad, She is then perfectly consistent about never doing that bad thing Herself. And when She decides that so-and-so is great, She is then perfectly consistent about always doing so-and-so.

That seems pretty surprising to me\!  

If DCT is true, we apparently should be thinking of the 3-O God as a being who is *maximally powerful, maximally knowledgeable, and perfectly non-hypocritical.* (\!\!\!)

Q: Who enforces morality on DCT?

If by "enforce" you mean, *makes sure that rule-breakers receive a just punishment,* then the answer is probably *God.* God is the enforcer of Her own moral rules. She's the one who metes out punishment and reward for moral and immoral behavior.

If by "enforce" you mean, *makes sure that the rules are always followed and never broken,* then the answer is *No one.* On most accounts of moral realism \-- including DCT \-- the moral rules are like *legal statutes* not like *laws of* nature. Laws of nature **can't** be broken; you can't, e.g., go faster than the speed of light, no matter how hard you try. They're baked into the universe in such a way as to be unbreakable. That's not what moral laws are like. Moral laws are like everyday *laws of the land.* It's against the law to steal, but you **can** physically steal. It's *possible* to steal.  But you mustn't, and you'll be punished if you do. That's what moral laws are like. There's a moral rule against murdering. But you *can* murder. That's a physical possibility. Except you mustn't, and you'll be punished (by God \-- and also by the U.S. govt) if you do.

Q: On moral progress

Yeah, good\! I'm really glad you're raising this point. (I thought about including it in lecture, but cut it in the interests of time.)

Lots of people are attracted to this idea, call it, 

**Moral Progress** \-- humans have been able to make real moral progress (though, slowly and not-at-all smoothly.) Over the last 200-300 years, many ethical truths that used to be controversial or dismissed entirely are now widely accepted. (e.g. w*omen have equal moral worth to men*, *slavery is awful and so is racism*, and *child labor is bad*)

This idea has a lot of important implications.

The first is that it seems to require that moral realism be true. For morality to **progress** (from worse to better) there has to be some sort of objective moral standards for us to be making progress towards. (Otherwise, all we'd have is **changing moral tastes,** not progress. We don't say, for instance, that humans are *making progress in fashion.* As though, we *used* to like objectively ugly stuff but *now* we like objectively pretty stuff.)

A second is that, as you point out, the possibility of **Moral** **Progress** may give us a way to make sense of our conflicting moral intuitions about the past. 

A third is that **Moral Progress** may count as some corroborating evidence for the Soul-making Theodicy. Like, we'd *expect* to see moral progress, if the story we get from the SMT is true. God created mankind as immature and savage, but gradually and over the course of many millennia, she is helping us mature (both individually and as a species). We are *making moral progress* *under God's guidance,* in other words.

A fourth implication of **Moral Progress** is the possibility that we, ourselves, are *really confused* about morality. We look back at, e.g., Thomas Jefferson and can easily point out all of his moral shortcomings and problematic beliefs. But then surely, if humans *are* making moral progress, our descendants 200 years from now will be able to do the same with us. We should expect that; we should expect that we ourselves have moral blind spots potentially s big as Jefferson's. That seems to recommend that we be humble and open-minded about our own possible moral failings.

(Those are all things that follow **IF** we accept **Moral Progress.** \-- and not reasons to think the claim is actually true. That's a whole 'nother discussion. For now, I think, it suffices to point out that a lot of people do think the claim plausible.)

Any thoughts about that? (E.g. other implications, reasons for- or against believing in **moral progress**, objections to some of those tiny arguments just given, etc.)

Q: On why I think the DCT explanations are unsatisfying

Ahh, that's really interesting\!

I don't know that I have any truly helpful things to say about how satisfying those various explanations are. But I can speak to what I think is driving my own intuitive response to the DCT explanations. (e.g. "Racism is bad b/c God hates it.") 

First, I suspect that part of why I, personally, dislike the DCT explanation is the fact that it's conditional on God's stable preferences. Like, at the moment, God and I seem to mostly agree about which things are right and which things are wrong. When someone asks, *But **why** is child sex trafficking wrong?* I get to say, *Because God hates it\! And I agree with Her.* But I'm uncomfortable with the counterfactual worlds were God changes her mind. In a world where God has stopped disliking child sex trafficking, we're left in the uncomfortable position of both disagreeing with God and being wrong. And insofar as I'm inclined to say, "But of course God would never change her mind," it seems like the *reason* why She's not must be that her preferences are non-arbitrary. If they *are* arbitrary, then who am I to be so confident that God won't ever whimsically change her preferences?

Second, I suspect I also may be partly importing a worry about *moral epistemology.* If God's attitudes are what make the right actions right, then surely we need access to those attitudes to determine the moral facts. Since we **don't** seem to have ready access to those attitudes, that makes it hard to do, say, the ethics of emerging technology. I want to ask questions like, *What does God think about Autonomous Weapons Systems \-- are those a morally better or a morally worse way to conduct war? What does God think about virtual crime \-- does God mind if we murder people or commit sexual assaults in videogames? What are God's preferences concerning sperm donation? Or Deep Brain Stimulation? etc.* Presumably on DCT, there must be **a fact of the matter** about what God prefers in each of those cases. But... how do we go about discovering those facts? And if we can't discover the facts, how are we supposed to live morally? It prolly won't work to say, "Just extrapolate based on God's known preferences" b/c, on DCT, there shouldn't be any unifying, independent principle tying God's preferences together. There's no reason for her to be "consistent". (Whatever *consistency* even amounts to in a purely subjective domain\!)

I don't think either of those consideration is *decisive* or anything\! I just suspect that they're are partly driving my intuitions.

Q: Does DCT’s unattractiveness explain the increase in non-religious people?

Maybe? I’m not totally sure what to say about the connection between the increasing rates of non-religious people and decreasing interest in Divine Command Theory. I can think of a number of different possible explanations for what's going on, but I'm not sure which one(s) is correct.

(1) Lots of people are leaving religion for reasons that have nothing to do with morality. But, having left religion behind, they do still find themselves engaging in ethical inquiry. (e.g. arguing about whether abortion should be legal, advocating for/against universal healthcare, etc.) So they embrace some secular account of ethical realism.

(2) Lots of people are leaving religion *because* they find the metaethical underpinnings of DCT unsatisfying. (e.g. maybe they consider something akin to Plato's worries about the apparent arbitrariness of an ethics grounded in God's dictates.)

(3) People are leaving religion and embracing secular metaethics b/c of a growing distaste for the ethics they hear espoused by religious people. (i.e. the cause is not really a **metaethical** worry about the source of morality, nor is the cause wholly independent from ethical concerns.) In the U.S., for instance, religiosity is strongly correlated with conservatism. (In one recent poll, 78% of conservatives said they are "absolutely certain" that God exists compared with only 45% of liberals.) For a politically liberal person, then, the association between religion and political/moral views they detest could push them way from religion.

No doubt there are other possible explanations, too. Those are just the first few to occur to me. 

I'm inclined to think that (2) is mostly **not** what's going on? At least, it doesn't seem like the non-philosophers I know spend hardly any time thinking about metaethics. But I'm not sure about the relative merits of (1) and (3).

Any thoughts about this? (you or anyone else\!) Other explanations for these trends?

Q: How does virtue ethics fit into all of this?

That's a really interesting\! If we had more time in the course, I'd love to have had a chance to talk about the connections between our major normative ethical theories (viz. Consequentialism, Deontology, Virtue Ethics) and God.

Virtue ethics is especially interesting, given the focus on **morally good character** rather than on **morally good actions.** I've been focusing our discussions on the the latter ("Why are the right action right?), but we could have isomorphic conversations about the former\! ("Why are the virtuous character traits virtuous?") Divine Command Theory gives us a really tidy answer here, I think. It tells us that *morality consists in having those character traits that God likes and avoiding those character traits that God dislikes \--* which, since God herself is morally perfect, exhibiting all the good traits and none of the bad ones, amounts to the simple claim that, *morality consists of having a character like God's.*

As you point out, that reply will be subject to Plato's now-familiar concerns. *Consider the virtues: courage, benevolence, temperance, humility, magnanimity, etc. God has all these traits and all of these traits are good to have. But: **are these traits good to have (merely) because God has them OR does God have them b/c they are (genuinely and independently) good to have?*** *If the former, then we are being told to mimic God while being given no good reason for doing so. If the latter, then virtue does not come directly from God.* And so on.

If I'm understanding you, it sounds like you're working to make sense of that latter option (where the virtues, the good character traits, are worth having b/c they are genuinely, independently good \-- and not merely because they happen to be God's character traits.) 

I wonder if we could tell a story similar to Aristotle's \-- i.e. the one we talked about in Theory of Natural Law lecture? Like, 

*Humans need to live together, cooperatively, in order to survive and thrive. To successfully live together, the people in a community need to have certain pro-social character traits and dispositions. The things we call the 'virtues' are just those traits: the character traits such that, when everyone has them, a society tends to be successful and stable. Obversely, the **vices** are just those character traits that DON'T promote social stability when everyone has them \-- things like cruelty, greed, and arrogance. Thus, moral goodness is just the solution to a coordination problem. ("Which traits are such that if everyone has them in a human community, that community is likely to survive and thrive?")*

 

I spit-balling here; I don't know if that works. Any thoughts about that? Other suggestions? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: On amnesia as an objection to same memories view

Excellent question / objection\! Your argument, if I'm understanding you correctly, is something like this:

P1) If the Mental Content view is correct, then a person, X, who completely loses their memories is **not identical** to the earlier persons w/ the same continuous body as X

P2) But X **IS** identical those earlier people (despite not remembering them)

C) The Mental Content view is not correct

What the Mental Content proponent says here is going to depend on whether they think **memory** is the only important thing for personhood. If they *do* think that memory is all that matters, I suspect they'll bite the bullet. ("I accept the implication. X is not the same person as those earlier people w/ whose bodies X is continuous.") As you point out though, most people aren't going to be inclined to agree.

But a lot of Mental Content View fans **dont** think memory is all that matters. They think that sameness of personality is also part of what matters. (and other things too: sameness of values, dispositions, core beliefs.) If **that's** what matters for personhood, then we should reject P1. It simply isn't true that that version of the Mental Content view implies that X is strictly non-identical to those earlier people. Although X has lost her memories, she retains pretty much all of the other important stuff. (At least, the 50 First Dates character retains most of the other important stuff. It's not like each date finds her with a radically different personality\!)

 

Here's a complication, though: while the Memories Only view gives us clean answers about which people are identical ("X and Y are the same person just in case one of them remembers being the other."), the Memory/Personality/Values/Etc. view doesn't always give us clean answers. 

Consider a person, P, who suffers a very serious blow to the head, one that significantly alters her personality/values etc. *Is post-injury P the same person as pre-injury-P?* Suppose post-P is \~20% different from pre-P. Maybe in that case we'll say that post-P and pre-P are the same person. But what if the difference is 21%? 22%? 23%? 30%? 40%? 60%?

At some point, it's going to obvs that post-P and pre-P are different people. (e.g. when they're 80% different.) But where does the change happen? Is there some precise value at which post-P and pre-P cease to count as the same person? Surely not\! Surely that's absurd\! (We have to say things like, "If one more neuron had been damaged, P would have died. But, thankfully, that didn't happen. So P is exactly the same person as before." ... That sounds *very implausible* to me. How could **one neuron** among billions make the difference between complete survival and complete destruction?\!)

This is a common objection to the Memory/Personality/Values/etc. view. *Unlike* the Memories Only view, it doesn't give us clean answers about who's identical to whom in these partial-change cases. (And, insofar as we require the view to give us answers, it has to draw an arbitrary line: *any tiny change beyond this point and the two people are not the same.*)

Q: On parfit’s ‘No Further Fact’ view

If we had more time, I'd have you all read a famous article by the philosopher Derek Parfit who responds to this objection by embracing what he calls a "No Further Fact" View about personal identity. Parfit accepts the Mental Content view of Personal ID, but he also argues that it's a mistake to insist that there must always been a clean Yes/No answer to questions about whether 2 people are literally the same person. (i.e. a mistake to insist that there will always be **some further fact.**) That's a bit telescopic, so let me give you a different case where it's easy to make sense of a No Further Fact View.

Imagine that you and I are having a bit of small talk, and you ask me where I'm from. I say, "It's complicated\! I was born in Oklahoma, but i was only there for 8 months. Then I spent 2 years in NC. Then 7 years in KS. Then 5 years in ND. Then 4 years in NE. Then 1 year in MT. Then 7 years in CO. Then 2 years in MO." 

You say, "Oh, interesting\! That's a lot of places\! But which of those are you **from,** though?"

I say, "I don't think it's like that. I was in OK for a little bit. Then NC for a bit. Then KS, ND, NE, MT, CO, and MO. That's where I've been; that's where I'm from."

You say, "Nonono, you're not understanding me. Like, which particular place is *The Place That You are From."*

I say, "I.... don't think that question has an answer? **There's No Further Fact** of the matter about where I'm from over-and-above the list I just gave you. The list is all there is."

In this case, I have a No Further Fact View about *Where I'm from.* I can list off where I've been, but, i think, it's a mistake to insist that there is some further fact, over-and-above the list, about where exactly I'm from.

That's what Parfit thinks about personal ID. Suppose we describe another blow-to-the-head case where the person experiences really significant changes in their mental content. (e.g. they lost 67% of their memories, have a personality that's 35% different, have 28% different values, etc.) If we ask Parfit whether post-P is the same person as pre-P, he'll say, "Well, they lost 67% of their memories, 35% of their personality, 28% of their values..." That's it; he'll give us that list but won't say *Yes* or *No.*

And if we *insist* ("I know all that\! But **are they the same person?**"), Parfit is going to shrug and say the same thing, "Well, they lost..." He doesn't think the *Yes* or *No* question has an answer.

On his view, it's a mistake to insist that there always must be some further fact of the matter about whether two people existing at different times count as *the same.* (in the same way that it's a mistake to insist that there must always be some further fact about where exactly each person is from.)

Any thoughts / objections / questions about that dialectic? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: On the science of souls

Right, so there is *definitely* a lot more physics to be done. (like, **a LOT**\!) Thus, it would *definitely* be too quick to say, "We've seen everything there is to see. We didn't see souls. Therefore, souls aren't physical." We haven't seen everything, not by a long shot.  
In the meantime, I'm not sure exactly what sort of doxastic attitude is warranted. It seems a bit overeager to positively *believe* in material souls, given our paucity of evidence. (Or, at least, given the evidence I'm aware of\!) But it seems equally overeager to be confident that they *don't* exist. So... maybe a suspension of belief about material souls? Or, hope for material souls? A moderate skepticism about material souls?

Q: Key difference in the tower analogy: God makes us both times

Yeah\! That's a great point. One key **disanalogy** between our body and the block tower is that the block tower is recreated by a different person (first built by your son, then recreated by you), whereas the human body is recreated by the original author (first built by God, then recreated by God.)

So we should ask: does this difference matter? (after all, one cannot reject an analogy merely by pointing to **a** difference. By definition, there will always be differences between any two analogues\! What matters is whether the difference *affects the quality of the comparison*.) And the answer, i think, is *Probably\!* Consider a variation on the block case where we've eliminated this difference: *Your son builds a tower. You knock it down. Your son rebuilds the tower exactly as before.* This version is more similar to the God/body case. And, in this version, it is much more plausible to think that the rebuilt tower *literally is the same tower.* 

Thus, it should be plausible to think in the God/body case that the rebuilt body *literally is the same body.*

That's a nice argument\!

I'm not sure what proponents of the block tower analogy might say at this point, but maybe:

*The new version of the analogy actually **doesn't** resolve the difference in question. In the new block tower case, your son is the proximate cause of the original and rebuilt towers. His hands place the blocks. But that's not what's going on with God and my body. Now God **may** literally assemble my recreated body in heaven, atom by atom, with Her own hands. I don't know how She does that. But we **do** know how my original body was made. It was assembled by my mother's body, cell by cell, in her womb. Thus, my son's act of recreating his own block tower (piece by piece, with his own hands both times) is **not** similar to the recreation of my body in heaven. And so, even if we agree that my son manages to recreate **the exact same tower,** it's not obvious that we should say the same for my body in heaven. My son literally and directly authors both versions of the tower; God does not literally and directly author both versions of my body. (Unless God has my mother's reassembled womb up there and uses that to remake my body?\!)*


That's **A** reply, anyway. Any thoughts about that? (you or anyone else\!) Other consideration that might resolve this disagreement about the Block Tower Analogy?

Q: What if God split the soul from its contents and gave half to 2 people?

Oooh, that's a super interesting question\!

Right, so:

Suppose we each have souls. And suppose that our souls contain our mental content. This soul+content is what survives the corruption and decay of our earthly bodies.

Usually God brings the whole soul+content along when She brings us into the afterlife, but suppose She decided not to that for a particular person, X.

In X's case, God detaches the content from the soul and sends them to different places: the content is housed in a new soul in heaven, and the soul is housed in a new body (and filled with new content) on earth.

We ask, *Is this case possible/coherent? If not, why not? If so, which thing(s) is X? The new soul \+ old content in heaven? The old soul \+ new body \+ new content on earth? Both? Neither?*

 

I don't know what to say about whether this case is coherent. I don't have a worked out view about what souls are, so I don't know whether it violates anything intrinsic about soulness to imagine pulling a soul apart from its content.

BUT I do have some rough intuitions about the other questions, about which of the new things counts as identical to X.

To say that **neither** is X seems coherent but maybe overly pessimistic. The being in heaven acts exactly like X, remembers being X, has all of X's values and dispositions and goals. Those seem like good reasons to think that the person in heaven **IS** X\! Likewise, the person on earth literally has X's soul\! So that seems like a good reason to think that the person on earth **IS** X\!

That said, it seems confused to me to say that **both** beings are X \-- i.e. to say that both the being in heaven and the being on earth is *literally the same person* as X. Before all this happened, X was a person with a single body, a single soul, a single personality, and a single set of memories. But if we say that **both** the heaven-person and the earth-person are identical to that earlier person, we have to say that that earlier, singular person now has two souls, two personalities, two sets of memories. That... seems confused to me? At least on its face.

Which leaves us with the last two options: (i) saying that the heaven person with X's mental content is X, (ii) saying that the earth person with X's soul is X. 

My intuitive judgment here is in favor of (i). That's the person who *acts and thinks* like X (and *thinks of herself **as** X\!)*. The other person acts and thinks in entirely distinct ways (including *thinking of herself as someone other than X\!*)

But I don't have any extra arguments to give in support of that intuition. Probably some people will judge that (ii) is the better X-candidate, in virtue of sharing X's soul.

Thoughts about any of that? (you or anyone else\!)

Q: Microbiome

Your comments about the Block Tower analogy also reminds me of another objection to the Mental Content view that I've sometimes heard from Same Body fans, which goes something like this:

There's some emerging biology research into the human microbiome \-- and especially, the part that lives in our intestines \-- which suggests that the kinds / varieties / quantities of bacteria that live in our bodies can have a pretty big impact on our cognition, altering our moods, desires, etc. (Although, the mechanisms by which that might happen are, to my knowledge, still poorly understood.)

If that's right, then it may turn out that we cannot wholly separate the sameness of our bodies from the sameness of memory personality. My personality, the way that I think, may be influenced by the state of my whole body and not just the state of my brain. It may be the case that my personality itself changes a bit depending on, say, what kinds of biota are right now living in my colon. If so, that would seem to imply that preservation of my cognition (making sure that I'm psychologically connected to some future person) may require some continuity of body.

Like, perhaps God needs to bring my whole GI tract up with me into heaven if She wants to preserve my personality. (or, at least, maybe She needs to give me a new **heaven colon** that has the same microbial composition \-- if not literally the same exact bacterial cells.)
